title: The trigonometric reduction is equivalent on |z|=1. For n=3 it yields a polynomial condition

identity.short_name: Unit-circle roots of (z+1)^6 - (z^3+1)^2

problem_spec.summary: Count the distinct roots on the unit circle of a polynomial designed so that, after the substitution z=e^{iθ}, both terms share the same complex phase and the equation collapses to a real trigonometric identity solvable via the triple-angle formula.
givens: Restrict attention to roots z with |z|=1.
ask: Determine the number of distinct complex roots of P(z) on the unit circle.

parameter(s).name: n
parameter(s).purpose: Defines the family P_n(z)=(z+1)^{2n}-(z^n+1)^2; the instance uses n=3 (so degree 6 vs squared degree 3).

invariant(s).name: unit_circle_phase_alignment
invariant(s).statement: For z=e^{iθ}, (z+1)^{2n} and (z^n+1)^2 both carry the phase factor e^{inθ}, so P_n(e^{iθ})=0 reduces to a purely real equation 2^{2n-2} cos^{2n}(θ/2) = cos^2(nθ/2).

invariant(s).name: triple_angle_closure_for_n3
invariant(s).statement: When n=3, the reduced equation involves cos(3x), allowing elimination via cos(3x)=4cos^3 x - 3cos x to obtain a polynomial equation in cos x.

essential_assumption(s): z lies on the unit circle so z=e^{iθ} with θ real.
Cancelling the common factor e^{i3θ} is valid since it is never zero.

title: purpose: simplify dim_F2(I/2I) into a pure size/dimension condition on I.

identity.short_name: Count Aut(R)-orbits of ideals with dim_F2(I/2I)=2 in Z4[x]/(x^3,2x)

subdomain(s): ideal-lattice group actions and orbit counting (Burnside)

problem_spec.summary: In a finite quotient ring R, consider the action of Aut(R) on the set of proper nonzero ideals. Restrict to those ideals whose mod-2 quotient I/2I has prescribed F2-dimension, and count the number of Aut(R)-orbits on that constrained set.
givens: Aut(R) acts on ideals by φ \cdot I = φ(I).
= { ideals I ⊊ R : I \neq (0), dim_{F_2}(I/2I)=2 }.
ask: Compute the number of Aut(R)-orbits on .

parameter(s).name: base_ring
parameter(s).purpose: controls 2-torsion and nilpotence behavior (here forces 2 \cdot =0 for proper ideals).

parameter(s).name: quotient_relations
parameter(s).purpose: determines ring structure (here x^3=0 and 2x=0).

parameter(s).name: invariance_scope
parameter(s).purpose: shifts from "Aut-invariant ideals to counting orbits under the full automorphism action.

parameter(s).name: mod_p_dimension_constraint
parameter(s).purpose: selects a constrained stratum of ideals (here p=2, dimension 2).

parameter(s).name: orbit_count_method
parameter(s).purpose: specifies orbit enumeration via fixed-point counts.

invariant(s).name: normal_form_in_R
invariant(s).statement: Every element has a unique representative a+bx+cx^2 with a\inZ/4Z and b,c\inF2, since x^3=0 and 2x=0.

invariant(s).name: maximal_ideal_and_2_annihilation
invariant(s).statement: The unique maximal ideal is =(2,x) consisting of elements with even constant term, and 2=0. Hence for any proper ideal I⊆, we have 2I=0 so I/2I≅I as F2-vector spaces.

invariant(s).name: ideal_as_T_invariant_subspace
invariant(s).statement: For proper ideals I⊆, the ideal condition is equivalent to I being an F2-subspace of  invariant under multiplication-by-x, i.e. T(I)⊆I where T(y)=xy.

invariant(s).name: automorphisms_determined_by_image_of_x
invariant(s).statement: Any automorphism fixes constants and is determined by x↦x+αx^2+2β with α,β\inF2; thus |Aut(R)|=4. Additionally x^2 is fixed by all automorphisms because (x+αx^2+2β)^2=x^2.

essential_assumption(s): Automorphisms preserve 1 and the defining relations x^3=0, 2x=0.
The quotient I/2I is viewed as an F2-vector space via R\toR/2R.
Proper ideals lie in the maximal ideal  and thus satisfy 2I=0 in this ring.

uniqueness_certificate(s).statement: The constrained set  is finite and is completely determined by the invariant-subspace description: dim_F2(I/2I)=2 ⇔ |I|=4 ⇔ I is a 2D T-invariant subspace of  under T(y)=xy. This yields a finite explicit list.

Aut(R) is finite and explicitly parameterized by (α,β)\inF2^2. The orbit count is uniquely determined by the action on the explicit list of ideals, equivalently by Burnside's lemma applied to fixed-point counts.

technique(s).name: explicit_element_normal_form_and_ideal_size_bounds
technique(s).purpose: obtain element normal forms and deduce finiteness and sizes.

title: Degree-4 irreducibles over F3 with parity-projection irreducible over F2

identity.short_name: Degree-4 irreducibles over F3 with parity-projection irreducible over F2

subdomain(s): constrained coefficient patterns via digitwise reduction

problem_spec.summary: Count degree-4 polynomials P over F_3 that are irreducible over F_3, subject to the constraint that the polynomial Q over F_2 formed by reducing each coefficient of P (viewed as an integer in {0,1,2}) modulo 2 via pi(1)=1 and pi(0)=pi(2)=0 has degree 4 and is irreducible over F_2. Output the count as a three-digit zero-padded integer.
givens: P(x) in F_3[x] with deg(P)=4.
pi: {0,1,2} -> F_2 given by pi(0)=0, pi(1)=1, pi(2)=0 (coefficientwise, using the unique reps 0,1,2).
Q(x) = sum_{i=0}^4 pi(a_i) x^i in F_2[x], where P(x)=sum_{i=0}^4 a_i x^i with a_i in F_3.
Constraints: deg(Q)=4 and Q is irreducible over F_2.
Constraint: P is irreducible over F_3.
ask: Determine N = #{ P in F_3[x] : deg(P)=4, P irreducible over F_3, deg(Q)=4, Q irreducible over F_2 }.
Output N as a three-digit, zero-padded integer.

parameter(s).name: p
parameter(s).purpose: base field for P

parameter(s).name: r
parameter(s).purpose: target field for projected Q

parameter(s).name: n
parameter(s).purpose: degree where reducibility splits are only 1+3 or 2+2

parameter(s).name: projection_rule
parameter(s).purpose: digitwise coefficient constraint linking P to Q

invariant(s).name: leading_and_constant_for_degree4_Q
invariant(s).statement: If deg(Q)=4 and Q is irreducible over F_2, then Q is monic with constant term 1; hence pi(a_4)=1 and pi(a_0)=1, forcing a_4=1 and a_0=1 in F_3 (since only 1 maps to 1 under pi).

invariant(s).name: forced_coefficients_from_projection
invariant(s).statement: For i=1..3: if Q has coefficient 1 at x^i then a_i=1 in F_3; if Q has coefficient 0 at x^i then a_i is restricted to {0,2} in F_3.

invariant(s).name: irreducible_degree4_over_field_iff_no_linear_or_irreducible_quadratic_factor
invariant(s).statement: Over any field, a degree-4 polynomial is reducible iff it has a linear factor or an irreducible quadratic factor (since the only nontrivial degree splits are 1+3 and 2+2; a product of two reducible quadratics would already have a linear factor).

essential_assumption(s): The projection is the set-map on representatives {0,1,2} followed by mod 2, not a ring homomorphism F_3 -> F_2.
Irreducibility is tested in F_3[x] and F_2[x] respectively.
uniqueness_certificate(s): The invariants force a_4=a_0=1 and restrict (a_1,a_2,a_3) to a finite discrete set. Listing all irreducible Q of degree 4 over F_2 and lifting them produces a complete finite candidate set. A complete factor-sieve (roots in F_3 plus all monic irreducible quadratics over F_3) decisively accepts or rejects each candidate, yielding a unique count N.

technique(s).name: count_irreducibles_over_F2_via_gauss_formula
technique(s).purpose: establish the number of monic irreducible quartics over F_2 and justify that a short explicit list is exhaustive

title: Multiplicative d'Alembert FE on (0,∞) via log-cosh + modular recurrence (seed 6)

identity.short_name: Multiplicative d'Alembert FE on (0,∞) via log-cosh + modular recurrence (seed 6)

problem_spec.summary: Classify a function on (0,∞) satisfying a multiplicative d'Alembert-type equation under boundedness and monotonicity, then compute $f(2^{2025})$ modulo 1000 from the induced linear recurrence.
givens: Domain $D=(0,\infty)$.
Functional equation $f(xy)+f(x/y)=2f(x)f(y)$ for all $x,y>0$.
$f$ is bounded on $[1,2]$.
$f$ is strictly increasing on $[1,\infty)$.
Anchor value $f(2)=2$.
Output normalization: $N\equiv f(2^{2025})\pmod{1000}$, $0\le N<1000$.
ask: Determine the unique integer $N$.

parameter(s).name: equation_form
parameter(s).purpose: Select the functional-equation archetype.

parameter(s).name: domain_model
parameter(s).purpose: Controls allowable substitutions (here, log-substitution on (0,∞)).

parameter(s).name: regularity_guard
parameter(s).purpose: Choose which constraint atoms are available to eliminate pathological solutions.

invariant(s).name: scaling_relation
invariant(s).statement: From equal_inputs, $f(x^2)=2f(x)^2-f(1)$, yielding a Chebyshev-style scaling recursion.

invariant(s).name: parity_or_period
invariant(s).statement: swap_variables forces $f(x)=f(1/x)$ (evenness under log); the trigonometric branch yields periodicity in the log-variable, incompatible with monotonicity.

invariant(s).name: interface_quantity_T
invariant(s).statement: The interface data $T=f(2)$ fixes the hyperbolic parameter after forcing multiplicativity of $h(t)=g(t)+\sqrt{g(t)^2-1}$.

invariant(s).name: domain_and_constraints
invariant(s).statement: Boundedness on $[1,2]$ plus contradiction_via_growth enforces continuity at 0 after log-substitution, and monotonicity selects the non-periodic branch.

essential_assumption(s): The domain $(0,\infty)$ is closed under $xy$ and $x/y$.
Boundedness on a nontrivial interval is sufficient (with the generated recursion) to enforce regularity.
uniqueness_certificate(s): After log-substitution $g(t)=f(e^t)$ satisfies dAlembert_type. A growth contradiction forces continuity at 0. With $g(t)\ge 1$ for $t\ge 0$, the constructed $h(t)=g(t)+\sqrt{g(t)^2-1}$ is multiplicative; multiplicative_Cauchy plus continuity gives $h(t)=e^{ct}$, hence $f(x)=\cosh(c\ln x)$. The condition $f(2)=2$ fixes $c$ (up to sign, which leaves $f$ unchanged), so $f(2^{2025})$ and $N$ are uniquely determined.

technique(s).name: invariant_extraction
technique(s).purpose: Extract $f(1)=1$, $f(x)=f(1/x)$, and set up the log-substitution reduction.

technique(s).name: generate_recursion
technique(s).purpose: Derive Chebyshev-type recursions, including the linear recurrence for $a_n=f(2^n)$.

technique(s).name: contradiction_via_growth
technique(s).purpose: Use blow-up of iterated doubling when $g(t)>1+\varepsilon$ to force $g(t)\to 1$ as $t\to 0$, i.e., continuity at 0.

technique(s).name: force_multiplicativity
technique(s).purpose: Construct $h(t)=g(t)+\sqrt{g(t)^2-1}$ and prove $h(u+v)=h(u)h(v)$.

technique(s).name: modular_matrix_exponentiation
technique(s).purpose: Compute $a_{2025}\bmod 1000$ using matrix powering and periodicity reduction.

theorem(s).name: dAlembert_type
theorem(s).role: Converts the functional equation into a cosine-type recursion and supplies identities used to build $h$.

theorem(s).name: multiplicative_Cauchy
theorem(s).role: Classify continuous solutions of $h(u+v)=h(u)h(v)$ as exponentials.

theorem(s).name: Chinese_Remainder_Theorem
theorem(s).role: Combine modular period information mod 8 and mod 125 to obtain a period mod 1000.

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: recursion_from_functional_equation
growth_vs_boundedness

critical_trick(s): Log-substitution turns multiplicative d'Alembert into additive d'Alembert.
Iterated doubling gives a growth contradiction forcing continuity.
Exponentialization $h=g+\sqrt{g^2-1}$ forces a multiplicative Cauchy equation.
Period reduction + matrix powering computes the large-index recurrence modulo 1000.

library(s).name: sympy
library(s).reason: Exact arithmetic in $\mathbb{Z}[\sqrt3]$ and matrix powers modulo 8/125/1000.

library(s).name: random
library(s).reason: Independent floating-point spot checks of the recovered closed form against the functional equation.

task(s).name: primary_structural_check
task(s).reason: Verify the recurrence $a_{n+1}=4a_n-a_{n-1}$ for $a_n=f(2^n)$ and compute $a_{2025}\bmod 1000$ via matrix exponentiation; confirm consistency with the closed-form model.

task(s).name: secondary_independent_check
task(s).reason: Compute $(2+\sqrt3)^{2025}$ in $\mathbb{Z}[\sqrt3]$ modulo 1000 via fast powering and extract the integer coefficient giving $a_{2025}$; also numerically test $f(xy)+f(x/y)=2f(x)f(y)$ for random positive $(x,y)$ using $c=\operatorname{arcosh}(2)/\ln 2$.

title: Pexider-Jensen with boundedness forcing linearity (seed 5)

identity.short_name: Pexider-Jensen with boundedness forcing linearity (seed 5)

problem_spec.summary: Determine a uniquely forced affine form from a Pexider-Jensen equation plus boundedness and algebraic anchor constraints, then compute an encoded integer output.
givens: Domain model: functions \mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}.
Pexider-Jensen equation $f((x+y)/2)=(g(x)+h(y))/2$ for all real x,y.
Regularity guard: f is bounded above on [0,1].
Additional constraint atom: f is strictly increasing on [0,\infty).
ask: Compute the integer N defined by reducing $f(2026)=p/q$ and taking $N\equiv p+q\pmod{1000}$, $0\le N<1000$.

parameter(s).name: domain_model
parameter(s).purpose: Choose ambient domain.

parameter(s).name: equation_form
parameter(s).purpose: Select functional-equation template.

parameter(s).name: regularity_guard
parameter(s).purpose: Select which constraint eliminates pathological solutions.

invariant(s).name: translation_invariance
invariant(s).statement: The Pexider-Jensen equation forces the invariant difference g(x)-h(x)=constant, reducing to the Jensen midpoint equation for f.

invariant(s).name: rational_scaling
invariant(s).statement: After forcing additivity for F(x)=f(x)-f(0), one gets F(qx)=qF(x) for all rational q.

invariant(s).name: interface_quantity_T
invariant(s).statement: The slope parameter T=F(1)=f(1)-f(0) determines f on \mathbb{Q} and, under regularity, on \mathbb{R}.

invariant(s).name: constraint_atoms_rigidity
invariant(s).statement: Boundedness on an interval forces continuity at 0 for additive solutions, hence linearity; monotonicity selects the correct branch among finitely many slopes.

essential_assumption(s): \mathbb{R} is closed under required midpoint and addition operations.
Boundedness on [0,1] holds to regularize Jensen/additive solutions.
uniqueness_certificate(s): Pexider reduction gives the Jensen midpoint equation, which forces additivity for F=f-f(0). Boundedness implies continuity at 0, hence f is affine with uniquely determined slope from the anchor constraints, and monotonicity eliminates the remaining branch.

technique(s).name: plug_special_values
technique(s).purpose: obtain basepoint relations such as f(0) and links among g(0),h(0).

technique(s).name: swap_variables
technique(s).purpose: derive symmetry/cancellation identities leading to g-h constant.

technique(s).name: equal_inputs
technique(s).purpose: create scaling and anchoring identities.

technique(s).name: bounding_trap
technique(s).purpose: force continuity of an additive function by shrinking arguments.

technique(s).name: classify_piecewise
technique(s).purpose: split into slope cases and eliminate incompatible ones using monotonicity.

technique(s).name: force_additivity
technique(s).purpose: reduce to additive_Cauchy for the normalized function F.

technique(s).name: show_linearity
technique(s).purpose: conclude affine/linear form for f.

theorem(s).name: additive_Cauchy
theorem(s).role: additive + continuity at 0 implies linearity.

theorem(s).name: Jensen_type
theorem(s).role: midpoint Jensen equation reduces to additivity for F=f-f(0); boundedness regularizes.

theorem(s).name: Pexider_type
theorem(s).role: swapping variables forces g-h constant and collapses Pexider-Jensen to Jensen.

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: functional_equation_as_invariant_constraint
regularity_eliminates_pathologies

critical_trick(s): Extract the invariant offset k=g-h by swapping variables, then eliminate it to obtain the Jensen midpoint equation for f.
Normalize by F=f-f(0) to force additivity, then apply boundedness to obtain continuity and linearity.
Use the algebraic anchor constraints to solve for the slope and select the correct branch via monotonicity.

library(s).name: sympy
library(s).reason: symbolic verification of the functional identity under the derived affine forms and solving the resulting parameter constraints.

library(s).name: random
library(s).reason: randomized numerical testing of the derived closed forms on sampled real inputs.

task(s).name: primary_structural_check
task(s).reason: Use SymPy to (i) verify that the derived affine form satisfies the Pexider-Jensen equation identically and (ii) solve the anchor constraint system to confirm the only possible slopes are {1,-5} with k=\pm2, then apply monotonicity to select c=1.

task(s).name: secondary_independent_check
task(s).reason: Randomly sample real pairs (x,y) to numerically confirm $f((x+y)/2)=(g(x)+h(y))/2$ for the chosen representative (k=2) and compute N from the reduced fraction of f(2026).

title: Cyclic adjacent-sum squares under majorization cap

identity.short_name: Cyclic adjacent-sum squares under majorization cap

problem_spec.summary: Optimize a cyclic sum of a convex kernel applied to adjacent sums by combining rearrangement on the cyclic order with majorization/Schur-convexity under a fixed-sum plus cap constraint.
givens: n is fixed as 5.
Variables satisfy $0\le x_i\le \frac{2}{5}$ for all $i$ and $\sum_{i=1}^5 x_i=1$.
For a permutation $\pi$ of $\{1,2,3,4,5\}$ define $S(\pi)=\sum_{i=1}^5(x_{\pi(i)}+x_{\pi(i+1)})^2$ with $\pi(6)=\pi(1)$.
Let $K=\max S(\pi)$ over all admissible $(x_1,\dots,x_5)$ and permutations $\pi$.
Write $K=\frac{p}{q}$ in lowest terms and define $N\in\{0,\dots,999\}$ by $N\equiv p+q\pmod{1000}$.
ask: Determine the exact value of $K$ and all equality cases.
Compute the normalized integer output $N$.

parameter(s).name: n
parameter(s).purpose: Controls dimension for n_variable inequalities and cyclic rearrangement structure.

parameter(s).name: constraint_type
parameter(s).purpose: Chooses the constrained_domain on which optimization occurs.

parameter(s).name: convex_kernel
parameter(s).domain: a specified convex/concave function $\phi$ with decidable properties
parameter(s).purpose: Allows Jensen/Karamata style reductions and changes which inequality_chain_design applies.

invariant(s).name: majorization_order
invariant(s).statement: Under fixed_sum and box-cap constraints, iterated mixing_variables produces a vector that majorizes the original while preserving the sum; Schur-convex objectives increase along this process, forcing an extremal two-level boundary vector.

invariant(s).name: cyclic_structure
invariant(s).statement: The target is cyclic in the chosen order of variables; rearrangement/sorting of the cyclic order is an essential part of extremal classification.

invariant(s).name: equality_characterization
invariant(s).statement: Equality occurs at a boundary two-level vector (forced by majorization under the cap) together with a rearrangement-maximizing cyclic order.

invariant(s).name: interface_quantity_T
invariant(s).statement: The majorization interface quantity is the sorted partial-sum vector; it is used to compare admissible vectors to the maximal majorization vector under the cap.

essential_assumption(s): The constrained_domain $\{x\in\mathbb{R}^5: 0\le x_i\le 2/5,\sum x_i=1\}$ is compact, so the maximum exists.
The convex_kernel $\phi(t)=t^2$ is convex on the working interval $[0,4/5]$, enabling Jensen/Karamata/Schur-convex arguments.
uniqueness_certificate(s): The objective $M(x)=\max_{\pi}\sum \phi(x_{\pi(i)}+x_{\pi(i+1)})$ is symmetric and convex, hence Schur-convex. Every admissible vector is majorized by the unique cap-saturating boundary vector $(2/5,2/5,1/5,0,0)$, forcing the unique extremal value $K=M(2/5,2/5,1/5,0,0)$. A rearrangement/swap argument then certifies the maximal cyclic order and computes $K$ exactly.

technique(s).name: majorization
technique(s).purpose: Compare admissible vectors to a cap-saturating majorization maximum and transfer extremality to that boundary vector.
outputs: reduction to a finite set of boundary candidate configurations

technique(s).name: smoothing
inputs: fixed_sum vectors with a cap constraint
technique(s).purpose: Use mixing_variables to push mass to the boundary (0 or cap) consistent with Schur-convex monotonicity.

technique(s).name: Rearrangement
technique(s).purpose: Identify the cyclic order maximizing the adjacent-product contribution.
outputs: explicit extremizing permutation pattern (blocks of large values adjacent)

technique(s).name: inequality_chain_design
technique(s).purpose: Combine convexity/majorization with rearrangement to obtain a sharp global bound and equality cases.

technique(s).name: direct_derivation
inputs: local swap inequalities and fixed-multiset counting
technique(s).purpose: Compute the extremal value for the forced boundary multiset without auxiliary variables.
outputs: exact value of $K$ and explicit equality arrangements

theorem(s).name: Jensen
theorem(s).role: From convexity and symmetry, show averaging (mixing) two coordinates cannot increase the objective, establishing Schur-convexity.

theorem(s).name: Karamata
theorem(s).role: Convert the majorization relation $x\prec y$ into the inequality $M(x)\le M(y)$ for the Schur-convex objective.

theorem(s).name: Rearrangement
theorem(s).role: Justify that placing larger values adjacent yields larger cyclic adjacent-product contributions.

theorem(s).name: Chebyshev
theorem(s).role: Provide the local swap comparison $(u-v)(a-b)\ge 0$ used to monotonically improve cyclic order toward the extremizer.

theorem(s).name: Hardy_Littlewood_Polya_Schur_convexity
theorem(s).role: Bridge between convex symmetric objectives, majorization, and extremal two-level vectors.

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: convexity_and_majorization
monotone_improvement_via_smoothing

critical_trick(s): Rewrite the cyclic objective as a maximum over permutations of a convex function; use symmetry+convexity (Jensen) to obtain Schur-convexity.
Use majorization under the cap to force a unique boundary multiset.
Use rearrangement/swap arguments to compute the extremal value for that multiset and certify equality cases.

library(s).name: itertools
library(s).reason: Enumerate all permutations of 5 indices to compute $\max_\pi S(\pi)$ exactly for candidate extreme points.

library(s).name: fractions
library(s).reason: Exact rational arithmetic for $K=p/q$ and for exact evaluation of $S(\pi)$.

library(s).name: random
library(s).reason: Independent Monte Carlo sampling inside the feasible region for a secondary numerical check.

library(s).name: math
library(s).reason: Floating computations and comparisons during random sampling.

task(s).name: primary_structural_check
task(s).reason: Enumerate the extreme points (permutations of $(2/5,2/5,1/5,0,0)$) and for each compute $M(x)=\max_\pi S(\pi)$ by scanning all permutations; confirm the exact maximum $K=6/5$ and compute $N\equiv p+q\pmod{1000}$.

task(s).name: secondary_independent_check
task(s).reason: Randomly sample many admissible vectors with $\max x_i\le 2/5$, compute $M(x)$ by scanning all permutations using floats, and confirm $M(x)\le 6/5$ with near-attainment close to the predicted equality pattern.

equality_cases: x is any permutation of (2/5,2/5,1/5,0,0) and the cyclic order is a shift (or reversal) of (2/5,2/5,1/5,0,0)

title: Sharp constant via uvw+SOS on a degree-4 symmetric form

identity.short_name: Sharp constant via uvw+SOS on a degree-4 symmetric form

problem_spec.summary: Under $a+b+c=1$, determine the sharp constant $K$ in a symmetric homogeneous degree-4 inequality and output $(p+q)\bmod 1000$ for $K=p/q$.
givens: Variables satisfy $a,b,c>0$.
Normalization constraint: $a+b+c=1$.
Fixed coefficient $\lambda=\frac{132}{35}$ in the target form.
Define $K=\frac{p}{q}$ in lowest terms and $N\in\{0,\dots,999\}$ by $N\equiv p+q\pmod{1000}$.
ask: Find the largest real constant $K$ such that $(a^2+b^2+c^2)^2+\lambda abc(a+b+c)\ge K(a+b+c)^4$ holds for all $a,b,c>0$, and characterize all equality cases.
Compute the integer output $N$ defined above.

parameter(s).name: constraint_model
parameter(s).purpose: Select the normalization used by normalize.

parameter(s).name: degree_d
parameter(s).purpose: Controls the total degree after homogenize and thus the SOS/uvw complexity.

parameter(s).name: coefficient_profile
parameter(s).purpose: Varies the symmetric polynomial form $F$ while keeping the same SOS/uvw skeleton.

invariant(s).name: symmetric_homogeneous_form
invariant(s).statement: After homogenize and normalize, the task becomes a three-variable symmetric homogeneous polynomial inequality of total degree $d$ on a compact normalization slice, and the sharp constant is a minimum value.

invariant(s).name: uvw_reduction_validity
invariant(s).statement: For fixed $u=a+b+c$ and $v=ab+bc+ca$, the target expression is affine in $w=abc$ (degree $4$ case), enabling reduction to the two-equal boundary.

invariant(s).name: equality_locus
invariant(s).statement: Equality is attained on the $b=c$ boundary at the minimizer of the reduced one-variable polynomial, and by symmetry its permutations give all equality cases.

invariant(s).name: interface_quantity_T
invariant(s).statement: Define $T=(u,v)$ where $u=a+b+c$ and $v=ab+bc+ca$ after normalize; uvw treats $T$ as fixed and reduces extremality in $w$ to boundary cases.

essential_assumption(s): The normalization constraint makes the feasible set bounded (and its closure compact), so the sharp constant is well-defined as a minimum on the closure.
Coefficient_profile excludes trivial factorization or loss of symmetry.
uniqueness_certificate(s): uvw_reduction makes the gap polynomial affine in $w$, so the minimum occurs at boundary configurations with two equal variables; the sharp $K$ is forced by a double-root (tangency) condition in the one-variable reduction and is certified by a factor/SOS decomposition.

technique(s).name: normalize
technique(s).purpose: Fix scale (here $a+b+c=1$) to reduce degrees of freedom and expose polynomial structure.
outputs: compactified feasible slice and a minimum-value interpretation of $K$

technique(s).name: uvw_reduction
inputs: symmetric polynomial inequality in three variables
technique(s).purpose: Reduce to a one-variable extremal problem by fixing $(u,v)$ and using affinity in $w$.

technique(s).name: SOS
technique(s).purpose: Provide an explicit nonnegativity certificate via factorization and discriminant/complete-square.

technique(s).name: substitution
technique(s).purpose: Convert the symmetric inequality into a single-variable rational/polynomial inequality.

technique(s).name: extremal_principle
inputs: continuous symmetric function on a normalized compact closure
technique(s).purpose: Guarantee an extremum exists and justify searching on uvw boundary cases.

technique(s).name: univariate_critical_point_analysis
technique(s).purpose: Locate the unique global minimizer by factoring $g'(t)$ and performing a sign chart.

theorem(s).name: AM_GM
theorem(s).role: Provide baseline inequality $\frac{a}{b}+\frac{b}{c}+\frac{c}{a}\ge 3$ and identify symmetry-equality conditions.

theorem(s).name: weighted_AM_GM
theorem(s).role: Bound $abc\le \left(\frac{a+b+c}{3}\right)^3$ under normalization.

theorem(s).name: Cauchy_Schwarz
theorem(s).role: Relate $u,v,w$ via $(ab+bc+ca)^2\ge 3abc(a+b+c)$ and rewrite symmetric squares in $(u,v)$.

theorem(s).name: Engel_Titu
theorem(s).role: Bound $\frac{a}{b}+\frac{b}{c}+\frac{c}{a}\ge \frac{(a+b+c)^2}{ab+bc+ca}$ to control the admissible $v$-range.

theorem(s).name: Schur
theorem(s).role: Give a canonical inequality $u^3+9w\ge 4uv$ used to constrain $w$ in uvw arguments.

theorem(s).name: quadratic_discriminant_test
theorem(s).role: Conclude a quadratic is positive for all real $t$ from negative discriminant and positive leading coefficient.

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: symmetry_and_homogeneity_as_invariant_information
nonnegativity_via_sum_of_squares

critical_trick(s): Normalize by $a+b+c=1$ and rewrite the gap polynomial in $(u,v,w)$.
Use uvw affinity in $w$ to restrict to the two-equal boundary.
Solve the one-variable minimization exactly and certify by factor/SOS.

task(s).name: secondary_independent_check

title: sharp K via Maclaurin + power-sum reduction (n=4)

identity.short_name: sharp K via Maclaurin + power-sum reduction (n=4)

problem_spec.summary: Determine the sharp constant K in a homogeneous inequality relating a quadratic power-sum mean and the third elementary symmetric sum for 4 variables; output N=(p+q) mod 1000.
givens: Nonnegative reals a,b,c,d not all zero.
Define K as the largest real constant such that (a^2+b^2+c^2+d^2)^2(a+b+c+d)^2 >= K(abc+abd+acd+bcd)^2 holds for all such quadruples.
After writing K=p/q in lowest terms, output N in {0,...,999} with N \equiv p+q (mod 1000).
ask: Determine the sharp constant K and compute the normalized integer output N.

parameter(s).name: n
parameter(s).purpose: Dimension of the variable tuple and combinatorial constants in Maclaurin.

title: GCD \to discriminant \to digit sum \to engineered (2γ+1) derivative \to splitting field \to log modulus

identity.short_name: GCD \to discriminant \to digit sum \to engineered (2γ+1) derivative \to splitting field \to log modulus

problem_spec.summary: Compute F=gcd(P,Q) for two integer polynomials, take the discriminant δ of the (quadratic) gcd and its digit sum γ. Over F_p, define an engineered polynomial f(x)=x^{pδ}+c x^{2γ+1}-x where c is the inverse of (2γ+1) in F_p. In characteristic p the derivative kills x^{pδ} and the choice of c forces f'(x)=x^{2γ}-1. Determine the smallest finite field extension where x^{2γ}-1 splits using multiplicative order, evaluate log_{p^2}(m), and compute δ modulo 2 \cdot log_{p^2}(m)+γ.
givens: P(x), Q(x) are degree-6 polynomials in ℤ[x].
F(x)=gcd(P(x),Q(x)) in ℤ[x], fixed by requiring positive leading coefficient.
F(x) is quadratic: F(x)=ax^2+bx+c with a>0.
δ = discriminant(F) = b^2-4ac (an integer).
γ = sum of decimal digits of δ.
p is a prime (this instance uses p=29).
Work in _p and define c = (2γ+1)^{-1} \in _p (assumed to exist).
Define f(x)=x^{pδ}+c x^{2γ+1}-x in _p[x] and let f'(x) be its formal derivative.
m is the smallest prime power such that f'(x) splits completely into linear factors over _m.
ask: Compute output = δ mod (2 \cdot log_{p^2}(m)+γ), with the residue taken in {0,...,2 \cdot log_{p^2}(m)+γ-1}.

parameter(s).name: P_coeffs
parameter(s).domain: integer coefficient list defining a degree-6 polynomial in ℤ[x]
parameter(s).purpose: determines the gcd F and hence δ and γ

parameter(s).name: Q_coeffs
parameter(s).domain: integer coefficient list defining a degree-6 polynomial in ℤ[x]
parameter(s).purpose: determines the gcd F and hence δ and γ

parameter(s).name: base_prime_p
parameter(s).purpose: sets characteristic for the formal derivative and determines finite-field sizes p^d

parameter(s).name: exponent_template
parameter(s).domain: f(x)=x^{pδ}+c x^{2γ+1}-x with c=(2γ+1)^{-1} in F_p
parameter(s).purpose: forces derivative cancellation of the pδ term and normalizes the x^{2γ} coefficient to 1

parameter(s).name: log_base
parameter(s).purpose: fixes the logarithm base so log_{p^2}(p^d)=d/2 and 2 \cdot log_{p^2}(m)=d is an integer

invariant(s).name: gcd_uniqueness_in_Zx
invariant(s).statement: F(x)=gcd(P,Q) is uniquely determined up to ±1, fixed by requiring positive leading coefficient.

invariant(s).name: discriminant_is_integer
invariant(s).statement: For quadratic F(x)=ax^2+bx+c in ℤ[x], δ=b^2-4ac is an integer determined by coefficients.

invariant(s).name: derivative_cancellation_in_char_p
invariant(s).statement: In characteristic p, (x^{pδ})' = pδ \cdot x^{pδ-1} = 0, so the x^{pδ} term vanishes in f'(x).

invariant(s).name: engineered_inverse_for_unit_x_2gamma_term
invariant(s).statement: With c=(2γ+1)^{-1} in _p, we have (c x^{2γ+1})' = c(2γ+1)x^{2γ} = x^{2γ}, forcing f'(x)=x^{2γ}-1.

invariant(s).name: splitting_of_xn_minus_1
invariant(s).statement: If gcd(p,2γ)=1, then x^{2γ}-1 splits over _{p^d} iff 2γ | (p^d-1). The minimal such d is ord_{2γ}(p).

invariant(s).name: log_p2_prime_power
invariant(s).statement: If m=p^d then log_{p^2}(m)=d/2, hence 2 \cdot log_{p^2}(m)=d is an integer.

essential_assumption(s): The computed gcd F(x) has degree 2 (quadratic).
2γ+1 is nonzero in _p (equivalently p ∤ (2γ+1)), so c=(2γ+1)^{-1} exists.
gcd(p,2γ)=1 so x^{2γ}-1 has distinct roots and the roots-of-unity splitting criterion applies cleanly.
m is taken among finite fields _{p^d} (prime-power sizes).
uniqueness_certificate(s): F is uniquely determined as the gcd in ℤ[x] with positive leading coefficient, hence δ and γ are deterministic. The coefficient c=(2γ+1)^{-1} is unique in _p, so f' is uniquely x^{2γ}-1. The smallest extension degree d for splitting is uniquely ord_{2γ}(p), hence m=p^d is unique. Since 2 \cdot log_{p^2}(m)=d and γ are fixed, the modulus (d+γ) and the residue δ mod (d+γ) are uniquely determined.

technique(s).name: compute_gcd_by_structured_difference
technique(s).purpose: compute F=gcd(P,Q) using that gcd divides P-Q and a visible common quadratic factor

technique(s).name: compute_quadratic_discriminant
technique(s).purpose: obtain δ=b^2-4ac

technique(s).name: digit_sum_evaluation
technique(s).purpose: compute γ as the sum of decimal digits of δ

technique(s).name: invert_scalar_in_Fp
technique(s).purpose: compute c=(2γ+1)^{-1} to normalize the derivative coefficient

technique(s).name: formal_derivative_engineering_to_x_2gamma_minus_1
technique(s).purpose: use characteristic-p cancellation and inverse normalization to force f'(x)=x^{2γ}-1

technique(s).name: minimal_splitting_field_for_xn_minus_1
technique(s).purpose: find minimal d such that n | (p^d-1), i.e. d=ord_n(p), so m=p^d

technique(s).name: evaluate_logarithm_of_prime_power_and_reduce_modulus
technique(s).purpose: compute 2 \cdot log_{p^2}(m)=d and then reduce δ modulo d+γ

theorem(s).name: Euclidean_algorithm_in_Zx
theorem(s).role: defines and guarantees uniqueness of gcd up to units in ℤ[x]

theorem(s).name: quadratic_discriminant_formula
theorem(s).role: δ=b^2-4ac for ax^2+bx+c

theorem(s).name: formal_derivative_in_characteristic_p
theorem(s).role: (x^{pk})' = 0 and (x^n)' = n x^{n-1} with coefficients reduced mod p

theorem(s).name: finite_field_multiplicative_group_is_cyclic
theorem(s).role: _{p^d}^× is cyclic of order p^d-1

theorem(s).name: roots_of_unity_splitting_criterion
theorem(s).role: x^n-1 splits over _q iff n | (q-1) when gcd(n,char)=1

theorem(s).name: multiplicative_order_mod_n
theorem(s).role: minimal d with p^d \equiv 1 (mod n) equals ord_n(p)

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: Euclidean_algorithm_and_divisibility
cyclic_group_order_divisibility
formal_derivative_rules_in_char_p

critical_trick(s): Use P-Q to isolate the quadratic gcd immediately.
Engineer f so that the pδ-exponent term vanishes under differentiation and the x^{2γ} coefficient becomes 1 via c=(2γ+1)^{-1}.
Reduce splitting to an order computation ord_{2γ}(p) and turn 2 \cdot log_{p^2}(m) into the integer d.

title: GCD \to discriminant \to digit sum \to engineered derivative \to splitting field \to affine-log modulus

Determine the smallest finite field extension where x^γ-1 splits using multiplicative order, evaluate log_{p^2}(m), and compute δ modulo an affine function 2 \cdot log_{p^2}(m)+γ.

m is the smallest prime power such that f'(x) splits completely into linear factors over _m.

title: operator(s):

identity.short_name: Count bounded cubics with exactly one integer root among {0,1}

subdomain(s): discriminant criteria for quadratic integer roots and repeated roots

problem_spec.summary: Count monic integer cubics with coefficients in a bounded box that have at least one forced root in {0,1}, no repeated roots, and exactly one integer root; split on which forced root is the unique integer root and filter the quadratic cofactor via a discriminant "not a square condition.
givens: Forced root condition: P(0)P(1)=0 (so at least one of 0 or 1 is a root).
P has no repeated roots (squarefree over ℂ).
P has exactly one integer root.
ask: Count the number of such polynomials P.

parameter(s).name: coefficient_box
parameter(s).purpose: finite search space enabling exact counting

parameter(s).name: forced_root_set
parameter(s).purpose: guarantees at least one small integer root and drives case split

parameter(s).name: uniqueness_of_integer_root
parameter(s).purpose: eliminates cases with two integer roots and forces the remaining quadratic factor to have no integer roots

parameter(s).name: multiplicity_constraint
parameter(s).purpose: excludes repeated roots (discriminant nonzero)

invariant(s).name: forced_root_case_split
invariant(s).statement: Since P(0)P(1)=0 and P has exactly one integer root, the unique integer root r is exactly one of {0,1}.

invariant(s).name: factorization_by_integer_root
invariant(s).statement: If r is a root then P(x)=(x-r)Q(x) with Q monic quadratic with integer coefficients.

invariant(s).name: quadratic_integer_root_test
invariant(s).statement: Q has an integer root iff its discriminant is a perfect square; Q has a repeated root iff its discriminant is 0.

title: reasoning_components:

identity.short_name: Count non-palindromic bounded quartics with reciprocal product a square

subdomain(s): integer polynomials and squares in Z[x]
gcd factor splitting in polynomial rings

problem_spec.summary: For monic quartics with coefficients in a bounded integer set, impose that the product with the reciprocal polynomial is a perfect square in Z[x], and additionally exclude palindromic polynomials; classify and count the remaining solutions.
givens: Define P*(x)=x^4 P(1/x)=x^4 + c x^3 + b x^2 + a x + 1.
Condition: P(x)P*(x) is a perfect square in Z[x].
Non-palindromic filter: a \neq c.
ask: Count the number of such polynomials P.

parameter(s).name: coefficient_set
parameter(s).purpose: bounds the search space for (a,b,c)

parameter(s).name: reciprocal_operator
parameter(s).purpose: imposes reciprocal symmetry constraints

parameter(s).name: square_condition
parameter(s).purpose: forces even multiplicities of irreducible factors in P \cdot P*

parameter(s).name: nonpalindromic_filter
parameter(s).purpose: excludes the trivial palindromic branch (a=c) where P \cdot P* is automatically a square

invariant(s).name: palindromic_implies_square
invariant(s).statement: If P*=P (equivalently c=a), then P \cdot P* = P^2 is a square in Z[x].

invariant(s).name: gcd_with_reciprocal_self_reciprocal
invariant(s).statement: D=gcd(P,P*) satisfies D*=±D; choosing D monic makes D*=D (self-reciprocal).

invariant(s).name: degree_2_gcd_forces_palindromic
invariant(s).statement: If deg gcd(P,P*)=2, then both the gcd and the remaining quadratic factor have constant term 1 and are self-reciprocal, forcing P*=P (palindromic).

invariant(s).name: coprime_product_square_forcing
invariant(s).statement: If gcd(P,P*)=1 and P \cdot P* is a square in Z[x], then P is a square in Z[x] up to a unit; monicity forces P=Q^2 with Q\inZ[x].

invariant(s).name: odd_degree_not_square
invariant(s).statement: A nonzero square polynomial in Z[x] has even degree; in particular, no cubic in Z[x] is a square.

title: Construct explicit sl2 raising-operator matrices e_V,e_W with invariant symmetric forms Q_V,Q_W,

identity.short_name: Centralizer dimension of a tensor-sum nilpotent in so(35)

problem_spec.summary: Use the Jacobson-Morozov sl2-model and Clebsch-Gordan decomposition to determine the Jordan partition of a tensor-sum nilpotent operator on an orthogonal tensor product space, then compute the dimension of its centralizer in so(U) from the partition via the gl-centralizer and the orthogonal correction term.
givens: V is a 7-dimensional complex vector space with a non-degenerate symmetric bilinear form.
W is a 5-dimensional complex vector space with a non-degenerate symmetric bilinear form.
A \in End(V) is nilpotent with one Jordan block of size 7 and is skew-symmetric w.r.t. the form on V.
B \in End(W) is nilpotent with one Jordan block of size 5 and is skew-symmetric w.r.t. the form on W.
U = V ⊗ W with the induced non-degenerate symmetric bilinear form Q.
T = A ⊗ I_W + I_V ⊗ B.
so(U,Q) = {S \in End(U) : S is skew-symmetric w.r.t. Q}.
ask: Compute dim_C { S \in so(U,Q) : ST = TS }.

parameter(s).name: dim_V
parameter(s).purpose: orthogonal sl2-irrep dimension for V (here 7)

parameter(s).name: dim_W
parameter(s).purpose: orthogonal sl2-irrep dimension for W (here 5)

parameter(s).name: tensor_pattern
parameter(s).purpose: determines the diagonal sl2-action and hence the Jordan partition of T

parameter(s).name: nilpotent_block_sizes
parameter(s).domain: single Jordan block in each factor
parameter(s).purpose: forces A,B to be principal nilpotents (raising operators)

invariant(s).name: orthogonal_structure
invariant(s).statement: The symmetric non-degenerate forms on V and W induce a symmetric non-degenerate form Q on U=V⊗W, and T lies in so(U,Q).

invariant(s).name: sl2_structure
invariant(s).statement: By Jacobson-Morozov, A and B are raising operators of sl2-triples, so V≅ and W≅ as sl2-modules; with the diagonal action on U, the raising operator is T.

invariant(s).name: jordan_partition_of_T
invariant(s).statement: Clebsch-Gordan gives U≅⊕⊕⊕⊕, hence the Jordan partition of T is λ=(11,9,7,5,3).

invariant(s).name: orthogonal_centralizer_dimension_formula
invariant(s).statement: If λ is the Jordan partition of a nilpotent in so, then dim Z_so(T) = (Σ_k (λ'_k)^2 - o(λ))/2, where λ' is the conjugate partition and o(λ) is the number of odd parts of λ.

essential_assumption(s): Field is C (characteristic 0), so Jacobson-Morozov and complete reducibility of sl2-modules apply.
The invariant bilinear forms are symmetric (odd-dimensional irreps), so the sl2-actions land in so.
T is interpreted as the diagonal sl2 raising operator on U.
uniqueness_certificate(s): The sl2-module structures of V and W are uniquely determined by the principal nilpotent hypotheses. Clebsch-Gordan uniquely fixes the decomposition of U and thus the Jordan partition λ of T. The orthogonal centralizer dimension is a deterministic function of λ, hence the answer is unique.

technique(s).name: Jacobson-Morozov sl2-triple construction
technique(s).purpose: realize A and B as sl2 raising operators on irreducible orthogonal modules

technique(s).name: Clebsch-Gordan decomposition
technique(s).purpose: decompose U=V⊗W into irreducible sl2-summands

technique(s).name: gl-centralizer via conjugate partition
technique(s).purpose: compute dim C_gl(T) = Σ (lambda'_k)^2

technique(s).name: orthogonal correction by odd-part count
technique(s).purpose: compute dim Z_so(T) = (dim_C_gl - o(lambda))/2

theorem(s).name: Jacobson-Morozov theorem
theorem(s).role: embeds a nilpotent element into an sl2-triple

theorem(s).name: Clebsch-Gordan rule for sl2
theorem(s).role: decomposes tensor products of sl2 irreducibles

theorem(s).name: C[t]-module centralizer formula
theorem(s).role: gives dim C_gl(T) = Σ (λ'_k)^2 for nilpotent Jordan type λ

theorem(s).name: orthogonal nilpotent centralizer dimension formula
theorem(s).role: gives dim Z_so(T) = (Σ (λ'_k)^2 - o(λ))/2

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: Jordan canonical form as a C[t]-module decomposition

Read off Jordan block sizes from the irreducible sl2 decomposition (Clebsch-Gordan).

identity.short_name: Double-root cubic count -> reciprocal quartic triple-square sum

subdomain(s): rational root theorem and Vieta relations

problem_spec.summary: Composite (CHAIN) family: compute bridge quantity T from component A (Count bounded cubics with exactly one double root and both roots in (2,2)); then use that T as a required parameter in component B (Triple products of squared roots via reciprocal Newton sums (quartic)) to determine the final output (reduced mod 1000). Instance summary: Count monic cubics with a prescribed double root and simple root in {-1,0,1} to get T, then compute a reciprocal quartic triple-product-of-squares sum via reciprocal Newton identities, reduced mod 1000.
givens: Cubic p has a double root r in {-1,0,1} and a simple root s in {-1,0,1}, rs.
T is the number of such cubics.
ask: Compute U mod 1000.

invariant(s).statement: For product 1, _{i<j<k} u_i^2u_j^2u_k^2= 1/u_i^2 = p2.

invariant(s).name: multiplicity_characterization
invariant(s).statement: A real monic cubic has exactly two distinct real roots iff it factors as (xr)^2(xs) with rs real, equivalently iff gcd(P,P) has degree 1 (or discriminant =0 but P not a perfect cube).

invariant(s).name: double_root_rational_then_integer
invariant(s).statement: The double root r is a root of gcd(P,P), hence rational; since P is monic with integer coefficients, the Rational Root Theorem forces r to be an integer dividing c.

invariant(s).name: interval_forces_small_root_set
invariant(s).statement: With r integer and r(2,2), we must have r{1,0,1}. Vieta then gives s=a2r, so s is also an integer and s(2,2) forces s{1,0,1}.

invariant(s).name: coefficient_reconstruction_from_roots
invariant(s).statement: If P(x)=(xr)^2(xs), then (a,b,c)=(-(2r+s), r^2+2rs, r^2 s). Thus each admissible ordered pair (r,s) yields exactly one coefficient triple.

invariant(s).name: complementary_product_identity
invariant(s).statement: For roots r1..r4 with product e4=a4, we have _{i<j<k} r_i^2 r_j^2 r_k^2 = e4^2 _{i=1}^4 1/r_i^2.

invariant(s).name: reciprocal_polynomial_roots
invariant(s).statement: If a40, then s_i=1/r_i are roots of the monic reciprocal polynomial Q(x) = (1/a4) x^4 P(1/x) = x^4 + (a3/a4)x^3 + (a2/a4)x^2 + (a1/a4)x + (1/a4).

invariant(s).name: newton_girard_for_p2
invariant(s).statement: For monic Q(x)=x^4+A1 x^3+A2 x^2+A3 x+A4 with roots s_i and p_k= s_i^k, Newton gives p1=-A1 and p2 = A1^2 - 2A2, uniquely determined by (A1,A2).

invariant(s).name: coefficient_only_closed_form
invariant(s).statement: Combining the reciprocal transform and Newton for p2 yields T = a4^2 \cdot p2 = a4^2 \cdot ((a3/a4)^2 - 2(a2/a4)) = a3^2 - 2 a2 a4, so T is a coefficient polynomial independent of a1.

essential_assumption(s): Distinctness rs ensures degree 3 with exactly one repeated root.
The interval is open; roots equal to ±2 are excluded.

title: Centered symmetric quartic no-real-root count -> cubic power-sum partial sum

identity.short_name: Centered symmetric quartic no-real-root count -> cubic power-sum partial sum

problem_spec.summary: Composite (CHAIN) family: compute bridge quantity T from component A (Count monic quartics symmetric about 1 with no real roots); then use that T as a required parameter in component B (Partial sum of cubic-root power sums modulo 1000) to determine the final output (reduced mod 1000). Instance summary: Count (a,b) in a centered-even quartic y^4+ay^2+b with positivity on y^2>=0 to get T, then use T as the endpoint of a fixed cubic-root power-sum recurrence sum modulo 1000.
givens: T counts pairs (a,b) for which P_{a,b} has no real roots.
alpha,beta,gamma are roots of y^3-3y+1=0; S_n=alpha^n+beta^n+gamma^n.
ask: Compute N=(\sum_{n=0}^T S_n) mod 1000.

invariant(s).statement: Let y=x-1 and Q(y)=P(y+1). Then Q is even: Q(-y)=Q(y), so Q(y)=y^4+by^2+d.

invariant(s).statement: For monic quartics with P(2-x)=P(x), necessarily a_3=-4 and a_1=8-2a_2. Equivalently P(x)=(x-1)^4+b(x-1)^2+d with integers b=a_2-6 and d=a_0-a_2+5.

invariant(s).name: positivity_reduction_to_quadratic
invariant(s).statement: With Q(y)=y^4+by^2+d, "no real roots (monic quartic) is equivalent to Q(y)>0 for all y, which is equivalent to z^2+bz+d>0 for all z>=0 via z=y^2.

invariant(s).statement: Each root r satisfies r^3 = 3r - 1.

invariant(s).name: partial_sum_determinism
invariant(s).statement: Once S_0,,S_N are determined, T=_{n=0}^N S_n is uniquely determined.

invariant(s).name: bridge_interface_invariant
invariant(s).statement: The composite is coupled by the interface variable T: component A uniquely determines T, and component B is defined/solved only after substituting this specific T into its parameter slot.

essential_assumption(s): Strict inequality is used so boundary cases (zero) are excluded, guaranteeing no real roots.
Coefficients are integers and the polynomial is monic of degree 4.
"No real roots" is interpreted as P(x)0 for all real x; for monic quartics this is equivalent to strict positivity.
Integer coefficient bounds ensure finiteness of the search/count.
The polynomial is monic of degree 3, so every root satisfies the same cubic relation, yielding a linear recurrence for S_n.
Roots are counted with multiplicity (standard for Vieta/Newton sums).
Modular reduction is applied to integer values obtained from the recurrence.
The bridge quantity T computed from component A is guaranteed to be an integer in the admissible range required by component B (e.g., as an index, bound, or group-action parameter).
uniqueness_certificate(s): The finite case split on a gives a unique integer T.
Given T, the recurrence uniquely determines the partial sum modulo 1000.

Symmetry about x=1 forces the unique representation P(x)=(x-1)^4+b(x-1)^2+d with integers b,d. The coefficient bounds translate to a finite set of integer pairs (b,d). The condition "no real roots is equivalent to a strict quadratic inequality z^2+bz+d>0 on z>=0, which yields necessary-and-sufficient inequalities in (b,d). Therefore the admissible set is a uniquely determined finite set of lattice points, so the final count is unique.
The recurrence S_n = 3S_{n-2} - S_{n-3} together with initial values S_0=3, S_1=0, S_2=6 uniquely determines S_0,,S_20, hence uniquely determines T and T mod 1000.
(N1 necessity) If T is not computed from component A, component B has multiple admissible outputs across different possible parameter choices; thus the composite is ill-posed without the bridge.
(N2 non-factorization) The final answer is not a trivial Combine(ansA, ansB): the computed T restricts/changes the admissible branch structure inside component B before its output is even defined.

title: Conjugated differential operator -> quadratic-probe resultant

identity.short_name: Conjugated differential operator -> quadratic-probe resultant

Instance summary: Solve two conjugated differential-operator equations for quadratics, form a complex squared-distance bridge parameter T, then compute a cubicquadratic resultant via evaluation at i, reduced mod 1000.
givens: Operators L_+(P)=e^{x} D^2(e^{-x}P) and L_-(P)=e^{-x} D^2(e^{x}P).
Quadratics P_+,P_- satisfy L_+(P_+)=x and L_-(P_-)=x.
ask: Compute N=Res(f,x^2+1) mod 1000.

invariant(s).name: integer_valued_symmetry

Fundamental Theorem of Algebra: P has exactly three complex roots counted with multiplicity.

Restricting to quadratics, T_{} becomes a linear map with nonzero action on the x^2 coefficient (12a for +, 20a for ), producing a triangular coefficient-matching system that uniquely determines (a,b,c). Subsequent evaluation and modular reduction are deterministic.

Because E(x)=xP(x)+Q(x), the product  E(r_i) equals  Q(r_i)=Res(P,Q). The resultant is uniquely determined by the coefficients of P and Q, hence the integer product is unique. Reducing modulo m gives a unique residue in {0,...,m-1}.

title: Finite-field inversion 2-cycle count -> central trinomial-type coefficient mod 1000

identity.short_name: Finite-field inversion 2-cycle count -> central trinomial-type coefficient mod 1000

subdomain(s): affine-linear constraint counting over finite fields

problem_spec.summary: Composite (CHAIN) family: compute bridge quantity T from component A (Count F13 degree-8 twisted-reciprocal polynomials with exactly two inversion 2-cycles as roots); then use that T as a required parameter in component B (Coefficient lag autocorrelation via Fourier coefficient and cosine moments) to determine the final output (reduced mod 1000). Instance summary: Count reciprocal quartics over F11 whose roots are exactly two inversion 2-cycles to obtain T, then compute the coefficient [x^T](1+x+x^2)^{2T} via coefficient-extraction/Parseval viewpoint and reduce mod 1000.
givens: Field is F_11; f(x)=x^4+ax^3+bx^2+ax+1 is monic reciprocal.
Goodness: roots in F_11^* form exactly two inversion 2-cycles (no 1 roots).
ask: Compute N = [x^T]A(x) mod 1000.

invariant(s).statement: Using x^(q-1)=1 on F_q^* and comparing reduced coefficients modulo x^(q-1)-1, the condition f(x)=x^4 f(1/x) on F_13^* forces a5=a6=a7=0 and a4=a0, a3=a1, leaving the template f(x)=x^8 + a0 x^4 + a1 x^3 + a2 x^2 + a1 x + a0.

invariant(s).name: parameter_reduction_by_fixed_coefficient
invariant(s).statement: Fixing the x^3 coefficient a3=5 forces a1=5, so the family is parameterized by (a0,a2)F_13^2.

invariant(s).name: inversion_orbits_in_Fq_star
invariant(s).statement: Inversion partitions F_13^* into fixed points {1,-1} and five 2-cycles: {2,7},{3,9},{4,10},{5,8},{6,11}.

invariant(s).name: root_orbit_closure_under_twisted_symmetry
invariant(s).statement: If rF_13^* and f(r)=0, then 0=f(r)=r^4 f(1/r), so f(1/r)=0. Thus roots occur in inversion-orbits.

invariant(s).name: exact_root_count_to_orbit_selection
invariant(s).statement: With fixed points excluded and root_count_target=4, the root set in F_13^* must consist of exactly two inversion 2-cycles (and no other orbit).

invariant(s).name: orbit_root_conditions_are_affine_lines
invariant(s).statement: For each 2-cycle representative r, the condition f(r)=0 is an affine-linear equation in (a0,a2), i.e. a line in F_13^2. Thus "exactly two 2-cycles are roots corresponds to points lying on exactly two of the five orbit-lines and not on the fixed-point root lines.

invariant(s).name: correlation_is_fourier_coefficient
invariant(s).statement: If P(x)= a_k x^k, then _k a_k a_{k+t} = [x^t] P(x)P(x^{-1}) = (1/2)_0^{2} |P(e^{i})|^2 e^{-it} d. For real coefficients, this equals (1/2) |P(e^{i})|^2 cos(t) d.

invariant(s).name: bridge_interface_invariant
invariant(s).statement: The composite is coupled by the interface variable T: component A uniquely determines T, and component B is defined/solved only after substituting this specific T into its parameter slot.

essential_assumption(s): Distinct polynomials correspond to distinct root multisets in F_11^*.
The symmetry f(x)=x^4 f(1/x) is required only on F_13^*, not as a polynomial identity in F_13[x].
Roots counted are distinct and lie in F_13^* (x=0 excluded).
Fixed-point exclusion f(1)0 is enforced as part of the specification.
P has real coefficients so P(e^{-iθ}) is the complex conjugate of P(e^{iθ}).
Orthogonality of exponentials on [0,2\pi] is used to identify coefficients via integrals.
Even-power cosine moment formula applies: (1/2)_0^{2} cos^{2j}() d = C(2j,j)/4^j.
The bridge quantity T computed from component A is guaranteed to be an integer in the admissible range required by component B (e.g., as an index, bound, or group-action parameter).
uniqueness_certificate(s): There are exactly 4 nontrivial inversion pairs, hence unique T=6.
Given T, the coefficient in A(x) is uniquely determined and reduced mod 1000.

The symmetry and fixed coefficient reduce f to a finite 2-parameter family (a0,a2)F_13^2. Each inversion 2-cycle root condition is a single affine-linear constraint in these parameters. Therefore the valid polynomials correspond to a finite, explicitly enumerable set of parameter points, uniquely determining the count.

Because every element of F_13^* lies in exactly one inversion-orbit, verifying that a parameter point lies on exactly two orbit-lines (and on neither fixed-point line) is equivalent to verifying the exact root-orbit pattern; hence correctness is checkable and the answer is unique.

The polynomial P is fixed by the givens, so the coefficient sequence (a_k) is unique. The target C is the coefficient [x^t] of the uniquely determined Laurent polynomial P(x)P(x^{-1}), equivalently a unique Fourier coefficient of the uniquely determined function |P(e^{iθ})|^2. Therefore C is uniquely determined.
(N1 necessity) If T is not computed from component A, component B has multiple admissible outputs across different possible parameter choices; thus the composite is ill-posed without the bridge.
(N2 non-factorization) The final answer is not a trivial Combine(ansA, ansB): the computed T restricts/changes the admissible branch structure inside component B before its output is even defined.

The computed gcd F(x) has degree 2 (quadratic).
2γ+1 is nonzero in _p (equivalently p ∤ (2γ+1)), so c=(2γ+1)^{-1} exists.

F is uniquely determined as the gcd in ℤ[x] with positive leading coefficient, hence δ and γ are deterministic. The coefficient c=(2γ+1)^{-1} is unique in _p, so f' is uniquely x^{2γ}-1. The smallest extension degree d for splitting is uniquely ord_{2γ}(p), hence m=p^d is unique. Since 2 \cdot log_{p^2}(m)=d and γ are fixed, the modulus (d+γ) and the residue δ mod (d+γ) are uniquely determined.

title: Involutions in truncated-ring automorphisms -> Gaussian-binomial codim-2 subspace count

identity.short_name: Involutions in truncated-ring automorphisms -> Gaussian-binomial codim-2 subspace count

subdomain(s): composition of truncated power series over finite fields
finite abelian groups and invariant factors
quotienting by a fixed cyclic subgroup

problem_spec.summary: Composite (CHAIN) family: compute bridge quantity T from component A (Count order-2 automorphisms of F7[x]/(x^6)); then use that T as a required parameter in component B (Count HG with gH and G/H(2^5,2^2), mod 1000) to determine the final output (reduced mod 1000). Instance summary: Count involutions in Aut(F7[x]/(x^3)) by composing truncated series to obtain T, then count codimension-2 subspaces containing a fixed vector in F2^T using Gaussian binomial coefficients; output mod 1000.
givens: R=F_7[x]/(x^3); automorphisms are phi_{a,b}(x)=ax+bx^2.
Involution means phi_{a,b}^2=id; T counts such automorphisms.
V=F_2^T with fixed g0; N counts subspaces H containing g with dim(V/H)=2.
ask: Compute N mod 1000.

parameter(s).purpose: parametrizes automorphisms via the image of x

parameter(s).purpose: ambient finite abelian 2-group

parameter(s).domain: specified element g in G, typically a 2^t multiple of a generator in one summand
parameter(s).purpose: imposes the constraint g  H

parameter(s).domain: nonincreasing tuple (b1,...,bs) with bj>=1 defining Q Z/2^{bj}Z
parameter(s).purpose: required quotient type

parameter(s).purpose: final reduction (here m=1000)

parameter(s).domain: derived integer computed from component A invariants
parameter(s).purpose: interface quantity produced by component A and consumed by component B (non-stapled coupling).

invariant(s).statement: phi_{a,b}^2(x)a^2x+b(a+a^2)x^2 (mod x^3).

invariant(s).statement: # {codim-2 subspaces containing g} = [T-1 choose 2]_2.

invariant(s).statement: is an F_p-algebra automorphism iff (x)=a x + higher terms (mod x^n) with aF_p^.

invariant(s).name: composition_mod_truncation
invariant(s).statement: ^k=id is equivalent to f^{k}(x)  x (mod x^n) where f(x)=(x) as a truncated series.

invariant(s).name: leading_coefficient_constraint
invariant(s).statement: From the x-coefficient in f^{k}(x)x, one gets a^k=1 in F_p^ (here a^2=1).

invariant(s).name: triangular_coefficient_system
invariant(s).statement: Expanding f^{2}(x) mod x^n yields a (nearly) triangular system of polynomial equations in b,c,d,e solvable by casework on a.

invariant(s).name: containment_reduction_to_quotient
invariant(s).statement: Subgroups HG with gH and G/HQ correspond to kernels of surjections f:GQ with f(g)=0, equivalently to surjections G/gQ. Hence N = |Epi(G/g,Q)| / |Aut(Q)|.

invariant(s).name: hom_count_depends_only_on_invariant_factors
invariant(s).statement: |Hom(G,Q)| is determined by the exponents via |Hom(Z/2^a,Q)| = |Q[2^a]| = 2^{Σ_j min(a,bj)}.

invariant(s).name: epimorphisms_via_maximal_subgroups_rank2
invariant(s).statement: For rank-2 Q, maximal subgroups have index 2 and correspond to the 3 nonzero lines in Q/2Q(Z/2)^2; inclusionexclusion over these maximal subgroups yields |Epi(G,Q)|.

invariant(s).name: bridge_interface_invariant
invariant(s).statement: The composite is coupled by the interface variable T: component A uniquely determines T, and component B is defined/solved only after substituting this specific T into its parameter slot.

essential_assumption(s): Vector spaces are over F2; g is nonzero.
Automorphisms are F_7-algebra automorphisms (fix scalars in F_7).
Composition is computed in R, i.e., all series are truncated mod x^6.
All groups are finite abelian 2-groups written in invariant-factor form.
The condition gH is interpreted literally as subgroup containment.
Aut(Q) acts freely on Epi(G,Q) by postcomposition, so dividing counts kernels.
The bridge quantity T computed from component A is guaranteed to be an integer in the admissible range required by component B (e.g., as an index, bound, or group-action parameter).
uniqueness_certificate(s): The involution constraints determine a finite count T uniquely.
Given T, the Gaussian binomial coefficient gives a unique N.

Each automorphism corresponds to a unique coefficient tuple (a,b,c,d,e); the system f^{2}(x)x (mod x^6) has finitely many solutions in F_7, so |I| is uniquely determined once the coefficient system is solved.

The constraint gH forces reduction to G' = G/g. The kernel of any surjection G'Q gives such an H, and two surjections have the same kernel iff they differ by Aut(Q). Since |Epi(G',Q)| and |Aut(Q)| are uniquely determined from invariant factors and maximal-subgroup inclusionexclusion, N mod 1000 is unique.
(N1 necessity) If T is not computed from component A, component B has multiple admissible outputs across different possible parameter choices; thus the composite is ill-posed without the bridge.

title: Order-3 determinant-2 matrices in GL2(F7) -> weighted Hilbert coefficient

identity.short_name: Order-3 determinant-2 matrices in GL2(F7) -> weighted Hilbert coefficient

subdomain(s): Chevalley restriction and Weyl group invariants
graded dimension / Hilbert series coefficient extraction

problem_spec.summary: Composite (CHAIN) family: compute bridge quantity T from component A (Order-3 automorphisms of F7[x,y]/(x,y)^3 with det=2 linear part (mod 1000)); then use that T as a required parameter in component B (SL4 adjoint invariant polynomials (degree 50)) to determine the final output (reduced mod 1000). Instance summary: Count matrices A in GL2(F7) with det=2 and A^3=I by eigenvalue/conjugacy-class analysis to get T, then count weighted-degree monomials for generator degrees 2,3,4 at degree T, reduced mod 1000.
givens: A in GL2(F7) with det(A)=2 and A^3=I; T is the count.
Invariant ring generator degrees are 2,3,4.
ask: Compute U=#{(i,j,k)>=0:2i+3j+4k=T} mod 1000.

parameter(s).purpose: ensures ^3=0 so cubic expansions truncate linearly in the quadratic part

parameter(s).purpose: torsion constraint on the linear part and the automorphism cube

parameter(s).purpose: replaces the SL2 constraint by a fixed determinant constraint in GL2

parameter(s).name: modulus
parameter(s).purpose: output reduction (here 1000)

parameter(s).name: polynomial_degree_d
parameter(s).purpose: selects the graded piece of the invariant polynomial ring to be counted

parameter(s).purpose: fixes the Weyl group (S_4) and fundamental invariant degrees (2,3,4) for the adjoint action

parameter(s).domain: derived integer computed from component A invariants

invariant(s).statement: A^3=I implies eigenvalues lie in {1,2,4} over F7.

invariant(s).name: conjugacy_class_size
invariant(s).statement: Class size = |GL2| / |centralizer| for a representative.

invariant(s).name: hilbert_monomial_count
invariant(s).statement: U is # solutions to 2i+3j+4k=T for i,j,k>=0.

invariant(s).name: graded_piece_decomposition
invariant(s).statement: V=/^2 is 2-dimensional and W=^2 is 3-dimensional; moreover W≅Sym^2(V) as a GL(V)-module since char(p)\neq2.

invariant(s).name: automorphism_parameterization
invariant(s).statement: Any _p-algebra automorphism of _p[x,y]/(x,y)^3 is determined uniquely by (A,u) with A\inGL(V) and u\inHom(V,W).

invariant(s).name: cube_condition_linearizes
invariant(s).statement: With ^3=0, (A,u)^3=(A^3,(I+T+T^2)u), where T(u)=Sym^2(A)∘u∘A^{-1}. Hence ψ^3=id iff A^3=I and (I+T+T^2)u=0.

invariant(s).name: determinant_fixed_order3_types_in_GL2_F7
invariant(s).statement: Over _7, det(A)=2 and A^3=I with A\neqI forces A to be either the scalar 4I or conjugate to diag(1,2). These two conjugacy types have sizes 1 and 56 respectively.

invariant(s).name: self_duality_of_adjoint
invariant(s).statement: V^*  V as G-modules via the nondegenerate Killing form, so dimensions of invariant subspaces in Sym^d(V^*) and Sym^d(V) agree.

invariant(s).name: chevalley_restriction_for_adjoint
invariant(s).statement: The invariant ring (Sym(V^*))^G is isomorphic (as a graded algebra) to (Sym(h^*))^W where h is a Cartan subalgebra and W is the Weyl group of G.

invariant(s).name: generator_degrees_for_sl4
invariant(s).statement: For sl_4 (type A_3), (Sym(V^*))^G is a polynomial algebra on three homogeneous generators of degrees 2,3,4, corresponding to the elementary symmetric polynomials e2,e3,e4 in eigenvalues under the trace-zero constraint.

invariant(s).name: graded_dimension_as_monomial_count
invariant(s).statement: If (Sym(V^*))^G  C[f2,f3,f4] with deg(f2)=2, deg(f3)=3, deg(f4)=4, then dim (Sym^d(V^*))^G = #{(a,b,c) in Z_{>=0}^3 : 2a+3b+4c=d}.

invariant(s).name: bridge_interface_invariant
invariant(s).statement: The composite is coupled by the interface variable T: component A uniquely determines T, and component B is defined/solved only after substituting this specific T into its parameter slot.

essential_assumption(s): Matrices are counted in GL2(F7) (det nonzero).
The ring is truncated at degree 3 (^3=0), so quadratic corrections do not create higher-degree terms.
char(_7)=7 does not divide 3, so the C3-actions are semisimple.
The "order 3 constraint excludes A=I.
Work over C (characteristic 0), so Chevalley restriction applies and invariant rings behave as expected.
The adjoint action of SL_4 on sl_4 is the standard conjugation action on traceless matrices.
Grading is by total polynomial degree, and dim_C of each graded piece is finite.
The bridge quantity T computed from component A is guaranteed to be an integer in the admissible range required by component B (e.g., as an index, bound, or group-action parameter).
uniqueness_certificate(s): Eigenvalue and determinant constraints uniquely determine the two conjugacy types and thus T.
Given T, the Diophantine count U is unique.

The count is uniquely determined by: (i) classifying/counting A\inGL2(_7) with det=2 and order 3 via orbit-stabilizer, and (ii) for each conjugacy type of A, computing dim ker(I+T+T^2) on Hom(V,W) from eigenvalues. Summing the uniquely determined contributions yields a unique ||, hence a unique residue mod 1000.

The graded algebra (Sym(V^*))^G is uniquely determined up to isomorphism by Chevalley restriction and the Lie type A_3, yielding a polynomial ring with generator degrees 2,3,4. Therefore the degree-d dimension is the unique coefficient in the Hilbert series 1/((1-t^2)(1-t^3)(1-t^4)), equivalently a unique finite count of solutions to 2a+3b+4c=d. Hence dim_C(W) is uniquely determined for each d.
(N1 necessity) If T is not computed from component A, component B has multiple admissible outputs across different possible parameter choices; thus the composite is ill-posed without the bridge.
(N2 non-factorization) The final answer is not a trivial Combine(ansA, ansB): the computed T restricts/changes the admissible branch structure inside component B before its output is even defined.

title: Palindromic quartic square-product constraint count -> fourth-difference invariant for root set {0,1,2,T}

identity.short_name: Palindromic quartic square-product constraint count -> fourth-difference invariant for root set {0,1,2,T}

problem_spec.summary: Composite (CHAIN) family: compute bridge quantity T from component A (Count palindromic quartics with \sum_{i<j} r_i^2 r_j^2 = 9 in a bounded coefficient box); then use that T as a required parameter in component B (Pairwise difference fourth powers from quartic roots) to determine the final output (reduced mod 1000). Instance summary: Use Newton identities to express a palindromic quartic's squared-root pairwise product sum as b^2-2a^2+2, count bounded integer solutions giving S=9 to obtain T, then compute Σ_{i<j}(u_i-u_j)^4 for roots {0,1,2,T} via power-sum identity, reduced mod 1000.
givens: S_{a,b}=Σ_{i<j} r_i^2 r_j^2 and constraint S_{a,b}=9 defines admissible pairs.
T counts admissible (a,b). P_T(x)=x(x-1)(x-2)(x-T).

invariant(s).name: square_product_sum_formula
invariant(s).statement: For palindromic quartic, Σ_{i<j} r_i^2 r_j^2 = b^2-2a^2+2.

invariant(s).name: fourth_difference_identity

invariant(s).name: newton_closed_form_for_pairwise_squared_products
invariant(s).statement: For any monic quartic x^4+a1 x^3+a2 x^2+a3 x+a4 with roots r_i, \sum_{i<j} r_i^2 r_j^2 = a2^2 - 2 a1 a3 + 2 a4.

invariant(s).name: palindromic_specialization_formula
invariant(s).statement: For P_{a,b}(x)=x^4+ax^3+bx^2+ax+1, the closed form becomes S(a,b)=b^2-2a^2+2.

invariant(s).name: bounded_integer_count_reduction
invariant(s).statement: With |a|,|b|\leqN, solving S(a,b)=target reduces to a finite search over a-values and checking whether a derived integer expression is a perfect square within the b-bound.

invariant(s).name: vieta_map
invariant(s).statement: For P(x)=x^4 - e1 x^3 + e2 x^2 - e3 x + e4 with roots r_i, e1 = sum r_i, e2 = sum_{i<j} r_i r_j, e3 = sum_{i<j<k} r_i r_j r_k, e4 = product r_i.

invariant(s).name: newton_degree4
invariant(s).statement: The power sums p_k = sum_i r_i^k satisfy: p1=e1, p2=e1 p1 - 2e2, p3=e1 p2 - e2 p1 + 3e3, p4=e1 p3 - e2 p2 + e3 p1 - 4e4.

invariant(s).name: pairwise_difference_fourth_power_identity
invariant(s).statement: T = sum_{i<j}(r_i - r_j)^4 equals 4p4 - 4p1 p3 + 3p2^2.

invariant(s).name: bridge_interface_invariant

essential_assumption(s): All computations are over C for roots, but final symmetric invariants are integers.
Polynomial is monic and roots counted with multiplicity (Newton identities apply).
Constant term is 1 so 0 is not a root (though not required for this particular S-expression).
The coefficient box is finite so the counting task is finite.
Polynomial is monic quartic (degree exactly 4) so Vieta/Newton formulas apply directly.
Roots may be complex; symmetric polynomials in roots are still well-defined and equal to coefficient expressions.
Modulo operation is applied to the final integer value of T (after simplification).
The bridge quantity T computed from component A is guaranteed to be an integer in the admissible range required by component B (e.g., as an index, bound, or group-action parameter).
uniqueness_certificate(s): The bounded Pell-type equation has finitely many solutions; thus T is unique.
Given T, the roots of P_T are fixed, hence U is unique.

Newton identities uniquely determine the symmetric quantity S from the coefficients, giving S=a2^2-2a1a3+2a4. Within the palindromic subfamily this becomes S=b^2-2a^2+2, so S=9 is equivalent to b^2=2a^2+7. The bounded box yields finitely many (a,b) to test, producing a unique count.

T is symmetric in the roots and is therefore a polynomial in the elementary symmetric sums (e1,e2,e3,e4), which are uniquely determined by the coefficients of P. Newton identities compute the required power sums uniquely from (e1,e2,e3,e4), so T (and T mod m) is uniquely determined for each instance.
(N1 necessity) If T is not computed from component A, component B has multiple admissible outputs across different possible parameter choices; thus the composite is ill-posed without the bridge.
(N2 non-factorization) The final answer is not a trivial Combine(ansA, ansB): the computed T restricts/changes the admissible branch structure inside component B before its output is even defined.

title: Quadratic-probe resultant -> discriminant of a T-dependent factored polynomial

Instance summary: Compute a bridge coefficient T as Res(p,x^2+1) via evaluation at i, then compute Disc(F) for a linearquadraticquadratic product using the discriminant product rule and resultants, reduced mod 1000.
ask: Compute N=Disc(F) mod 1000.

invariant(s).name: engineered_remainder_identity
invariant(s).statement: Since E(x)=xP(x)+Q(x), we have E(x)  Q(x) (mod P(x)), hence for every root r of P, E(r)=Q(r).

invariant(s).name: resultant_equals_product_over_roots
invariant(s).statement: For monic P, Res(P,Q)=_{P(r)=0} Q(r) (counted with multiplicity), so the target product equals a resultant.

invariant(s).name: integer_valued_symmetry
invariant(s).statement: Res(P,Q) is an integer polynomial in the coefficients of P and Q; therefore the product is an integer.

invariant(s).name: discriminant_as_resultant
invariant(s).statement: Disc(P) = (-1)^{n(n-1)/2} * Res(P, P') / a_n for deg(P)=n and leading coefficient a_n.

invariant(s).name: iterated_three_factor_rule
invariant(s).statement: For monic A,B,C, Disc(ABC)=Disc(A)Disc(B)Disc(C)Res(A,B)^2Res(A,C)^2Res(B,C)^2.

invariant(s).name: resultant_linear_evaluation
invariant(s).statement: For Q in Z[x], Res(x-r,Q)=Q(r).

invariant(s).name: quadratic_discriminant_formula
invariant(s).statement: For monic quadratic x^2+bx+c, Disc = b^2 - 4c.

invariant(s).name: bridge_interface_invariant
invariant(s).statement: The composite is coupled by the interface variable T: component A uniquely determines T, and component B is defined/solved only after substituting this specific T into its parameter slot.

essential_assumption(s): Compute over C for the probe; final resultant/discriminant are integers.
Fundamental Theorem of Algebra: P has exactly three complex roots counted with multiplicity.
Work in characteristic 0 so resultants and division algorithm behave as usual.
The product over roots uses multiplicity (standard convention), matching the resultant identity.
The discriminant is taken in the standard convention via resultant with the derivative.
Modular output uses the canonical residue in {0,1,...,m-1}.
Computations are in Z[x] and Q[x], using UFD properties as needed.
The bridge quantity T computed from component A is guaranteed to be an integer in the admissible range required by component B (e.g., as an index, bound, or group-action parameter).
uniqueness_certificate(s): Resultant evaluation gives a unique integer T.
Given T, Disc(F) computed from fixed factors is unique; mod_1000 normalization is deterministic.

Because E(x)=xP(x)+Q(x), the product  E(r_i) equals  Q(r_i)=Res(P,Q). The resultant is uniquely determined by the coefficients of P and Q, hence the integer product is unique. Reducing modulo m gives a unique residue in {0,...,m-1}.

The discriminant is a deterministic integer-valued function of polynomial coefficients, and the factorization/product identities are equalities; therefore Disc(P) mod m is uniquely determined for fixed P and m.
(N1 necessity) If T is not computed from component A, component B has multiple admissible outputs across different possible parameter choices; thus the composite is ill-posed without the bridge.
(N2 non-factorization) The final answer is not a trivial Combine(ansA, ansB): the computed T restricts/changes the admissible branch structure inside component B before its output is even defined.

title: Quartic pairwise fourth-difference invariant -> Hilbert coefficient count

identity.short_name: Quartic pairwise fourth-difference invariant -> Hilbert coefficient count

subdomain(s): Chevalley restriction and Weyl group invariants
graded dimension / Hilbert series coefficient extraction

problem_spec.summary: Composite (CHAIN) family: compute bridge quantity T from component A (Pairwise difference fourth powers from quartic roots); then use that T as a required parameter in component B (SL4 adjoint invariant polynomials (degree 50)) to determine the final output (reduced mod 1000). Instance summary: Compute T=Σ_{i<j}(r_i-r_j)^4 from quartic roots using power-sum identities, then use T as a degree parameter in a Hilbert-series monomial count for generators of degrees 2,3,4, reduced mod 1000.
givens: p(x)=x^4-6x^3+11x^2-6x with roots r_1..r_4.
Invariant ring generators have degrees 2,3,4.
ask: Compute U = #{(i,j,k)>=0:2i+3j+4k=T} mod 1000.

parameter(s).name: quartic_coeffs
parameter(s).purpose: determines T uniquely
