invariant(s).name: hilbert_monomial_count
invariant(s).statement: Degree-T dimension equals #{(i,j,k):2i+3j+4k=T}.

invariant(s).name: vieta_map
invariant(s).statement: For P(x)=x^4 - e1 x^3 + e2 x^2 - e3 x + e4 with roots r_i, e1 = sum r_i, e2 = sum_{i<j} r_i r_j, e3 = sum_{i<j<k} r_i r_j r_k, e4 = product r_i.

invariant(s).name: newton_degree4
invariant(s).statement: The power sums p_k = sum_i r_i^k satisfy: p1=e1, p2=e1 p1 - 2e2, p3=e1 p2 - e2 p1 + 3e3, p4=e1 p3 - e2 p2 + e3 p1 - 4e4.

invariant(s).name: pairwise_difference_fourth_power_identity
invariant(s).statement: T = sum_{i<j}(r_i - r_j)^4 equals 4p4 - 4p1 p3 + 3p2^2.

invariant(s).name: self_duality_of_adjoint
invariant(s).statement: V^* ≅ V as G-modules via the nondegenerate Killing form, so dimensions of invariant subspaces in Sym^d(V^*) and Sym^d(V) agree.

invariant(s).name: chevalley_restriction_for_adjoint
invariant(s).statement: The invariant ring (Sym(V^*))^G is isomorphic (as a graded algebra) to (Sym(h^*))^W where h is a Cartan subalgebra and W is the Weyl group of G.

invariant(s).name: generator_degrees_for_sl4
invariant(s).statement: For sl_4 (type A_3), (Sym(V^*))^G is a polynomial algebra on three homogeneous generators of degrees 2,3,4, corresponding to the elementary symmetric polynomials e2,e3,e4 in eigenvalues under the trace-zero constraint.

invariant(s).name: graded_dimension_as_monomial_count
invariant(s).statement: If (Sym(V^*))^G ≅ C[f2,f3,f4] with deg(f2)=2, deg(f3)=3, deg(f4)=4, then dim (Sym^d(V^*))^G = #{(a,b,c) in Z_{>=0}^3 : 2a+3b+4c=d}.

invariant(s).name: bridge_interface_invariant
invariant(s).statement: The composite is coupled by the interface variable T: component A uniquely determines T, and component B is defined/solved only after substituting this specific T into its parameter slot.

essential_assumption(s): Weighted degree uses generator degrees exactly 2,3,4.
Polynomial is monic quartic (degree exactly 4) so Vieta/Newton formulas apply directly.
Roots may be complex; symmetric polynomials in roots are still well-defined and equal to coefficient expressions.
Modulo operation is applied to the final integer value of T (after simplification).
Work over C (characteristic 0), so Chevalley restriction applies and invariant rings behave as expected.
The adjoint action of SL_4 on sl_4 is the standard conjugation action on traceless matrices.
Grading is by total polynomial degree, and dim_C of each graded piece is finite.
The bridge quantity T computed from component A is guaranteed to be an integer in the admissible range required by component B (e.g., as an index, bound, or group-action parameter).
uniqueness_certificate(s): Power sums are uniquely determined by coefficients; hence T is unique.
Given T, the Diophantine count U is unique.

T is symmetric in the roots and is therefore a polynomial in the elementary symmetric sums (e1,e2,e3,e4), which are uniquely determined by the coefficients of P. Newton identities compute the required power sums uniquely from (e1,e2,e3,e4), so T (and T mod m) is uniquely determined for each instance.

The graded algebra (Sym(V^*))^G is uniquely determined up to isomorphism by Chevalley restriction and the Lie type A_3, yielding a polynomial ring with generator degrees 2,3,4. Therefore the degree-d dimension is the unique coefficient in the Hilbert series 1/((1-t^2)(1-t^3)(1-t^4)), equivalently a unique finite count of solutions to 2a+3b+4c=d. Hence dim_C(W) is uniquely determined for each d.
(N1 necessity) If T is not computed from component A, component B has multiple admissible outputs across different possible parameter choices; thus the composite is ill-posed without the bridge.
(N2 non-factorization) The final answer is not a trivial Combine(ansA, ansB): the computed T restricts/changes the admissible branch structure inside component B before its output is even defined.

title: Reciprocal quartic triple-square sum -> cubic Newton recurrence partial sum

identity.short_name: Reciprocal quartic triple-square sum -> cubic Newton recurrence partial sum

problem_spec.summary: Composite (CHAIN) family: compute bridge quantity T from component A (Triple products of squared roots via reciprocal Newton sums (quartic)); then use that T as a required parameter in component B (Partial sum of cubic-root power sums modulo 1000) to determine the final output (reduced mod 1000). Instance summary: Compute a reciprocal-quartic symmetric triple-product-of-squares invariant T, then use T as the endpoint in a Newton/Vieta-derived recurrence for cubic-root power sums; output the partial sum mod 1000.
givens: P(x)=x^4-2x^3+x^2-2x+1 is monic; r_1,...,r_4 are its roots.
alpha,beta,gamma are the roots of y^3-3y+1=0; S_n=alpha^n+beta^n+gamma^n.
ask: Compute T=\sum_{i<j<k} r_i^2 r_j^2 r_k^2.
Compute N=\left(\sum_{n=0}^{T} S_n\right) mod 1000.

parameter(s).name: quartic_coeffs
parameter(s).purpose: pins the reciprocal quartic instance

parameter(s).name: cubic_coeffs
parameter(s).purpose: pins the cubic power-sum recurrence instance

parameter(s).name: degree
parameter(s).purpose: fixes Newton-identity depth and the symmetric-expression arity (triples of 4 roots).

parameter(s).name: coefficients
parameter(s).domain: (a1,a2,a3,a4) \in ℤ^4 for a monic quartic P(x)=x^4+a1 x^3+a2 x^2+a3 x+a4, with a4\neq0
parameter(s).purpose: controls the root symmetric sums; nonzero constant term enables reciprocal-root methods.

parameter(s).name: target_expression
parameter(s).purpose: selects the triple-product-of-squares symmetric polynomial.

parameter(s).name: cubic_coefficients
parameter(s).domain: monic cubic x^3 - s1 x^2 + s2 x - s3 with integer coefficients
parameter(s).purpose: determines the symmetric sums and the induced power-sum recurrence

parameter(s).name: N
parameter(s).purpose: partial-sum cutoff index (here N=20)

parameter(s).name: m
parameter(s).purpose: modulus for the final reduction (here m=1000)

parameter(s).name: output_statistic
parameter(s).purpose: output is T=\sum_{n=0}^N S_n rather than a single S_N

parameter(s).name: T_bridge
parameter(s).domain: derived integer computed from component A invariants
parameter(s).purpose: interface quantity produced by component A and consumed by component B (non-stapled coupling).

invariant(s).name: reciprocal_root_multiset
invariant(s).statement: Since P is monic with constant term 1, if r is a root then 1/r is a root (with multiplicity).

invariant(s).name: triple_square_to_reciprocal_square
invariant(s).statement: \sum_{i<j<k} r_i^2 r_j^2 r_k^2=(\prod r_i)^2 \sum_i 1/r_i^2.

invariant(s).name: cubic_power_sum_recurrence
invariant(s).statement: From y^3=3y-1, the power sums satisfy S_n=3S_{n-2}-S_{n-3} for n>=3.

invariant(s).name: complementary_product_identity
invariant(s).statement: For roots r1..r4 with product e4=a4, we have \sum_{i<j<k} r_i^2 r_j^2 r_k^2 = e4^2 \sum_{i=1}^4 1/r_i^2.

invariant(s).name: reciprocal_polynomial_roots
invariant(s).statement: If a4\neq0, then s_i=1/r_i are roots of the monic reciprocal polynomial Q(x) = (1/a4) x^4 P(1/x) = x^4 + (a3/a4)x^3 + (a2/a4)x^2 + (a1/a4)x + (1/a4).

invariant(s).name: newton_girard_for_p2
invariant(s).statement: For monic Q(x)=x^4+A1 x^3+A2 x^2+A3 x+A4 with roots s_i and p_k=\sum s_i^k, Newton gives p1=-A1 and p2 = A1^2 - 2A2, uniquely determined by (A1,A2).

invariant(s).name: coefficient_only_closed_form
invariant(s).statement: Combining the reciprocal transform and Newton for p2 yields T = a4^2Â \cdot p2 = a4^2Â \cdot ((a3/a4)^2 - 2(a2/a4)) = a3^2 - 2 a2 a4, so T is a coefficient polynomial independent of a1.

invariant(s).name: root_polynomial_relation
invariant(s).statement: Each root r satisfies r^3 = 3r - 1.

invariant(s).name: induced_linear_recurrence_for_power_sums
invariant(s).statement: For n\geq3, S_n = 3S_{n-2} - S_{n-3}.

invariant(s).name: partial_sum_determinism
invariant(s).statement: Once S_0,...,S_N are determined, T=\sum_{n=0}^N S_n is uniquely determined.

invariant(s).name: bridge_interface_invariant
invariant(s).statement: The composite is coupled by the interface variable T: component A uniquely determines T, and component B is defined/solved only after substituting this specific T into its parameter slot.

essential_assumption(s): All sums are over roots with multiplicity; power sums are well-defined in C.
Constant term a4 \neq 0 so no root is 0 and reciprocals 1/r_i exist.
Roots are counted with multiplicity so symmetric identities apply directly.
Polynomial is monic so Newton-Girard identities use the standard monic form.
The polynomial is monic of degree 3, so every root satisfies the same cubic relation, yielding a linear recurrence for S_n.
Roots are counted with multiplicity (standard for Vieta/Newton sums).
Modular reduction is applied to integer values obtained from the recurrence.
The bridge quantity T computed from component A is guaranteed to be an integer in the admissible range required by component B (e.g., as an index, bound, or group-action parameter).
uniqueness_certificate(s): T is determined uniquely by the quartic coefficients via symmetric polynomials.
Given T, the recurrence/Vieta initial conditions determine the partial sum uniquely; the mod 1000 normalization is deterministic.

The target T is symmetric in the multiset of roots, hence determined uniquely by coefficients. The complementary-product identity reduces T to e4^2 times the power sum p2 of reciprocal roots. Newton-Girard identities compute p2 uniquely from the first two coefficients of the reciprocal polynomial, so T is uniquely determined (equivalently by the closed form T=a3^2-2a2a4).
The recurrence S_n = 3S_{n-2} - S_{n-3} together with initial values S_0=3, S_1=0, S_2=6 uniquely determines S_0,...,S_20, hence uniquely determines T and T mod 1000.
(N1 necessity) If T is not computed from component A, component B has multiple admissible outputs across different possible parameter choices; thus the composite is ill-posed without the bridge.
(N2 non-factorization) The final answer is not a trivial Combine(ansA, ansB): the computed T restricts/changes the admissible branch structure inside component B before its output is even defined.

Symmetric expressions in roots are determined by coefficients; invert-roots via reciprocal polynomials to access reciprocal power sums.
Linear dependence of powers of a root modulo its defining polynomial induces a recurrence for symmetric power sums.

title: Transaction-determined parameter -> discriminant via factor/resultant chain

identity.short_name: Transaction-determined parameter -> discriminant via factor/resultant chain

subdomain(s): integer equations from transactional balance constraints

problem_spec.summary: Composite (CHAIN) family: compute bridge quantity T from component A (Find red-apple price with Store 3 buying exactly one red (square-first)); then use that T as a required parameter in component B (Discriminant mod 1000 via linear+quadratic factor chain) to determine the final output (reduced mod 1000). Instance summary: Solve a bulk-discount linear pricing system to determine T, then compute the discriminant of a linearquadraticquadratic factorization using product-rule discriminants and resultants, reduced mod 1000.
givens: Pricing equations: 2r+g=17 and r+4g=19.
Store 3 check: (r+5g) - (5^2-5) = 2.
Define T=r and F(x)=(x-T)(x^2+Tx+1)(x^2-x+1).
ask: Compute N = Disc(F) mod 1000.

parameter(s).purpose: interface quantity produced by component A and consumed by component B (non-stapled coupling).

invariant(s).name: linear_price_uniqueness
invariant(s).statement: Two independent linear equations uniquely determine (r,g) over Z.

invariant(s).name: discriminant_product_formula
invariant(s).statement: Disc(AB)=Disc(A)Disc(B)Res(A,B)^2 for coprime A,B.

invariant(s).name: transaction_balance_conservation
invariant(s).statement: Each stores remaining balance equals initial balance plus credits from returns minus costs of purchases under that stores applicable pricing rule.

invariant(s).name: discounted_red_unit_price
invariant(s).statement: In Stores 1 and 2, if the final red purchase buys at least 3 reds then per-red cost is R-1; otherwise per-red cost is R.

invariant(s).name: square_first_cost_formula
invariant(s).statement: In Store 3, a purchase transaction with per-item price p and quantity q1 costs p^2+(q-1)p (with discounted p if bulk discount applies); a single-item return credits p^2.

invariant(s).name: fixed_red_quantity_equation_store3
invariant(s).statement: With n3=1, Store 3s final red purchase costs R^2 and must equal Store 3s remaining balance after its prior transactions.

invariant(s).name: discriminant_as_resultant
invariant(s).statement: Disc(P) = (-1)^{n(n-1)/2} * Res(P, P') / a_n for deg(P)=n and leading coefficient a_n.

invariant(s).name: discriminant_product_rule
invariant(s).statement: For monic polynomials A,B in Z[x], Disc(AB)=Disc(A)Disc(B)Res(A,B)^2.

invariant(s).name: iterated_three_factor_rule
invariant(s).statement: For monic A,B,C, Disc(ABC)=Disc(A)Disc(B)Disc(C)Res(A,B)^2Res(A,C)^2Res(B,C)^2.

invariant(s).name: resultant_linear_evaluation
invariant(s).statement: For Q in Z[x], Res(x-r,Q)=Q(r).

invariant(s).name: quadratic_discriminant_formula
invariant(s).statement: For monic quadratic x^2+bx+c, Disc = b^2 - 4c.

invariant(s).name: bridge_interface_invariant
invariant(s).statement: The composite is coupled by the interface variable T: component A uniquely determines T, and component B is defined/solved only after substituting this specific T into its parameter slot.

essential_assumption(s): All prices are integers; the stated equations are consistent.
Listed prices are integers and transactions are executed exactly as described.
In Stores 1 and 2, the final red purchase is a single transaction that spends the entire remaining balance.
In Store 3, the final red purchase buys exactly n3 reds and spends the entire remaining balance.
The discriminant is taken in the standard convention via resultant with the derivative.
Modular output uses the canonical residue in {0,1,...,m-1}.
Computations are in Z[x] and Q[x], using UFD properties as needed.
The bridge quantity T computed from component A is guaranteed to be an integer in the admissible range required by component B (e.g., as an index, bound, or group-action parameter).
uniqueness_certificate(s): The system for (r,g) has a unique solution, so T is unique.
With fixed T, discriminant via factor/resultant identities yields a unique integer, then mod_1000 normalization is deterministic.
Stores 1 and 2 restrict R to a small finite set via case splits (n=1,2 vs n3) and divisibility constraints.
Store 3, with square-first pricing and a fixed final red quantity, yields a single equation in R that selects a unique value from the intersection.

The discriminant is a deterministic integer-valued function of polynomial coefficients, and the factorization/product identities are equalities; therefore Disc(P) mod m is uniquely determined for fixed P and m.
(N1 necessity) If T is not computed from component A, component B has multiple admissible outputs across different possible parameter choices; thus the composite is ill-posed without the bridge.
(N2 non-factorization) The final answer is not a trivial Combine(ansA, ansB): the computed T restricts/changes the admissible branch structure inside component B before its output is even defined.

title: Truncated ring automorphisms -> Burnside orbit count on square-zero elements

identity.short_name: Truncated ring automorphisms -> Burnside orbit count on square-zero elements

subdomain(s): composition of truncated power series over finite fields
ideal-lattice group actions and orbit counting (Burnside)

problem_spec.summary: Composite (CHAIN) family: compute bridge quantity T from component A (Count order-2 automorphisms of F7[x]/(x^6)); then use that T as a required parameter in component B (Count Aut(R)-orbits of ideals with dim_F2(I/2I)=2 in Z4[x]/(x^3,2x)) to determine the final output (reduced mod 1000). Instance summary: Model automorphisms of F7[x]/(x^3) via truncated series x->ax+bx^2, identify the induced square-scaling action on square-zero elements, compute T=|(F7*)^2|, then apply Burnside to count orbits mod 1000.
givens: R=F_7[x]/(x^3). Automorphisms satisfy phi(x)=ax+bx^2 with a in F_7^*, b in F_7.
ask: Compute N = (# Aut(R)-orbits of S) mod 1000.

parameter(s).domain: derived integer computed from component A invariants
parameter(s).purpose: interface quantity produced by component A and consumed by component B (non-stapled coupling).

invariant(s).name: square_zero_characterization
invariant(s).statement: u^2=0 in F7[x]/(x^3) iff the x-coefficient of u vanishes.

invariant(s).name: induced_scaling_by_square
invariant(s).statement: phi(bx^2)= (a^2 b)x^2, so the induced action on S is scaling by squares a^2.

invariant(s).name: automorphism_parameterization
invariant(s).statement: is an F_p-algebra automorphism iff (x)=a x + higher terms (mod x^n) with aF_p^.

invariant(s).statement: ^k=id is equivalent to f^{k}(x)  x (mod x^n) where f(x)=(x) as a truncated series.

invariant(s).name: leading_coefficient_constraint
invariant(s).statement: From the x-coefficient in f^{k}(x)x, one gets a^k=1 in F_p^ (here a^2=1).

invariant(s).name: triangular_coefficient_system
invariant(s).statement: Expanding f^{2}(x) mod x^n yields a (nearly) triangular system of polynomial equations in b,c,d,e solvable by casework on a.

invariant(s).name: normal_form_in_R
invariant(s).statement: Every element has a unique representative a+bx+cx^2 with aZ/4Z and b,cF2, since x^3=0 and 2x=0.

invariant(s).name: maximal_ideal_and_2_annihilation
invariant(s).statement: The unique maximal ideal is =(2,x) consisting of elements with even constant term, and 2=0. Hence for any proper ideal I⊆, we have 2I=0 so I/2I≅I as F2-vector spaces.

invariant(s).name: ideal_as_T_invariant_subspace
invariant(s).statement: For proper ideals I⊆, the ideal condition is equivalent to I being an F2-subspace of  invariant under multiplication-by-x, i.e. T(I)⊆I where T(y)=xy.

invariant(s).name: automorphisms_determined_by_image_of_x
invariant(s).statement: Any automorphism fixes constants and is determined by xx+x^2+2 with ,F2; thus |Aut(R)|=4. Additionally x^2 is fixed by all automorphisms because (x+x^2+2)^2=x^2.

invariant(s).name: bridge_interface_invariant
invariant(s).statement: The composite is coupled by the interface variable T: component A uniquely determines T, and component B is defined/solved only after substituting this specific T into its parameter slot.

essential_assumption(s): Automorphisms are ring automorphisms fixing F7 pointwise.
Automorphisms are F_7-algebra automorphisms (fix scalars in F_7).
Composition is computed in R, i.e., all series are truncated mod x^6.
Automorphisms preserve 1 and the defining relations x^3=0, 2x=0.
The quotient I/2I is viewed as an F2-vector space via RR/2R.
Proper ideals lie in the maximal ideal  and thus satisfy 2I=0 in this ring.
The bridge quantity T computed from component A is guaranteed to be an integer in the admissible range required by component B (e.g., as an index, bound, or group-action parameter).
uniqueness_certificate(s): The induced action on S is uniquely determined by the coefficient a mod 7; Burnside's lemma yields a unique orbit count.

Each automorphism corresponds to a unique coefficient tuple (a,b,c,d,e); the system f^{2}(x)x (mod x^6) has finitely many solutions in F_7, so |I| is uniquely determined once the coefficient system is solved.

The constrained set  is finite and is completely determined by the invariant-subspace description: dim_F2(I/2I)=2 ⇔ |I|=4 ⇔ I is a 2D T-invariant subspace of  under T(y)=xy. This yields a finite explicit list.

Aut(R) is finite and explicitly parameterized by (,)F2^2. The orbit count is uniquely determined by the action on the explicit list of ideals, equivalently by Burnsides lemma applied to fixed-point counts.
(N1 necessity) If T is not computed from component A, component B has multiple admissible outputs across different possible parameter choices; thus the composite is ill-posed without the bridge.
(N2 non-factorization) The final answer is not a trivial Combine(ansA, ansB): the computed T restricts/changes the admissible branch structure inside component B before its output is even defined.

identity.short_name: Reciprocal-product square filter -> Hilbert-series degree count

problem_spec.summary: Composite (CHAIN) family: compute bridge quantity T from component A (Count non-palindromic bounded quartics with reciprocal product a square); then use that T as a required parameter in component B (SL4 adjoint invariant polynomials (degree 50)) to determine the final output (reduced mod 1000). Instance summary: Impose that a quadratic's reciprocal product is a perfect square (excluding palindromic/anti-palindromic degeneracies) to obtain a count T, then use T to set a Hilbert-series coefficient problem for an invariant ring with degrees 2,3,4; output mod 1000.
givens: Strict non-(anti-)palindromic: x^2P_{a,b}(1/x) !=  P_{a,b}(x).
Invariant ring degrees are 2,3,4; U counts solutions to 2i+3j+4k=10+T.
ask: Compute U mod 1000.

invariant(s).name: reciprocal_product_palindromic
invariant(s).statement: R_{a,b}(x) is palindromic by construction.

invariant(s).name: square_coefficient_constraints
invariant(s).statement: If R_{a,b}=(ux^2+vx+w)^2, then b=u^2=w^2 and coefficient matching forces strong constraints on a.

invariant(s).name: hilbert_series_counting
invariant(s).statement: Dimension in degree d equals #{(i,j,k)>=0:2i+3j+4k=d}.

invariant(s).name: palindromic_implies_square
invariant(s).statement: If P*=P (equivalently c=a), then P \cdot P* = P^2 is a square in Z[x].

invariant(s).name: gcd_with_reciprocal_self_reciprocal
invariant(s).statement: D=gcd(P,P*) satisfies D*=±D; choosing D monic makes D*=D (self-reciprocal).

invariant(s).name: degree_2_gcd_forces_palindromic
invariant(s).statement: If deg gcd(P,P*)=2, then both the gcd and the remaining quadratic factor have constant term 1 and are self-reciprocal, forcing P*=P (palindromic).

invariant(s).name: coprime_product_square_forcing
invariant(s).statement: If gcd(P,P*)=1 and PP* is a square in Z[x], then P is a square in Z[x] up to a unit; monicity forces P=Q^2 with QZ[x].

invariant(s).name: odd_degree_not_square
invariant(s).statement: A nonzero square polynomial in Z[x] has even degree; in particular, no cubic in Z[x] is a square.

invariant(s).name: self_duality_of_adjoint
invariant(s).statement: V^*  V as G-modules via the nondegenerate Killing form, so dimensions of invariant subspaces in Sym^d(V^*) and Sym^d(V) agree.

invariant(s).name: chevalley_restriction_for_adjoint
invariant(s).statement: The invariant ring (Sym(V^*))^G is isomorphic (as a graded algebra) to (Sym(h^*))^W where h is a Cartan subalgebra and W is the Weyl group of G.

invariant(s).name: generator_degrees_for_sl4
invariant(s).statement: For sl_4 (type A_3), (Sym(V^*))^G is a polynomial algebra on three homogeneous generators of degrees 2,3,4, corresponding to the elementary symmetric polynomials e2,e3,e4 in eigenvalues under the trace-zero constraint.

invariant(s).name: graded_dimension_as_monomial_count
invariant(s).statement: If (Sym(V^*))^G  C[f2,f3,f4] with deg(f2)=2, deg(f3)=3, deg(f4)=4, then dim (Sym^d(V^*))^G = #{(a,b,c) in Z_{>=0}^3 : 2a+3b+4c=d}.

problem_spec.summary: Composite (CHAIN) family: compute bridge quantity T from component A (Count bounded cubics with exactly one integer root among {0,1}); then use that T as a required parameter in component B (Partial sum of cubic-root power sums modulo 1000) to determine the final output (reduced mod 1000). Instance summary: Count bounded integer-coefficient cubics having exactly one root in {0,1} to get T, then evaluate the Newton/Vieta power-sum sequence S_n of a fixed cubic at n=T modulo 1000.
givens: -2 <= a,b,c <= 2 and p(x)=x^3+ax^2+bx+c.
Goodness: exactly one of p(0), p(1) equals 0.
alpha,beta,gamma are roots of y^3-3y+1=0; S_n=alpha^n+beta^n+gamma^n.
ask: Compute T = # {good (a,b,c)}.
Compute N = S_T mod 1000.

identity.short_name: Count bounded cubics with exactly one double root and both roots in (-2,2)

subdomain(s): rational root theorem and Vieta relations
topic_path: counting coefficient triples by multiplicity and root-location constraints

problem_spec.summary: Count monic cubic polynomials with coefficients in a finite integer box that have exactly two distinct real roots (a double root and a simple root), and require both distinct roots to lie in a prescribed open interval. Use the repeated-root criterion to force a rational (hence integer) double root, then apply Vieta to pin down the remaining root and count admissible root pairs.
givens: Requirement: P has exactly two distinct real roots, both lying in (-2,2) (so P has one double real root and one simple real root).
ask: Find the number of such polynomials P.

parameter(s).name: coefficient_box
parameter(s).purpose: finite search space for enumeration/counting.

parameter(s).name: target_interval
parameter(s).purpose: required location of the distinct real roots.

parameter(s).name: multiplicity_pattern
parameter(s).purpose: enforces exactly two distinct roots for a cubic.

invariant(s).name: multiplicity_characterization
invariant(s).statement: A real monic cubic has exactly two distinct real roots iff it factors as (x-r)^2(x-s) with r\neqs real, equivalently iff gcd(P,P′) has degree 1 (or discriminant \Delta=0 but P not a perfect cube).

invariant(s).name: double_root_rational_then_integer
invariant(s).statement: The double root r is a root of gcd(P,P′), hence rational; since P is monic with integer coefficients, the Rational Root Theorem forces r to be an integer dividing c.

invariant(s).name: interval_forces_small_root_set
invariant(s).statement: With r integer and r\in(-2,2), we must have r\in{-1,0,1}. Vieta then gives s=-a-2r, so s is also an integer and s\in(-2,2) forces s\in{-1,0,1}.

invariant(s).name: coefficient_reconstruction_from_roots
invariant(s).statement: If P(x)=(x-r)^2(x-s), then (a,b,c)=(-(2r+s), r^2+2rs, -r^2 s). Thus each admissible ordered pair (r,s) yields exactly one coefficient triple.

essential_assumption(s): The interval is open; roots equal to ±2 are excluded.

title: Coefficient lag autocorrelation via Fourier coefficient and cosine moments

identity.short_name: Coefficient lag autocorrelation via Fourier coefficient and cosine moments

problem_spec.summary: Compute a fixed-lag coefficient autocorrelation of a structured polynomial by rewriting the target as a Laurent coefficient of P(x)P(x^{-1}), converting it to a unit-circle Fourier integral via orthogonality, simplifying |P(e^{iθ})|^2 to a polynomial in cos(2θ), and evaluating the resulting integral using even cosine moments (central binomial coefficients).
givens: a_k is the coefficient of x^k in P(x).
C = Σ_{k} a_k a_{k+4}, summing over k such that k+4 \leq deg(P).
ask: Determine the exact integer value of C.

parameter(s).name: exponent
parameter(s).purpose: controls the power on each quadratic factor, hence the degree and sum length

parameter(s).name: linear_coefficient_u
parameter(s).purpose: sets the conjugate-like quadratic pair (x^2+u x+1),(x^2-u x+1) enabling cosine reduction

parameter(s).name: lag_t
parameter(s).purpose: specifies which coefficient correlation Σ a_k a_{k+t} is extracted as a Fourier coefficient

invariant(s).name: correlation_is_fourier_coefficient
invariant(s).statement: If P(x)=Σ a_k x^k, then Σ_k a_k a_{k+t} = [x^t] P(x)P(x^{-1}) = (1/2\pi)∫_0^{2\pi} |P(e^{iθ})|^2 e^{-itθ} dθ. For real coefficients, this equals (1/2\pi)∫ |P(e^{iθ})|^2 cos(tθ) dθ.

essential_assumption(s): P has real coefficients so P(e^{-iθ}) is the complex conjugate of P(e^{iθ}).
Orthogonality of exponentials on [0,2\pi] is used to identify coefficients via integrals.
Even-power cosine moment formula applies: (1/2\pi)∫_0^{2\pi} cos^{2j}(θ) dθ = C(2j,j)/4^j.
uniqueness_certificate(s): The polynomial P is fixed by the givens, so the coefficient sequence (a_k) is unique. The target C is the coefficient [x^t] of the uniquely determined Laurent polynomial P(x)P(x^{-1}), equivalently a unique Fourier coefficient of the uniquely determined function |P(e^{iθ})|^2. Therefore C is uniquely determined.

invariant(s).statement: Disc(P) = (-1)^{n(n-1)/2} * Res(P, P') / a_n for deg(P)=n and leading coefficient a_n.

invariant(s).statement: For monic polynomials A,B in Z[x], Disc(AB)=Disc(A)Disc(B)Res(A,B)^2.

title: Count palindromic quartics with \sum_{i<j} r_i^2 r_j^2 = 9 in a bounded coefficient box

identity.short_name: Count palindromic quartics with \sum_{i<j} r_i^2 r_j^2 = 9 in a bounded coefficient box

problem_spec.summary: Consider the 2-parameter palindromic family P_{a,b}(x)=x^4+ax^3+bx^2+ax+1. Using Newton identities, express S=\sum_{i<j} r_i^2 r_j^2 as a closed-form polynomial in (a,b), then count integer pairs (a,b) in a bounded box for which S equals a prescribed target.
givens: P_{a,b}(x) = x^4 + a x^3 + b x^2 + a x + 1 is a monic quartic (a,b \in ℤ).
r1,r2,r3,r4 are the complex roots of P_{a,b}(x)=0, counted with multiplicity.
Define S(a,b)=\sum_{1\leqi<j\leq4} r_i^2 r_j^2.
Constraint box: |a| \leq 5 and |b| \leq 5.
Target value: S(a,b)=9.
ask: Count the integer pairs (a,b) in the box such that S(a,b)=9.

parameter(s).name: bound_N
parameter(s).purpose: sets the finite coefficient search region for (a,b).

parameter(s).name: palindromic_parameters
parameter(s).purpose: restricts quartics to a 2D palindromic subfamily.

parameter(s).name: target_value
parameter(s).purpose: specifies the desired value of S(a,b) (instance target is 9).

invariant(s).name: newton_closed_form_for_pairwise_squared_products
invariant(s).statement: For any monic quartic x^4+a1 x^3+a2 x^2+a3 x+a4 with roots r_i, \sum_{i<j} r_i^2 r_j^2 = a2^2 - 2 a1 a3 + 2 a4.

invariant(s).name: palindromic_specialization_formula
invariant(s).statement: For P_{a,b}(x)=x^4+ax^3+bx^2+ax+1, the closed form becomes S(a,b)=b^2-2a^2+2.

invariant(s).name: bounded_integer_count_reduction
invariant(s).statement: With |a|,|b|\leqN, solving S(a,b)=target reduces to a finite search over a-values and checking whether a derived integer expression is a perfect square within the b-bound.

essential_assumption(s): Polynomial is monic and roots counted with multiplicity (Newton identities apply).
Constant term is 1 so 0 is not a root (though not required for this particular S-expression).
The coefficient box is finite so the counting task is finite.

uniqueness_certificate(s).statement: Newton identities uniquely determine the symmetric quantity S from the coefficients, giving S=a2^2-2a1a3+2a4. Within the palindromic subfamily this becomes S=b^2-2a^2+2, so S=9 is equivalent to b^2=2a^2+7. The bounded box yields finitely many (a,b) to test, producing a unique count.

technique(s).name: rewrite_target_via_power_sums
technique(s).purpose: express S=\sum_{i<j} r_i^2 r_j^2 as (p2^2-p4)/2.
outputs: S in terms of p2 and p4

technique(s).name: apply_newtons_identities_degree4
technique(s).purpose: compute p2 and p4 in terms of coefficients and simplify to a closed form.

technique(s).name: specialize_to_palindromic_family
technique(s).purpose: reduce S to a quadratic form in (a,b).

technique(s).name: bounded_diophantine_casework
technique(s).purpose: enumerate feasible a-values and count b-solutions by square-checking.

theorem(s).name: Newton-Girard identities
theorem(s).role: determines power sums from coefficients for monic polynomials.

theorem(s).name: Vieta's formulas
theorem(s).role: underlie the coefficient-symmetric-sum correspondence and justify coefficient specialization.

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: Symmetric polynomials in roots are determined by coefficients; reduce a root-expression to a coefficient Diophantine condition.

critical_trick(s): Derive the coefficient-only closed form S=a2^2-2a1a3+2a4, then specialize to the palindromic family to get S=b^2-2a^2+2.
Convert the target S=9 into b^2=2a^2+7 and count solutions inside a finite box by square checking.

library(s).name: sympy
library(s).reason: symbolic confirmation of the closed form and optional exact checking.

library(s).name: itertools
library(s).reason: brute-force enumeration of all (a,b) in the box for verification.

task(s).name: brute_force_verify_count
task(s).reason: iterate (a,b) in [-N,N]^2, compute S=b^2-2a^2+2, count those equal to target.

task(s).name: symbolic_identity_check
task(s).reason: verify S=a2^2-2a1a3+2a4 from Newton identities for a generic quartic.

title: Engineered remainder \to quadratic probe resultant product

identity.short_name: Engineered remainder \to quadratic probe resultant product

problem_spec.summary: Given a monic cubic P with integer coefficients and roots r_i, define an engineered expression E(x)=xP(x)+Q(x) where Q is a fixed quadratic probe (often x^2+1). Then E(r_i)=Q(r_i) for all roots. The desired product over roots equals Res(P,Q), which can be computed by evaluating P at the roots of Q (a conjugate pair), then reducing modulo 1000.
givens: P(x) is a monic cubic in ℤ[x] with complex roots r_1,r_2,r_3 counted with multiplicity.
Q(x) is a fixed quadratic probe in ℤ[x] (in this instance Q(x)=x^2+1).
E(x) is defined by E(x)=x \cdot P(x)+Q(x) (so deg(E)=4 and E\equivQ (mod P)).
Output is taken modulo m=1000.
ask: Compute T \equiv _{i=1}^3 E(r_i) (mod 1000).

parameter(s).name: cubic_coefficients
parameter(s).domain: monic cubic x^3 + a x^2 + b x + c with a,b,c \in ℤ
parameter(s).purpose: defines the root constraint ring ℤ[x]/(P) and the multiset of roots r_i

parameter(s).name: quadratic_probe
parameter(s).domain: integer-coefficient quadratic Q(x) with deg(Q)=2 (often chosen to have conjugate roots)
parameter(s).purpose: the reduced expression whose values are multiplied over the roots of P

parameter(s).name: engineered_expression_form
parameter(s).purpose: forces the remainder of E modulo P to be exactly Q, making reduction immediate and structural

parameter(s).name: modulus
parameter(s).purpose: final normalization by modular reduction

invariant(s).name: engineered_remainder_identity
invariant(s).statement: Since E(x)=xP(x)+Q(x), we have E(x) \equiv Q(x) (mod P(x)), hence for every root r of P, E(r)=Q(r).

invariant(s).name: resultant_equals_product_over_roots
invariant(s).statement: For monic P, Res(P,Q)=_{P(r)=0} Q(r) (counted with multiplicity), so the target product equals a resultant.

invariant(s).name: integer_valued_symmetry
invariant(s).statement: Res(P,Q) is an integer polynomial in the coefficients of P and Q; therefore the product is an integer.

essential_assumption(s): Fundamental Theorem of Algebra: P has exactly three complex roots counted with multiplicity.
Work in characteristic 0 so resultants and division algorithm behave as usual.
The product over roots uses multiplicity (standard convention), matching the resultant identity.
uniqueness_certificate(s): Because E(x)=xP(x)+Q(x), the product  E(r_i) equals  Q(r_i)=Res(P,Q). The resultant is uniquely determined by the coefficients of P and Q, hence the integer product is unique. Reducing modulo m gives a unique residue in {0,...,m-1}.

technique(s).name: engineered_remainder_reduction
technique(s).purpose: reduce E(r) to Q(r) instantly for all roots r of P

technique(s).name: resultant_swap_to_probe_roots
inputs: quadratic probe Q with roots α,β and leading coefficient a_Q
technique(s).purpose: compute  Q(r_i) as Res(P,Q)=a_Q^3 \cdot P(α)P(β)

technique(s).name: conjugate_norm_elimination
technique(s).purpose: multiply conjugates to remove radicals/imaginary parts and obtain an integer

technique(s).name: modular_postprocessing
technique(s).purpose: report the residue class modulo m

theorem(s).name: Polynomial Division Algorithm
theorem(s).role: guarantees remainder congruence and evaluation compatibility modulo P at roots

theorem(s).name: Resultant identity
theorem(s).role: Res(P,Q)=a_P^{deg Q} Q(r_i)=a_Q^{deg P} P(roots of Q)

theorem(s).name: Conjugate product (norm)
theorem(s).role: (u+vi)(u-vi)=u^2+v^2 (and similarly for quadratic radicals), ensuring integrality of the product

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: symmetry_of_root_expressions_via_resultants

critical_trick(s): Recognize/construct E(x) so that E(r)=Q(r) for P(r)=0 (engineered remainder).
Convert the product over cubic roots into evaluation of P at the two quadratic-probe roots via a resultant.
Use conjugate multiplication to eliminate i (or \sqrt\Delta) and obtain an integer, then reduce mod 1000.

library(s).name: sympy
library(s).reason: exact polynomial remainder/resultant computations for verification and regression testing

task(s).name: compute_resultant_directly
task(s).reason: verify T_full = resultant(P,E) = resultant(P,Q) exactly in ℤ

task(s).name: verify_engineered_remainder
task(s).reason: confirm E - xP - Q is identically zero and hence E\equivQ (mod P)

task(s).name: reduce_mod_m
task(s).reason: compute T_full mod 1000 reliably with exact arithmetic

title: domain: [sum_{i<j<k} r_i^2 r_j^2 r_k^2]

identity.short_name: Triple products of squared roots via reciprocal Newton sums (quartic)

problem_spec.summary: Given a monic quartic polynomial with roots r1,...,r4, compute the symmetric sum of triple products of squared roots: \sum_{i<j<k} r_i^2 r_j^2 r_k^2. Reduce it to a power sum of reciprocal roots using complementary products and then compute that power sum via Newton identities on the reciprocal polynomial.
givens: P(x) = x^4 - 2x^3 + x^2 + 3x + 2 is monic of degree 4.
r1,r2,r3,r4 are the complex roots of P(x), counted with multiplicity.
ask: Compute T = \sum_{1 \leq i < j < k \leq 4} r_i^2 r_j^2 r_k^2 as an integer.

parameter(s).name: degree
parameter(s).purpose: fixes Newton-identity depth and the symmetric-expression arity (triples of 4 roots).

parameter(s).name: coefficients
parameter(s).domain: (a1,a2,a3,a4) \in ℤ^4 for a monic quartic P(x)=x^4+a1 x^3+a2 x^2+a3 x+a4, with a4\neq0
parameter(s).purpose: controls the root symmetric sums; nonzero constant term enables reciprocal-root methods.

title: optional:

identity.short_name: Count F13 degree-8 twisted-reciprocal polynomials with exactly two inversion 2-cycles as roots

subdomain(s): affine-linear constraint counting over finite fields

problem_spec.summary: Over F_13, impose a twisted reciprocal functional symmetry f(x)=x^4 f(1/x) on F_13^* for a monic degree-8 polynomial, fix one coefficient, and require exactly four roots in F_13^* excluding the inversion fixed points ±1. Use inversion-orbit structure to convert "root-orbit conditions into affine-linear constraints in a small parameter family and count solutions.
givens: Work over the finite field F_13.
f(x) \in F_13[x] is monic of degree 8.
Twisted reciprocal symmetry: for all x \in F_13^*, f(x) = x^4 f(1/x).
The coefficient of x^3 in f(x) equals 5.
f has exactly 4 distinct roots in F_13^* and f(1)\neq0, f(-1)\neq0.
ask: Count the number of such polynomials f(x).

parameter(s).name: q
parameter(s).purpose: determines F_q^* cyclic structure and inversion orbits; instance uses q=13.

parameter(s).name: degree
parameter(s).purpose: polynomial degree; instance uses degree=8.

parameter(s).name: twist_exponent_k
parameter(s).purpose: defines twisted reciprocity f(x)=x^k f(1/x) on F_q^*; instance uses k=4.

parameter(s).name: fixed_coefficient
parameter(s).purpose: pins a coefficient in the reduced template; instance fixes a3=5.

parameter(s).name: root_count_target
parameter(s).purpose: target number of distinct roots in F_q^*; instance uses 4.

parameter(s).name: fixed_point_exclusion
parameter(s).purpose: forces roots to come only from inversion 2-cycles, not fixed points.

invariant(s).name: coefficient_template_from_twisted_symmetry
invariant(s).statement: Using x^(q-1)=1 on F_q^* and comparing reduced coefficients modulo x^(q-1)-1, the condition f(x)=x^4 f(1/x) on F_13^* forces a5=a6=a7=0 and a4=a0, a3=a1, leaving the template f(x)=x^8 + a0 x^4 + a1 x^3 + a2 x^2 + a1 x + a0.

invariant(s).name: parameter_reduction_by_fixed_coefficient
invariant(s).statement: Fixing the x^3 coefficient a3=5 forces a1=5, so the family is parameterized by (a0,a2)\inF_13^2.

invariant(s).name: inversion_orbits_in_Fq_star
invariant(s).statement: Inversion partitions F_13^* into fixed points {1,-1} and five 2-cycles: {2,7},{3,9},{4,10},{5,8},{6,11}.

invariant(s).name: root_orbit_closure_under_twisted_symmetry
invariant(s).statement: If r\inF_13^* and f(r)=0, then 0=f(r)=r^4 f(1/r), so f(1/r)=0. Thus roots occur in inversion-orbits.

invariant(s).name: exact_root_count_to_orbit_selection
invariant(s).statement: With fixed points excluded and root_count_target=4, the root set in F_13^* must consist of exactly two inversion 2-cycles (and no other orbit).

invariant(s).name: orbit_root_conditions_are_affine_lines
invariant(s).statement: For each 2-cycle representative r, the condition f(r)=0 is an affine-linear equation in (a0,a2), i.e. a line in F_13^2. Thus "exactly two 2-cycles are roots corresponds to points lying on exactly two of the five orbit-lines and not on the fixed-point root lines.

essential_assumption(s): The symmetry f(x)=x^4 f(1/x) is required only on F_13^*, not as a polynomial identity in F_13[x].
Roots counted are distinct and lie in F_13^* (x=0 excluded).
Fixed-point exclusion f(±1)\neq0 is enforced as part of the specification.

uniqueness_certificate(s).statement: The symmetry and fixed coefficient reduce f to a finite 2-parameter family (a0,a2)\inF_13^2. Each inversion 2-cycle root condition is a single affine-linear constraint in these parameters. Therefore the valid polynomials correspond to a finite, explicitly enumerable set of parameter points, uniquely determining the count.

Because every element of F_13^* lies in exactly one inversion-orbit, verifying that a parameter point lies on exactly two orbit-lines (and on neither fixed-point line) is equivalent to verifying the exact root-orbit pattern; hence correctness is checkable and the answer is unique.

technique(s).name: convert_pointwise_symmetry_to_coefficient_constraints
inputs: twisted symmetry f(x)=x^4 f(1/x) on F_13^*
technique(s).purpose: deduce relations among coefficients using reduction modulo x^(q-1)-1.
outputs: reduced coefficient template with few free parameters

technique(s).name: describe_inversion_orbits
technique(s).purpose: list fixed points and 2-cycles under inversion.
outputs: orbit decomposition used for root counting

technique(s).name: translate_root_conditions_to_affine_linear_constraints
inputs: reduced template and orbit representative r
technique(s).purpose: impose "orbit is a root by evaluating f(r)=0.
outputs: one affine-linear equation (a line) per orbit in parameter space

technique(s).name: intersection_and_parallelism_casework
technique(s).purpose: determine which orbit pairs can co-occur as roots and enumerate their intersections.
outputs: finite list of candidate parameter points

technique(s).name: enforce_exactly_two_orbits_and_exclude_fixed_points
technique(s).purpose: filter points that would create extra roots (third orbit or ±1 roots).

theorem(s).name: Fermat little theorem / cyclicity of F_q^*
theorem(s).role: use x^(q-1)=1 to reduce exponents and convert 1/x into x^(q-2).

theorem(s).name: uniqueness_of_reduced_representative_mod_x^(q-1)_minus_1
theorem(s).role: equality of functions on F_q^* corresponds to equality of reduced coefficients in degrees 0..q-2.

theorem(s).name: inversion_closure_from_twisted_reciprocity
theorem(s).role: f(r)=0 implies f(r^{-1})=0 because r^k\neq0.

theorem(s).name: linear algebra over finite fields
theorem(s).role: solving 2×2 affine-linear systems counts line intersections and detects parallel lines.

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: Finite-field multiplicative group structure (x^(q-1)=1) and orbit decomposition under inversion.

critical_trick(s): Use x^12=1 on F_13^* to turn twisted reciprocity into concrete coefficient vanishing/equalities.
Reduce the root-count condition to choosing inversion 2-cycles; each orbit-root condition becomes an affine line in parameter space.
Count valid polynomials by enumerating intersections of orbit-lines, excluding parallel pairs and forbidden fixed-point roots.

library(s).name: itertools
library(s).reason: brute-force over the 13^2 parameter pairs (a0,a2) and verify symmetry and root counts.

library(s).name: sage
library(s).reason: optional finite-field polynomial evaluation and root counting for independent verification.

task(s).name: brute_force_verify_count
task(s).reason: iterate over all (a0,a2)\inF_13^2, build f, check f(x)=x^4 f(1/x) on F_13^*, count roots, and enforce exclusions.

task(s).name: orbit_line_intersection_check
task(s).reason: compute affine lines from orbit representatives and verify the 5 surviving intersections.

title: Symmetry about x=1 forces the unique representation P(x)=(x-1)^4+b(x-1)^2+d with integers b,d.

identity.short_name: Count monic quartics symmetric about 1 with no real roots

problem_spec.summary: Count monic degree-4 integer-coefficient polynomials with bounded coefficients satisfying symmetry P(2-x)=P(x) (symmetry center 1), and having no real zeros. Use the shift x->y+1 to make an even quartic, reduce strict positivity to a quadratic inequality on z=y^2>=0, and count integer parameter pairs under the coefficient bounds.
givens: W = {P in V : P(2-x)=P(x) for all real x}.
Consider monic quartics P(x)=x^4+a_3x^3+a_2x^2+a_1x+a_0 in W with integers a_k and |a_k|<=7.
ask: Determine the number of such polynomials having no real roots.

parameter(s).name: degree
parameter(s).purpose: fixes quartic setting (even-after-shift reduction to y^4+by^2+d)

parameter(s).name: symmetry_center
parameter(s).purpose: determines the translation y=x-1 that makes the polynomial even

parameter(s).name: leading_coefficient
parameter(s).purpose: monic quartic so end behavior is +infinity and "no real roots implies strict positivity

parameter(s).name: coeff_bound
parameter(s).purpose: bounds integer coefficients to make the admissible set finite

invariant(s).name: symmetry_implies_even_after_shift
invariant(s).statement: Let y=x-1 and Q(y)=P(y+1). Then Q is even: Q(-y)=Q(y), so Q(y)=y^4+by^2+d.

invariant(s).name: coefficient_relations_from_center_one_symmetry
invariant(s).statement: For monic quartics with P(2-x)=P(x), necessarily a_3=-4 and a_1=8-2a_2. Equivalently P(x)=(x-1)^4+b(x-1)^2+d with integers b=a_2-6 and d=a_0-a_2+5.

invariant(s).name: positivity_reduction_to_quadratic
invariant(s).statement: With Q(y)=y^4+by^2+d, "no real roots (monic quartic) is equivalent to Q(y)>0 for all y, which is equivalent to z^2+bz+d>0 for all z>=0 via z=y^2.

essential_assumption(s): Coefficients are integers and the polynomial is monic of degree 4.

title: SL4 adjoint invariant polynomials (degree 50)

identity.short_name: SL4 adjoint invariant polynomials (degree 50)

subdomain(s): Chevalley restriction and Weyl group invariants
graded dimension / Hilbert series coefficient extraction

problem_spec.summary: Compute the dimension of the degree-d component of the invariant polynomial ring on the adjoint representation of SL_4(C). Use Chevalley restriction to identify the invariant ring with a Weyl-group invariant polynomial ring, then extract the degree-d dimension by counting monomials of prescribed degrees.
givens: G = SL_4(C).
V = sl_4(C) as the adjoint G-module.
W = (Sym^d(V^*))^G is the space of G-invariant homogeneous polynomial functions on V of degree d.
ask: Determine dim_C(W).

parameter(s).name: polynomial_degree_d
parameter(s).purpose: selects the graded piece of the invariant polynomial ring to be counted

parameter(s).name: lie_type_fixed
parameter(s).purpose: fixes the Weyl group (S_4) and fundamental invariant degrees (2,3,4) for the adjoint action

invariant(s).name: self_duality_of_adjoint
invariant(s).statement: V^* ≅ V as G-modules via the nondegenerate Killing form, so dimensions of invariant subspaces in Sym^d(V^*) and Sym^d(V) agree.

invariant(s).name: chevalley_restriction_for_adjoint
invariant(s).statement: The invariant ring (Sym(V^*))^G is isomorphic (as a graded algebra) to (Sym(h^*))^W where h is a Cartan subalgebra and W is the Weyl group of G.

invariant(s).name: generator_degrees_for_sl4
invariant(s).statement: For sl_4 (type A_3), (Sym(V^*))^G is a polynomial algebra on three homogeneous generators of degrees 2,3,4, corresponding to the elementary symmetric polynomials e2,e3,e4 in eigenvalues under the trace-zero constraint.

invariant(s).name: graded_dimension_as_monomial_count
invariant(s).statement: If (Sym(V^*))^G ≅ C[f2,f3,f4] with deg(f2)=2, deg(f3)=3, deg(f4)=4, then dim (Sym^d(V^*))^G = #{(a,b,c) in Z_{>=0}^3 : 2a+3b+4c=d}.

essential_assumption(s): Work over C (characteristic 0), so Chevalley restriction applies and invariant rings behave as expected.
The adjoint action of SL_4 on sl_4 is the standard conjugation action on traceless matrices.
Grading is by total polynomial degree, and dim_C of each graded piece is finite.
uniqueness_certificate(s): The graded algebra (Sym(V^*))^G is uniquely determined up to isomorphism by Chevalley restriction and the Lie type A_3, yielding a polynomial ring with generator degrees 2,3,4. Therefore the degree-d dimension is the unique coefficient in the Hilbert series 1/((1-t^2)(1-t^3)(1-t^4)), equivalently a unique finite count of solutions to 2a+3b+4c=d. Hence dim_C(W) is uniquely determined for each d.

technique(s).name: invariants_as_trivial_multiplicity
technique(s).purpose: interpret invariant polynomials as the trivial-representation multiplicity in a graded representation

technique(s).name: Chevalley_restriction_identify_invariant_ring
inputs: adjoint action of SL_4 on sl_4
technique(s).purpose: replace G-invariants on V by Weyl-group invariants on a Cartan subalgebra
outputs: invariant ring is a polynomial algebra with known generator degrees (2,3,4)

technique(s).name: Hilbert_series_coefficient_extraction
technique(s).purpose: express dim of the degree-d invariant space as a coefficient / monomial count

technique(s).name: linear_Diophantine_counting
technique(s).purpose: count the number of nonnegative integer solutions by parameterizing one variable and summing
outputs: explicit integer value of dim W

theorem(s).name: Killing_form_self_duality
theorem(s).role: identifies V^* with V for the adjoint representation

theorem(s).name: Chevalley_restriction_theorem
theorem(s).role: (Sym(g^*))^G ≅ (Sym(h^*))^W for the adjoint action

theorem(s).name: fundamental_theorem_of_symmetric_polynomials
theorem(s).role: describes (Sym(C^4)^*)^{S_4} as a polynomial ring on e1,e2,e3,e4, enabling the degree list 1,2,3,4

theorem(s).name: Hilbert_series_of_polynomial_ring
theorem(s).role: coefficient of 1/(1-t^{deg fi}) counts monomials in given total degree

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: invariants_as_fixed_vectors_in_graded_representations
reduction_to_Weyl_group_invariants_and_coefficient_extraction

critical_trick(s): Use Chevalley restriction (Weyl group invariants) to identify the invariant ring for the adjoint action as a polynomial ring.
Convert the dimension question into coefficient extraction in a Hilbert series, i.e. counting monomials of degrees 2,3,4 summing to d.
Count solutions to a linear Diophantine equation to obtain the integer dimension.

library(s).name: sympy
library(s).reason: optional exact coefficient extraction from generating functions and integer-solution enumeration

library(s).name: itertools
library(s).reason: optional bounded enumeration of (a,b,c) for verification

task(s).name: coefficient_check_via_series
task(s).reason: compute the coefficient of t^d in 1/((1-t^2)(1-t^3)(1-t^4)) to verify dim W

task(s).name: enumerate_diophantine_solutions
task(s).reason: brute-count nonnegative integer triples (a,b,c) with 2a+3b+4c=d as an independent verification

title: Pairwise difference fourth powers from quartic roots

identity.short_name: Pairwise difference fourth powers from quartic roots

problem_spec.summary: Given a monic quartic polynomial with roots r1..r4, compute the symmetric quantity T = sum_{i<j}(r_i - r_j)^4. Express T in terms of power sums p1..p4 by expanding pairwise differences, compute those power sums from coefficients using Newton identities, and report T modulo 1000.
givens: P(x) = x^4 - 5x^3 + x^2 - 8x + 3 is monic with roots r1,r2,r3,r4 in C.
ask: Compute T = sum_{i<j} (r_i - r_j)^4 modulo 1000.

parameter(s).name: quartic_coefficients
parameter(s).domain: Integers (or rationals) defining a monic quartic x^4 - e1 x^3 + e2 x^2 - e3 x + e4.
parameter(s).purpose: Controls the elementary symmetric sums (e1,e2,e3,e4) and hence determines the power sums p_k.

parameter(s).name: target_power
parameter(s).purpose: Specifies the exponent in (r_i - r_j)^4 for the pairwise-difference symmetric sum.

parameter(s).name: modulus
parameter(s).purpose: Controls output normalization by modular reduction.

invariant(s).name: vieta_map
invariant(s).statement: For P(x)=x^4 - e1 x^3 + e2 x^2 - e3 x + e4 with roots r_i, e1 = sum r_i, e2 = sum_{i<j} r_i r_j, e3 = sum_{i<j<k} r_i r_j r_k, e4 = product r_i.

invariant(s).name: newton_degree4
invariant(s).statement: The power sums p_k = sum_i r_i^k satisfy: p1=e1, p2=e1 p1 - 2e2, p3=e1 p2 - e2 p1 + 3e3, p4=e1 p3 - e2 p2 + e3 p1 - 4e4.

invariant(s).name: pairwise_difference_fourth_power_identity
invariant(s).statement: T = sum_{i<j}(r_i - r_j)^4 equals 4p4 - 4p1 p3 + 3p2^2.

essential_assumption(s): Polynomial is monic quartic (degree exactly 4) so Vieta/Newton formulas apply directly.
Roots may be complex; symmetric polynomials in roots are still well-defined and equal to coefficient expressions.
Modulo operation is applied to the final integer value of T (after simplification).
uniqueness_certificate(s): T is symmetric in the roots and is therefore a polynomial in the elementary symmetric sums (e1,e2,e3,e4), which are uniquely determined by the coefficients of P. Newton identities compute the required power sums uniquely from (e1,e2,e3,e4), so T (and T mod m) is uniquely determined for each instance.

technique(s).name: expand_pairwise_difference_power

title: x-weighted interlaced rational integral collapses via Newton sum

identity.short_name: x-weighted interlaced rational integral collapses via Newton sum

subdomain(s): change of variables in improper integrals
Viète and Newton identities for root sums
exact evaluation of compactly supported polynomial integrals

problem_spec.summary: For a monic degree-(r+1)/degree-r rational function with strictly interlacing real zeros and poles, evaluate an improper integral with an extra x-factor by splitting into monotone branches and changing variables y=F(x). A Newton-sum/Vieta identity gives Σ x_i(y) x_i'(y) as a simple polynomial in y, collapsing the integral to a fixed compact integral in y. Reduce the resulting rational number modulo 1000.
givens: zeros a = [-7, -2, 3, 10] and poles b = [-5, 1, 6] satisfy strict interlacing a1<b1<a2<b2<a3<b3<a4.
J = ∫_{-∞}^{∞} x \cdot h(F(x)) dx, interpreted as an improper integral over R.
Write J = p/q in lowest terms with p,q>0.
ask: Compute out = (p+q) mod 1000.

parameter(s).name: r
parameter(s).purpose: controls degrees deg(num)=r+1, deg(den)=r (here r=3)

parameter(s).name: zeros_a
parameter(s).domain: strictly increasing real list length r+1
parameter(s).purpose: numerator real roots

parameter(s).name: poles_b
parameter(s).domain: strictly increasing real list length r
parameter(s).purpose: denominator real roots

parameter(s).name: interlacing_pattern
parameter(s).purpose: guarantees monotone branch bijections

parameter(s).name: exponent_e
parameter(s).purpose: exponent in (1-u^2)_+^e (here 5)

parameter(s).name: weight_poly
parameter(s).purpose: extra even weight (here 1+2u^2)

parameter(s).name: x_weight
parameter(s).purpose: integrand includes an extra factor x

parameter(s).name: modulus
parameter(s).purpose: final reduction (here 1000)

invariant(s).name: residue_sign_invariant
invariant(s).statement: With strict interlacing and monic factors, residues α_j at poles b_j satisfy α_j<0. Consequently F'(x)=1+Σ(-α_j)/(x-b_j)^2>0 off the poles.

invariant(s).name: branch_surjectivity
invariant(s).statement: On each pole-separated interval, F is a C^1 increasing bijection onto R, hence has a unique C^1 inverse branch.

invariant(s).name: weighted_inverse_derivative_sum
invariant(s).statement: Let x_i(y) be the inverse branches solving F(x)=y (one per interval). For r=3 (quartic P_y), Σ_i x_i(y) x_i'(y) = y + (Σ a_i - Σ b_j), obtained from Newton sums (p2) and Viète coefficients.

essential_assumption(s): Strict interlacing a1<b1<a2<b2<a3<b3<a4.
Numerator and denominator are monic, so deg(F)=1 and branch limits are ±∞.
Poles are simple (distinct b_j), ensuring inverse branches are smooth.
h(u)=0 for |u|>1, ensuring the integral is finite (compact support after substitution).
uniqueness_certificate(s): Interlacing forces unique monotone inverse branches x_i(y) on each interval. The Newton/Vieta identity determines Σ x_i x_i' uniquely from the coefficients of P_y(x)=N(x)-yD(x). Therefore J reduces uniquely to a fixed integral over y\in[-1,1], giving a unique reduced fraction p/q and hence a unique residue (p+q) mod 1000.

title: Offset-epicycle simplicity threshold sum (mod 1000)

identity.short_name: Offset-epicycle simplicity threshold sum (mod 1000)

problem_spec.summary: For a two-frequency complex exponential curve with secondary frequency n+2 and discrete amplitude a=k/120, determine which (n,k) yield a simple curve (no multiple points) via a sharp sine-ratio threshold, then compute a weighted sum over all simple instances and reduce modulo 1000.
givens: For integers 0 \leq n \leq 50 and integers -200 \leq k \leq 200, set a = k/120 and m = n+2.
Define z_{n,a}(t) = e^{it} + a e^{imt} for 0 \leq t < 2\pi.
C_{n,a} is "simple" if there do not exist 0 \leq t < u < 2\pi with z_{n,a}(t)=z_{n,a}(u).
Define I(n,k)=1 if C_{n,k/120} is simple; otherwise I(n,k)=0.
ask: Compute out = S mod 1000 (representative in {0,1,...,999}).

parameter(s).name: N_max
parameter(s).purpose: upper limit of n (here 50)

parameter(s).name: K_bound
parameter(s).purpose: symmetric bound for k (here 200)

parameter(s).name: scale
parameter(s).purpose: discretizes a as a=k/scale (here 120)

parameter(s).name: frequency_shift
parameter(s).purpose: secondary frequency is m=n+frequency_shift (here n+2)

parameter(s).name: modulus
parameter(s).purpose: final reduction

invariant(s).name: simplicity_threshold_for_two_frequency_sum
invariant(s).statement: For integer m>=2, the curve t↦e^{it}+a e^{imt} has a multiple point iff |a|>1/m; hence it is simple iff |a|<=1/m.

invariant(s).name: discrete_k_threshold
invariant(s).statement: With a=k/scale and k integer, simplicity is equivalent to |k| <= floor(scale/m).

invariant(s).name: symmetric_k_cancellation
invariant(s).statement: For any integer M>=0, Σ_{k=-M}^{M} k = 0, so Σ_{k=-M}^{M} (n+k) = (2M+1)n.

essential_assumption(s): Multiple points are defined by equality z(t)=z(u) for distinct parameters t<u in [0,2\pi).
Since m=n+2>=2 for all n in range, no degenerate m=0,1 cases occur.
The k-range is wide enough that the active constraint comes from the simplicity threshold (here floor(120/2)=60<200).
uniqueness_certificate(s): The threshold |a|<=1/m uniquely determines I(n,k) for each (n,k) in the finite index set. Therefore S is uniquely determined, and its residue mod 1000 is unique.

technique(s).purpose: convert the complex equality into a sine-ratio equation in x=(u-t)/2

technique(s).name: sine_ratio_upper_bound
technique(s).purpose: bound |sin(mx)/sin x| by m to obtain the necessary condition |a|>1/m

title: Sech-soliton Gamma-limit to floor(exp) then binomial-square mod 1000

identity.short_name: Sech-soliton Gamma-limit to floor(exp) then binomial-square mod 1000

problem_spec.summary: Use the unique positive even L^2 ground state of u''+u^{2n+1}=u to define A_n=∫u_n^2, extract the constant L = lim (n(A_n-1)-ln(n+1)) via Gamma/digamma asymptotics, apply Φ=floor(e^L), then compute a binomial-square expression modulo 1000.
givens: For each integer n>=1, u_n is the unique u\inC^2(R) with u>0, u even, u,u'\inL^2(R), satisfying u''+u^{2n+1}=u.
Define A_n = ∫_{R} u_n(t)^2 dt.
Define L = lim_{n->∞} ( n(A_n-1) - ln(n+1) ).
Define Phi = floor(e^L).
Define R = (Σ_{j=0}^6 binom(6,j) Phi^j)^2 + 41 Phi, and reduce R modulo 1000.
ask: Compute R mod 1000.

parameter(s).name: binomial_degree
parameter(s).purpose: sets the binomial sum Σ_{j=0}^6 C(6,j)Phi^j

parameter(s).name: floor_map
parameter(s).purpose: converts the analytic limit L into an integer

parameter(s).name: modulus
parameter(s).purpose: final modular reduction

parameter(s).name: ode_family
parameter(s).purpose: defines the soliton/ground-state family producing A_n

invariant(s).name: energy_first_integral
invariant(s).statement: Multiplying the ODE by 2u' gives (u')^2 + (1/(n+1))u^{2n+2} - u^2 = C; L^2 decay forces C=0.

invariant(s).name: explicit_sech_profile
invariant(s).statement: The unique positive even L^2 solution is u_n(t)=(n+1)^{1/(2n)} sech(nt)^{1/n}.

invariant(s).name: digamma_constant_limit
invariant(s).statement: Using ψ(1/2)=-γ-2ln2, one gets ln A_n = (ln(n+1)+2ln2)/n + O(1/n^2), hence L = lim (n(A_n-1)-ln(n+1)) = 2 ln 2.

invariant(s).name: floor_exponential_is_integer
invariant(s).statement: Phi=floor(e^L)=floor(4)=4.

essential_assumption(s): The L^2 conditions imply u(t)->0 as |t|->∞, enabling determination of the integration constant.
The limit L exists as a real number (verified via asymptotic expansion).
uniqueness_certificate(s): The IVP determined by evenness and the forced amplitude u(0)=(n+1)^{1/(2n)} has a unique global solution, making A_n unique; the asymptotic expansion fixes L uniquely, and floor(exp(L)) then fixes Phi and R uniquely.

technique(s).purpose: determine C=0 and u(0)=(n+1)^{1/(2n)}

technique(s).name: solve_by_separation_to_sech
technique(s).purpose: obtain u_n(t)=(n+1)^{1/(2n)} sech(nt)^{1/n}

technique(s).name: compute_An_by_gamma_ratio
technique(s).purpose: evaluate A_n as a Gamma ratio

technique(s).name: asymptotic_limit_via_digamma
technique(s).purpose: compute L = lim (n(A_n-1)-ln(n+1))

technique(s).name: discrete_postprocess_and_mod
technique(s).purpose: compute Phi=floor(e^L) and R mod 1000

theorem(s).role: ensures unique solution for fixed initial data

theorem(s).role: gives ∫ sech^α = \sqrt\pi Γ(α/2)/Γ((1+α)/2)

theorem(s).role: Σ C(6,j)Phi^j=(1+Phi)^6

title: Distinct real-root count for 4-fold iterate hitting a critical value

identity.short_name: Distinct real-root count for 4-fold iterate hitting a critical value

problem_spec.summary: For a real cubic with two critical points, count distinct real solutions to an iterate equation P^{(k)}(x)=c when c is a critical value. Use monotonicity to determine the number of distinct real preimages of a value (1/2/3 depending on whether it is outside/at/inside the critical-value window), build a preimage-branching recursion that includes boundary nodes, and compute the level-k count.
givens: P(x)=x^3-3x+1 over the reals.
Count distinct real solutions to P^{(k)}(x)=c (multiplicity ignored).
ask: Determine the number of distinct real roots of P^{(4)}(x)=3.

parameter(s).name: k
parameter(s).purpose: iterate depth

parameter(s).name: target_value
parameter(s).purpose: value c whose real preimages under P^{(k)} are counted (here c is a critical value)

parameter(s).name: cubic
parameter(s).purpose: fixed cubic with two critical points at ±1

parameter(s).name: distinctness_convention
parameter(s).purpose: clarifies that repeated roots are counted once

invariant(s).name: critical_points_and_values
invariant(s).statement: P'(x)=3(x-1)(x+1), so critical points are x=-1,1 with critical values P(-1)=3 and P(1)=-1.

invariant(s).name: distinct_preimage_count_by_target_position
invariant(s).statement: For real α, the equation P(x)=α has: (i) 3 distinct real solutions iff -1<α<3, (ii) 1 real solution iff α<-1 or α>3, (iii) 2 distinct real solutions iff α\in{-1,3}.

invariant(s).name: boundary_node_transitions
invariant(s).statement: The boundary targets satisfy: P(x)=3 has distinct solutions x=-1 (double) and x=2; P(x)=-1 has distinct solutions x=1 (double) and x=-2.

invariant(s).name: disjoint_preimage_union
invariant(s).statement: For α1\neqα2, the sets {x:P(x)=α1} and {x:P(x)=α2} are disjoint; hence distinct-root counts add across nodes.

essential_assumption(s): Work over the reals, using monotonicity on (-∞,-1], [-1,1], [1,∞).
Distinct solutions are counted (ignore multiplicity).
Preimage sets for distinct parent values are disjoint, so counting is additive level-by-level.
uniqueness_certificate(s): The critical-value window and boundary behavior uniquely determine the distinct-preimage branching rules. Because preimage sets for distinct parent values are disjoint, the level counts satisfy a deterministic recurrence from a uniquely determined initial level. Therefore the final distinct real-root count is unique.

technique(s).name: critical_value_split_for_cubic_with_boundary
technique(s).purpose: Use critical points/values to determine when P(x)=α has 1,2,3 distinct real solutions, including the boundary cases α\in{-1,3}.

technique(s).name: interval_bracketing_for_preimages
technique(s).purpose: Use monotonicity and IVT to localize the distinct preimages into disjoint intervals, enabling type classification (A vs B) and confirming boundary-node solutions.

technique(s).name: type_based_preimage_tree_with_boundary_nodes
technique(s).purpose: Build a branching process that counts distinct real preimages level-by-level, incorporating special boundary nodes at α=3 and α=-1.

technique(s).name: solve_recurrence_and_sum
technique(s).purpose: compute the level-k total distinct root count

technique(s).name: distinctness_justification_via_disjoint_preimages
technique(s).purpose: prove the recurrence counts distinct roots (no overlaps across different parent values)

theorem(s).name: Intermediate Value Theorem
theorem(s).role: guarantees existence of roots in sign-change intervals used for localization

theorem(s).name: monotonicity_from_derivative_sign
theorem(s).role: ensures at most one solution per monotone interval, enabling 1/3/2 count rules

theorem(s).name: repeated_root_occurs_at_critical_values
theorem(s).role: boundary targets α\in{-1,3} correspond to double roots at critical points

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: monotonic branches intersect a horizontal line at most once; iterate counts via a branching recurrence

critical_trick(s): Treat the target as a critical value, so the first preimage step has 2 (not 3) distinct branches.
After boundary nodes are resolved, the remaining levels obey the standard B\toA+2B and A\toA branching.
Use disjointness of preimage sets to sum counts without overcounting (distinct-root focus).

title: 240^\circ overlap volume from two quadratic caps

identity.short_name: 240^\circ overlap volume from two quadratic caps

subdomain(s): intersection volume of solids of revolution
piecewise integration from min/max of radii

problem_spec.summary: Two planar regions bounded by y=0 and quadratic graphs (both above the x-axis) are each rotated through a fixed angle theta about the x-axis. The intersection of the two resulting partial solids of revolution has volume V, computed via cross-sectional disk sectors whose radius is the pointwise minimum of the two quadratics on the overlap x-interval. A final engineered expression of the form (K*pi)/(m*V) - C is evaluated.
givens: Region I: 0 <= y <= f(x) on an interval [x1,x2], with f a quadratic nonnegative on [x1,x2].
Region II: 0 <= y <= g(x) on an interval [x3,x4], with g a quadratic nonnegative on [x3,x4].
S_I is obtained by rotating Region I about the x-axis through angle theta.
S_II is obtained by rotating Region II about the x-axis through angle theta (same theta and same angular sector).
W = S_I ∩ S_II is the intersection solid and has volume V.
A final expression E = (K*pi)/(m*V) - C is requested (constants K,m,C given in the statement).
ask: Compute the exact value of E.

parameter(s).name: f
parameter(s).purpose: upper boundary above the x-axis for Region I

parameter(s).name: g
parameter(s).purpose: upper boundary above the x-axis for Region II

parameter(s).name: intervals
parameter(s).purpose: controls the overlap interval where both solids exist and where min(f,g) is evaluated

parameter(s).name: theta
parameter(s).purpose: fraction of full revolution for each solid

parameter(s).name: engineered_constants
parameter(s).purpose: chooses K,m,C so E evaluates to a small exact integer after substituting V

invariant(s).name: partial_rotation_scaling
invariant(s).statement: Rotating a planar region about the x-axis through angle theta produces disk-sector (or washer-sector) cross-sections whose area is scaled by factor theta/(2*pi) relative to a full 2*pi revolution.

invariant(s).name: overlap_cross_section_min_radius
invariant(s).statement: On the x-overlap interval [max(x1,x3), min(x2,x4)], the cross-section of the intersection solid W at position x is a disk-sector of radius r(x)=min(f(x), g(x)); outside the overlap interval the cross-section area is 0.

essential_assumption(s): f(x) and g(x) are nonnegative on their stated intervals so radii are valid without absolute values.
The two partial rotations share the same angular sector, so their intersection cross-section uses the same theta/(2*pi) scaling.
The interval overlap is nonempty; otherwise V=0 is still uniquely determined.
uniqueness_certificate(s): Given explicit quadratics f and g and explicit intervals, the overlap interval is fixed and the breakpoint set where f(x)=g(x) is finite and computable. This yields a unique piecewise-polynomial radius r(x)=min(f(x),g(x)), hence a unique volume V from a definite integral of a fully determined area function. Substituting V into E gives a unique real number; exact simplification yields a unique integer output.

technique(s).name: build_piecewise_min_area_and_integrate
technique(s).purpose: build the scaled cross-sectional area A(x) using r(x)=min(f(x),g(x)) and integrate to obtain V

technique(s).name: evaluate_engineered_expression
technique(s).purpose: compute E = (K*pi)/(m*V) - C exactly

theorem(s).role: V = ∫ A(x) dx for solids with known cross-sectional area A(x)

theorem(s).name: partial_rotation_factor
theorem(s).role: A_theta(x) = (theta/(2*pi)) * A_full(x)

theorem(s).name: polynomial_integration
theorem(s).role: exact antiderivatives for polynomials (hence for squared quadratics)

Find the unique switch point solving f(x)=g(x) to split the integral into polynomial pieces.
Use engineered constants so (K*pi)/(m*V) - C simplifies to an integer.

title: Rectangular grid lasers; parity forces a walk; count configs mod 1000

identity.short_name: Rectangular grid lasers; parity forces a walk; count configs mod 1000

problem_spec.summary: On a W×H rectangular grid of unit cells, each interior lattice junction emits exactly one directed unit laser segment to a neighboring junction. A move between adjacent cells is allowed iff their common side contains no laser. A Beautiful Walk is a path of adjacent cells from the southwest cell to the northeast cell using only allowed moves. For a specific rectangle (W,H), count configurations admitting at least one Beautiful Walk and reduce mod 1000.
givens: The grid is [0,W]×[0,H] with (W,H)=(7,5), tiled into W \cdot H unit square cabins.
Interior junctions are (a,b) with 1\leqa\leqW-1 and 1\leqb\leqH-1; each chooses exactly one directed unit segment to one of its four neighbors.
Boundary junctions emit no lasers. Laser segments may overlap.
A move between adjacent cabins is allowed iff their shared side contains no laser segment (either direction).
A Beautiful Walk starts at the southwest cabin and ends at the northeast cabin.
K is the number of configurations admitting at least one Beautiful Walk.
ask: Compute K mod 1000.

invariant(s).statement: If the northeast cabin is not reachable from the southwest cabin using allowed moves, the interface between reachable and non-reachable cabins (shared sides) forms a set of grid segments whose interior junction degrees are even; thus it contains an open boundary-to-boundary chain.

title: Maximize |(A+B) ∩ (A∪B)| when A,B partition {1..2n} inside {1..3n}

identity.short_name: Maximize |(A+B) ∩ (A∪B)| when A,B partition {1..2n} inside {1..3n}

problem_spec.summary: Choose disjoint A,B of size n from {1..3n} with the extra constraint that A∪B is exactly {1..2n}. Let E=A+B be the cross-sumset. Maximize how many elements of the partition set {1..2n} appear as cross-sums. Prove a sharp upper bound by a forcing-chain (induction) that would otherwise require too many elements in one set, and match it by an explicit construction covering an entire interval of sums.
givens: n = 100 (general n\geq3 used in derivation).
A,B ⊂ U are disjoint, |A|=|B|=n, and A ∪ B = {1,2,...,2n}.
E = {a+b : a\inA, b\inB}.
ask: Determine max |E ∩ (A ∪ B)| and evaluate it for n=100.

parameter(s).name: n
parameter(s).purpose: size of each set and scale of the labeled universe.

parameter(s).name: universe_multiplier
parameter(s).purpose: labels live in {1..3n} but only {1..2n} are occupied by A∪B.

parameter(s).name: occupied_prefix_constraint
parameter(s).purpose: forces A,B to be a partition of the first 2n labels (a stronger constraint than "any disjoint A,B").

parameter(s).name: target_functional
parameter(s).purpose: objective is the number of occupied labels representable as a cross-sum.

invariant(s).name: unavoidable_small_sum_exclusions
invariant(s).statement: 1 ∉ A+B always. Also 2 ∉ A+B because it would require 1+1 with 1 in both sets, impossible under disjointness.

invariant(s).name: forcing_chain_when_trying_to_cover_all_small_sums
invariant(s).statement: If 1\inA and 2\inB, then requiring 4\inA+B forces 3\inB; requiring 5\inA+B forces 4\inB; and in general, if {2,...,k}⊂B and k+2\inA+B then k+1\inB. Attempting to cover all sums 3..2n would force B to contain {2..2n-1}, contradicting |B|=n for n\geq3.

essential_assumption(s): A and B are disjoint subsets of {1,...,3n} of equal size n.
A ∪ B is fixed to be exactly the prefix {1,...,2n}.
E is the cross-sumset (only sums a+b with a\inA, b\inB).

uniqueness_certificate(s).statement: The forcing-chain argument shows max |E∩(A∪B)| \leq 2n-3 for all admissible partitions (n\geq3). A concrete construction achieves 2n-3, so the maximum is uniquely determined as 2n-3; for n=100 this equals 197.

technique(s).purpose: show certain small labels (1 and 2) can never be in the cross-sumset.

technique(s).name: forcing_chain_induction
technique(s).purpose: propagate forced membership of successive integers into one color class when attempting full coverage.

title: Cycle lengths enclosing exactly 4 interior vertices in side-5 triangular grid (sum)

identity.short_name: Cycle lengths enclosing exactly 4 interior vertices in side-5 triangular grid (sum)

problem_spec.summary: A robot moves between adjacent unit-triangle cells inside a side-5 triangular grid, forming a simple closed path (no backtrack, all visited cells distinct). The bounded region contains a specified number m of interior grid-vertices (where 6 unit triangles meet). Using duality and a perimeter formula k=6m-2e for the boundary length, enumerate the achievable cycle lengths for fixed m and compute their sum.
givens: T is an equilateral triangle of side length 5 partitioned into unit equilateral triangle cells.
Two cells are adjacent iff they share a common edge.
The robot moves between adjacent cells and never immediately backtracks.
A closed path of length k visits exactly k distinct cells (so it is a simple cycle in the adjacency graph).
Interior grid-vertices are vertices of the subdivision not on the boundary of T (six unit triangles meet there).
The region bounded by the cycle contains exactly m=4 interior grid-vertices.
ask: Let S be the set of all k for which such a cycle exists. Compute Σ_{k\inS} k and output it as a three-digit zero-padded number.

parameter(s).name: side_length
parameter(s).purpose: size of the triangular grid (here 5)

parameter(s).name: move_rule
parameter(s).purpose: forbids immediate reversal; with distinct-visit condition yields simple cycles

invariant(s).statement: Each interior grid-vertex corresponds to a hexagonal face in the dual graph (six unit triangles surround it). A simple cycle bounds a connected union of these faces.

invariant(s).name: fixed_m_perimeter_formula
invariant(s).statement: If the bounded region contains m interior vertices and the induced adjacency among them has e shared edges, then the boundary cycle length is k = 6m - 2e (each shared edge removes two boundary edges).

invariant(s).name: finite_internal_graph_for_side5
invariant(s).statement: For side_length=5, the interior-vertex adjacency graph has exactly 6 vertices and 9 edges, so for fixed m=4, the possible e values are finite and can be enumerated.

essential_assumption(s): A closed path visiting exactly k distinct cells is a simple cycle in the planar dual graph.
The region bounded by a simple cycle corresponds to a connected union of hexagonal faces (interior vertices).
The perimeter identity k=6m-2e applies to this dual-face union.
uniqueness_certificate(s): For fixed side_length=5 and m=4, every valid cycle length must equal 6m-2e for some connected 4-vertex subgraph of the fixed 6-vertex interior graph, and each achievable e yields an achievable cycle. Enumerating the finite set of possible e values uniquely determines S and hence Σ_{k\inS} k.

theorem(s).role: a connected graph on m vertices has at least m-1 edges

title: Sum of squares of achievable cycle lengths in a side-4 triangular grid

problem_spec.summary: In a side-4 triangular grid of unit triangles, robot closed paths with no immediate backtrack and "length = number of distinct visited cells" correspond to simple cycles in the dual adjacency graph. Use bipartite parity and a dual-face enclosure/perimeter model (bounded faces correspond to interior grid vertices) to enumerate achievable cycle lengths, then compute the sum of their squares.

Two cells are adjacent iff they share an edge; G is the resulting adjacency (dual) graph.
A robot moves between adjacent cells and never moves to the cell it occupied immediately before.
For integer k, we ask whether there exists a closed path of length k visiting exactly k distinct cells.

title: Expected misread error under 4-hour window on a 12-hour clock with identical hands

identity.short_name: Expected misread error under 4-hour window on a 12-hour clock with identical hands

subdomain(s): fractional part constraints on real variables

problem_spec.summary: Ambiguous readings with indistinguishable clock hands are characterized by a fractional-part system that reduces to discrete integer-part parameters. Under a bounded-error window, count all admissible ambiguous configurations, compute the expected misread error, and output the sum of numerator and denominator of the reduced fraction.
givens: The clock is a 12-hour cycle and is perfect, but the hour and minute hands are indistinguishable.
A valid reading (h,m) with 0<=h,m<12 satisfies h = floor(h) + m/12.
A configuration {h,m} is ambiguous if both (h,m) and (m,h) satisfy the valid-reading constraint and h != m.
Only configurations with |h-m| <= 4 are considered.
Choose uniformly among all such configurations; the error is |h-m|.
ask: Compute the expected error E. Write E=a/b in lowest terms with a,b positive integers and output a+b.
