title: Multiplicative order and exponent cycles (Euler/Fermat, primitive roots)

identity.short_name: Multiplicative order and exponent cycles (Euler/Fermat, primitive roots)

problem_spec.summary: Given integers $a,n$ with $\gcd(a,n)=1$, determine the multiplicative order $\mathrm{ord}_n(a)$ (the least $t\ge 1$ with $a^t\equiv 1\pmod n$), and use it to evaluate exponential congruences.
givens: $n\in\mathbb{N},\ n\ge 2$
$a\in\mathbb{Z}$
ask: Find the least positive integer $t$ such that $a^t\equiv 1\pmod n$.

parameter(s).name: n
parameter(s).purpose: varies the modulus; determines the unit group $(\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z})^\times$

parameter(s).name: a
parameter(s).purpose: selects an element of the unit group whose cycle length is sought

parameter(s).name: factorization_hint_mode
parameter(s).purpose: controls whether the instance supplies factorization information to compute the order efficiently

invariant(s).name: Euler_cycle_bound
invariant(s).statement: By Euler, $a^{\varphi(n)}\equiv 1\pmod n$, so $\mathrm{ord}_n(a)$ exists and divides $\varphi(n)$.

invariant(s).name: order_divides_phi
invariant(s).statement: $t=\mathrm{ord}_n(a)$ is the minimal positive period of the sequence $a^k\bmod n$, hence $t\mid\varphi(n)$.

essential_assumption(s): Coprimality $\gcd(a,n)=1$ (unit condition) is required so that a multiplicative order is defined.
If $n$ is not prime and no factorization data are given, computing $\mathrm{ord}_n(a)$ may be hard; the family allows a "hint mode" parameter to keep instances decidable.
uniqueness_certificate(s): The set $\{k\ge 1: a^k\equiv 1\pmod n\}$ is nonempty (Euler) and closed under gcd; its least element exists and is unique by well-ordering, hence $\mathrm{ord}_n(a)$ is well-defined and unique.

technique(s).name: Euler_theorem
technique(s).purpose: guarantee existence of a finite exponent cycle
outputs: certificate that some exponent (namely $\varphi(n)$) returns $1$ modulo $n$

technique(s).name: Fermat_little
technique(s).purpose: specialize Euler when $n$ is prime; simplifies order computation

technique(s).name: order_mod_n
technique(s).purpose: define and manipulate the minimal exponent period

technique(s).name: exponent_cycles
technique(s).purpose: reduce large exponents using periodicity

technique(s).name: lifting_solutions_mod_p_to_p^k_optional
inputs: solutions mod $p$ and local derivative condition
technique(s).purpose: (optional) lift order/congruence information from $p$ to $p^k$ when $n$ has prime power factors
outputs: constraints on order modulo prime powers

theorem(s).name: Euler_phi
theorem(s).role: provides the size of the unit group modulo $n$ and an exponent bound

theorem(s).name: Wilson_optional
theorem(s).role: optional tool to compute factorial residues modulo primes and deduce order information in special constructions

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: Units modulo $n$ form a finite multiplicative group; every element has finite order.

critical_trick(s): Use $a^{\varphi(n)}\equiv 1\pmod n$ to restrict the order to divisors of $\varphi(n)$, then test divisors (often via prime factorization of $\varphi(n)$).
Exploit prime-power structure (optional) to control orders when $n$ is not prime.

library(s).name: math
library(s).reason: factorial for $a=30!-3$, gcd/lcm arithmetic, and built-in pow(a,e,n) for modular exponentiation.

library(s).name: sympy
library(s).reason: totient and factorint to compute and factor $\varphi(n)$ for divisor-reduction order computation and cross-checking.

task(s).name: primary_structural_check
task(s).reason:
Correctness checks:
- Verified gcd(a,n)=1 and that the computed t satisfies pow(a,t,n)=1.
- "Computed t by divisor-reduction from phi(n): start t=phi(n) and repeatedly divide by prime factors q of t when pow(a,t/q,n)=1."
- Cross-checked component orders modulo 31, 31^2, and 5 and verified lcm(310,4)=620 gives pow(a,620,n)=1.

NT5 preflight attempts (cheap strategies that did not reveal t=620):
- "Small-mod-only attack on mods {2,4,5,7,8,11}: lcm of the resulting small orders was 60, not 620 (mods 3 and 9 were skipped since gcd(a,m) != 1)."
- "Tiny bounded scan: searched for the least t with pow(a,t,n)=1 in the range 1 <= t <= 500; none found."
- "Simple template probes: tested exponents {phi(n), phi(n)/2, phi(n)/3, phi(n)/4, phi(n)/5, 30, 930}; the smallest among these giving 1 was 1240, not the true order 620."

NT6 collision resistance:
- Plausible wrong-branch guess t=310 (ignoring the mod 5 component) differs from the true t=620 (mod 1000).

task(s).name: secondary_independent_check
task(s).reason: Independent brute-force order computation by incremental multiplication until the first return to 1 modulo n, confirming t=620.

title: Multiplicative order and exponent cycles (Euler/Fermat, primitive roots) - Buffed Seed

identity.short_name: Multiplicative order and exponent cycles (Euler/Fermat, primitive roots) - Buffed Seed

problem_spec.summary:
Let $\operatorname{ord}_m(a)$ denote the multiplicative order of $a$ modulo $m$ (for $\gcd(a,m)=1$), and for a prime $\ell$ let $v_\ell(x)$ be the exponent of $\ell$ in the prime factorization of the integer $x$. Fix odd primes $p
eq q$ and an integer $k\ge 2$, and suppose $r=2q+1$ is also prime with $r
eq p$. Put $n=p^k r$ and choose an integer $a$ coprime to $n$ such that $\operatorname{ord}_p(a)=q$, $v_p(a^q-1)=1$, and $a^q\equiv -1\pmod r$ but $a
ot\equiv -1\pmod r$; additionally assume $v_q(a-1)=1$. Consider the residue sequence $u_m\equiv a^m\pmod n$ and let $t$ be its least positive period.
givens: $p,q$ are odd primes with $p\neq q$ and $k\in\mathbb{Z}_{\ge 2}$.
$r=2q+1$ is prime and $r\neq p$; set $n=p^k r$.
$a\in\mathbb{Z}$ satisfies $\gcd(a,n)=1$.
$\operatorname{ord}_p(a)=q$ and $v_p(a^q-1)=1$.
$a^q\equiv -1\pmod r$ and $a\not\equiv -1\pmod r$.
For $m\ge 0$, define $u_m\equiv a^m\pmod n$.
ask: Stage 1 (order/period): Let $t$ be the least positive integer such that $u_{m+t}=u_m$ for all $m\ge 0$ (equivalently, $a^t\equiv 1\pmod n$). Determine $t$ explicitly in terms of $p,q,k$.
Stage 2 (valuation output): Compute $N\equiv t\,(v_p(a^t-1)+v_q(a^t-1))\pmod{1000}$ and report the unique integer $N\in\{0,1,\dots,999\}$.

parameter(s).name: p
parameter(s).purpose: supplies the prime-power factor $p^k$ that forces nontrivial order lifting

parameter(s).name: q
parameter(s).purpose: forces a prescribed nontrivial prime divisor of $\operatorname{ord}_p(a)$

parameter(s).name: k
parameter(s).purpose: controls the depth of the prime-power modulus and the size of the lifted order/valuation

parameter(s).name: a
parameter(s).domain: integer $a$ with $\gcd(a,p)=\gcd(a,r)=1$, $\operatorname{ord}_p(a)=q$, $v_p(a^q-1)=1$, $a^q\equiv -1\pmod r$ with $a\not\equiv -1\pmod r$, and $v_q(a-1)=1$, where $r=2q+1$ is prime and $n=p^k r$.
parameter(s).purpose: a unit whose order is lifted from $p$ to $p^k$ using valuation data, while simultaneously forcing a complementary order condition modulo $r$

invariant(s).name: Euler_cycle_bound
invariant(s).statement: By Euler, $a^{\varphi(n)}\equiv 1\pmod n$, so $\operatorname{ord}_n(a)$ exists and divides $\varphi(n)$.

invariant(s).name: order_divides_phi
invariant(s).statement: $t=\operatorname{ord}_n(a)$ is the minimal positive period of the sequence $a^m\bmod n$, hence $t\mid\varphi(n)$.

invariant(s).name: safe_prime_component
invariant(s).statement: $q$ is an odd prime and $r=2q+1$ is prime, so $(\mathbb{Z}/r\mathbb{Z})^{\times}$ is cyclic of order $2q$.

invariant(s).name: order_mod_r_forced
invariant(s).statement: From $a^q\equiv -1\pmod r$ and $a\not\equiv -1\pmod r$ (with $q$ odd), it follows that $\operatorname{ord}_r(a)=2q$.

invariant(s).name: p_power_lift_via_valuation
invariant(s).statement: The conditions $\operatorname{ord}_p(a)=q$ and $v_p(a^q-1)=1$ force $\operatorname{ord}_{p^k}(a)=q\,p^{k-1}$.

invariant(s).name: forced_divisor_and_nondivisor_in_global_period
invariant(s).statement: With $n=p^k r$ and $\gcd(p^k,r)=1$, we have $t=\operatorname{ord}_n(a)=\mathrm{lcm}(\operatorname{ord}_{p^k}(a),\operatorname{ord}_r(a))$, hence $q\,p^{k-1}\mid t$ and $2\mid t$, but $p^k\nmid t$ and $4\nmid t$.

invariant(s).name: valuation_nontriviality_two_primes
invariant(s).statement: The guards $v_p(a^q-1)=1$ and $v_q(a-1)=1$ ensure $v_p(a^t-1)=k\ge 2$ and $v_q(a^t-1)=2$ by LTE, so Stage 2 cannot collapse to a valuation-0 edge case.

essential_assumption(s): Coprimality $\gcd(a,n)=1$ (unit condition) is required so that a multiplicative order is defined.
The valuation guards $v_p(a^q-1)=1$ and $v_q(a-1)=1$ are essential; without them the lifted order modulo $p^k$ and the valuation term in Stage 2 are not forced.
Instances are intended to be solved by decomposing the order locally and then applying LTE-based lifting; brute-force testing of divisors of $\varphi(n)$ is excluded by design for this family.
uniqueness_certificate(s): The constraints imply $\operatorname{ord}_{p^k}(a)=q p^{k-1}$ (order lifting with $v_p(a^q-1)=1$) and $\operatorname{ord}_r(a)=2q$ (safe-prime modulus with $a^q\equiv -1$ and $a\not\equiv -1$). Since $\gcd(p^k,r)=1$, the order modulo $n=p^k r$ is uniquely

title: Multiplicative order via principal units + quadratic reciprocity (halved-period valuation mix)

identity.short_name: Multiplicative order via principal units + quadratic reciprocity (halved-period valuation mix)

problem_spec.summary: Modulus n=p^k r with p an odd prime and r=2q+1 a safe prime satisfying r\equiv3 (mod 8). A unit a is specified simultaneously as a principal unit modulo p^k (a\equiv1+p) and as a fixed residue modulo r (a\equiv2), so the order modulo p^k is forced by a principal-unit/LTE argument while the order modulo r is forced by quadratic reciprocity plus Euler's criterion. An extra congruence a\equiv1+q (mod q^2) forces a controlled q-adic valuation. The task is to compute the global period t=ord_n(a) and then a normalized quantity involving t/2 and two valuations.
givens: $p$ and $q$ are odd primes with $p\ne q$, and $k\in\mathbb{Z}_{\ge 2}$.
$q\equiv 1\pmod 4$ and $r=2q+1$ is prime with $r\ne p$; set $n=p^k r$.
$a\in\mathbb{Z}$ satisfies the congruences $a\equiv 1+p\pmod{p^k}$, $a\equiv 1+q\pmod{q^2}$, and $a\equiv 2\pmod r$.
For $m\ge 0$, define $u_m\equiv a^m\pmod n$ and let $t$ be the least positive period (equivalently $a^t\equiv 1\pmod n$).
ask: Stage 1 (period as order): Determine $t=\operatorname{ord}_n(a)$ explicitly in terms of $p,q,k$ under the above constraints.
Stage 2 (normalized valuation mix): Let $V=\frac{t}{2}\bigl(v_p(a^t-1)+v_q(a^t-1)\bigr)$ (it is forced that $t$ is even). Compute the unique $N\in\{0,1,\dots,999\}$ such that $N\equiv V\pmod{1000}$.

parameter(s).name: p
parameter(s).purpose: supplies the prime-power modulus $p^k$ whose order is forced by principal-unit structure

parameter(s).name: q
parameter(s).domain: odd prime $q\ge 5$ with $q\equiv 1\pmod 4$ and $r=2q+1$ prime, $r\ne p$
parameter(s).purpose: produces a safe prime $r$ with $r\equiv 3\pmod 8$ so $(\frac{2}{r})=-1$ and hence $\operatorname{ord}_r(2)=2q$

parameter(s).name: k
parameter(s).purpose: controls the depth of the principal-unit order and the forced $p$-adic valuation

parameter(s).name: a
parameter(s).domain: integer a satisfying a\equiv1+p (mod p^k), a\equiv1+q (mod q^2), and a\equiv2 (mod r), where r=2q+1 is prime and n=p^k r.
parameter(s).purpose: couples a principal-unit order computation modulo p^k with a quadratic-residue forcing modulo r and a controlled q-adic valuation

invariant(s).name: CRT_admissibility
invariant(s).statement: The moduli $p^k$, $q^2$, and $r$ are pairwise coprime, so the congruence system defining $a$ is solvable (CRT).

invariant(s).name: unit_condition
invariant(s).statement: From $a\equiv 1+p\pmod{p^k}$ and $a\equiv 2\pmod r$, we have $\gcd(a,n)=1$, so $\operatorname{ord}_n(a)$ exists.

invariant(s).name: principal_unit_order_forced
invariant(s).statement: The constraint $a\equiv 1+p\pmod{p^k}$ forces $\operatorname{ord}_{p^k}(a)=p^{k-1}$.

invariant(s).name: two_is_nonresidue_mod_r
invariant(s).statement: Since $q\equiv 1\pmod 4$ and $r=2q+1$, we have $r\equiv 3\pmod 8$, hence $(\frac{2}{r})=-1$.

invariant(s).name: order_mod_r_forced_by_Euler_criterion
invariant(s).statement: With $a\equiv 2\pmod r$ and $(\frac{2}{r})=-1$, Euler's criterion forces $a^q\equiv -1\pmod r$, hence $\operatorname{ord}_r(a)=2q$.

invariant(s).name: global_period_lcm
invariant(s).statement: Since $n=p^k r$ with $\gcd(p^k,r)=1$, we have $t=\operatorname{ord}_n(a)=\mathrm{lcm}(\operatorname{ord}_{p^k}(a),\operatorname{ord}_r(a))=\mathrm{lcm}(p^{k-1},2q)=2q\,p^{k-1}$.

invariant(s).name: forced_evenness
invariant(s).statement: The period $t$ is even (because $2\mid \operatorname{ord}_r(a)$), so $t/2\in\mathbb{Z}$ is well-defined.

invariant(s).name: forced_two_valuations
invariant(s).statement: The guards $v_p(a-1)=1$ and $v_q(a-1)=1$ together with $t=2q\,p^{k-1}$ force $v_p(a^t-1)=k$ and $v_q(a^t-1)=2$ by LTE.

essential_assumption(s): $p,q,r$ are odd primes with $p\ne q$, $r=2q+1$ prime, and $q\equiv 1\pmod 4$ (ensures $r\equiv 3\pmod 8$ so $(\frac{2}{r})=-1$).
The principal-unit constraint $a\equiv 1+p\pmod{p^k}$ is essential; it forces $\operatorname{ord}_{p^k}(a)=p^{k-1}$ and fixes the $p$-adic valuation via LTE.
The residue constraint $a\equiv 2\pmod r$ is essential; combined with quadratic reciprocity and Euler criterion it forces $\operatorname{ord}_r(a)=2q$.
The valuation guard $a\equiv 1+q\pmod{q^2}$ is essential; without it, $v_q(a^t-1)$ is not forced.
uniqueness_certificate(s): The congruences force $\operatorname{ord}_{p^k}(a)=p^{k-1}$ (principal-unit order) and, since $r\equiv 3\pmod 8$, quadratic reciprocity gives $(\frac{2}{r})=-1$; with $a\equiv 2\pmod r$, Euler's criterion forces $a^q\equiv -1\pmod r$, hence $\operatorname{ord}_r(a)=2q$. With $\gcd(p^k,r)=1$ this uniquely forces

title: Valuation-filtered p-th roots of unity modulo p^a q^b

identity.short_name: Valuation-filtered p-th roots of unity modulo p^a q^b

problem_spec.summary: Fix distinct odd primes p,q with p | (q-1) and integers a>=2, b>=1. Let M=p^a q^b and count residue classes x mod M satisfying x^p \equiv 1 together with mixed p-adic valuation constraints on x-1 at primes p and q. The solution splits locally via CRT, uses LTE to force the p-adic congruence under the exact valuation, uses cyclicity of (Z/q^bZ)^× plus Hensel lifting to locate the unique root congruent to 1 (mod q), then multiplies local counts.
givens: Distinct odd primes p,q with p < q.
Integers a>=2 and b>=1.
Divisibility guard p | (q-1).
Define M = p^a q^b.
Define S to be the set of residue classes x (mod M) such that:
x^p \equiv 1 (mod M),
ask: Let C = |S|. Output the unique integer A in {0,...,999} with A \equiv C (mod 1000).

parameter(s).name: p
parameter(s).purpose: exponent in x^p \equiv 1 and the p-adic valuation jump via LTE

parameter(s).name: q
parameter(s).domain: odd prime distinct from p with p | (q-1)
parameter(s).purpose: ensures existence of nontrivial p-th roots of unity modulo q and hence modulo q^b

parameter(s).name: a
parameter(s).purpose: sets the p-adic depth; makes v_p(x-1)=a-1 >= 1 so LTE forces x^p \equiv 1 (mod p^a)

parameter(s).name: b
parameter(s).purpose: sets the q-adic lifting depth; Hensel lifting preserves root classes from mod q to mod q^b

invariant(s).name: crt_local_global_factorization
invariant(s).statement: For M=p^a q^b with gcd(p^a,q^b)=1, residues x mod M correspond bijectively to pairs (x mod p^a, x mod q^b), and the defining congruences/valuations split into local constraints.

invariant(s).name: lte_valuation_jump_at_p
invariant(s).statement: If v_p(x-1)=a-1 (>=1) then LTE gives v_p(x^p-1)=a, so x^p \equiv 1 (mod p^a) is forced by the valuation constraint.

invariant(s).name: cyclic_units_at_q
invariant(s).statement: For odd prime q, (Z/q^bZ)^× is cyclic of order φ(q^b)=q^{b-1}(q-1).

invariant(s).name: hensel_simple_root_lifts_preserve_mod_q_class
invariant(s).statement: For f(X)=X^p-1 with q not dividing p, every root mod q is simple and lifts uniquely to a root mod q^b, preserving its residue class mod q.

invariant(s).name: q_valuation_filter_excludes_exactly_one_root
invariant(s).statement: When p | (q-1), X^p \equiv 1 (mod q^b) has exactly p roots, and exactly one is \equiv 1 (mod q); thus v_q(x-1)=0 keeps precisely p-1 roots.

essential_assumption(s): p and q are distinct odd primes so CRT applies and LTE/Hensel are valid in the stated forms.
The guard p | (q-1) is required so that nontrivial p-th roots of unity exist modulo q (hence modulo q^b); otherwise S may be empty.
a >= 2 ensures v_p(x-1)=a-1 >= 1 so the LTE forcing step is non-degenerate.
uniqueness_certificate(s): CRT splits the set S into independent local sets S_p and S_q. LTE forces the p-adic congruence once v_p(x-1)=a-1 and yields exactly p-1 classes modulo p^a. Cyclicity of (Z/q^bZ)^× gives exactly p roots to x^p \equiv 1 modulo q^b, and Hensel shows exactly one is \equiv1 (mod q); the filter v_q(x-1)=0 removes it, leaving p-1. Therefore C=(p-1)^2 is forced and the normalized output mod 1000 is unique.

technique(s).name: chinese_remainder_theorem
inputs: modulus factorization M=p^a q^b with gcd(p^a,q^b)=1
technique(s).purpose: split global conditions into independent local problems and multiply local counts

technique(s).name: p_adic_valuation_filtering
inputs: exact valuation constraints v_p(x-1)=a-1 and v_q(x-1)=0
technique(s).purpose: parametrize residue classes by exact divisibility/coprimality conditions and count admissible classes
outputs: local counts |S_p|=p-1 and |S_q|=p-1 under the stated guards

technique(s).name: lifting_the_exponent
technique(s).purpose: force v_p(x^p-1)=v_p(x-1)+1, turning the p-adic congruence into a consequence of the valuation constraint
outputs: x^p \equiv 1 (mod p^a) is automatic once v_p(x-1)=a-1

technique(s).name: cyclic_group_root_count
technique(s).purpose: count solutions to y^p=1 via gcd(p, φ(q^b))
outputs: exactly p solutions to y^p \equiv 1 (mod q^b) when p | (q-1)

technique(s).name: hensel_lifting
technique(s).purpose: lift roots from mod q to mod q^b uniquely and preserve the mod-q class
outputs: certify that exactly one p-th root is \equiv1 (mod q), enabling the valuation exclusion

theorem(s).name: chinese_remainder_theorem
theorem(s).role: bijection between residues mod p^a q^b and pairs mod p^a and q^b

theorem(s).name: lifting_the_exponent_lemma
theorem(s).role: gives v_p(x^p-1)=v_p(x-1)+1 when p is odd and p | (x-1)

theorem(s).name: primitive_root_theorem_for_prime_powers
theorem(s).role: ensures (Z/q^bZ)^× is cyclic for odd prime q

theorem(s).name: cyclic_group_root_count_lemma
theorem(s).role: in a cyclic group of order n, y^p=1 has exactly gcd(p,n) solutions

theorem(s).name: hensel_lemma_simple_root
theorem(s).role: lifts each simple root mod q uniquely to a root mod q^b, preserving its class mod q

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: Local-to-global structure via prime power moduli: CRT reduces global congruences to local ones, and p-adic valuation controls divisibility and root lifting.

critical_trick(s): Use LTE to turn v_p(x-1)=a-1 into the forced congruence x^p \equiv 1 (mod p^a), collapsing the p-adic side to a pure valuation count.
Use cyclicity of (Z/q^bZ)^× to count p-th roots modulo q^b, then use Hensel lifting to show only one root is \equiv1 (mod q), so the filter v_q(x-1)=0 excludes exactly one.
Multiply local counts using CRT to obtain C=(p-1)^2 and reduce modulo 1000.

library(s).name: math
library(s).reason: gcd checks and modular inverse scaffolding for CRT computations

library(s).name: sympy
library(s).reason: primitive_root for prime-power moduli and symbolic canonicalization for novelty equivalence audit

task(s).name: primary_structural_check
task(s).reason:
Correctness checks consistent with the intended math path:
- Verified gcd(p^a,q^b)=1 and computed M=p^a q^b.
- Enumerated the p-adic candidates x_p = 1 + p^(a-1)t (t=1..p-1) and verified v_p(x_p-1)=a-1 and x_p^p \equiv 1 (mod p^a).
- Enumerated all p-th roots modulo q^b via a primitive root and verified there are exactly p of them; confirmed only one is \equiv1 (mod q), so the filter v_q(x-1)=0 leaves p-1.
Preflight attempts:
- "Attempt A: naive guess C_guess=p-1 gives 22, but computed C=484 (mismatch)."
- "Attempt B: bounded search x in [0,200000) finds 0 solutions (the smallest solution exceeds this range), so brute scanning cannot shortcut the count."
- "Attempt C: dropping condition (iii) changes the count to C_relaxed=(p-1)p=506, showing partial information leaves multiple candidates in {0,...,999}."

task(s).name: secondary_independent_check
task(s).reason:
Independent verification:
- Constructed all solutions x mod M by CRT-combining the 22 admissible x_p classes with the 22 admissible x_q classes (total 484).
- Directly checked for every constructed x that (i) x^p \equiv 1 (mod M), (ii) v_p(x-1)=a-1, (iii) v_q(x-1)=0, and confirmed all 484 are distinct modulo M.

task(s).name: novelty_equivalence_audit
task(s).reason:
Canonicalized the parent core congruence polynomial (x-1, up to nonzero scaling) versus the daughter core polynomial (x^p-1) using sympy expand+simplify+together+factor and monic Poly normalization.
Tried deterministic one-variable disguises: x->x, -x, 1-x, x+1, x-1, 1/x, -1/x (clearing denominators and comparing monic numerators).
No transform matched x-1; degrees differ (1 vs p), so the families are not equivalent under the allowed disguises.

final_answer.normalization: A is the unique integer in {0,...,999} with A \equiv C (mod 1000).

title: $p$-adic valuation algebra (sum/product rules, compare $v_p$)

identity.short_name: $p$-adic valuation algebra (sum/product rules, compare $v_p$)

problem_spec.summary: Given exact powers of 2 dividing two monomials in integers x,y, determine the forced value of v_2 of a three-term expression built from x and y.
givens: $p=2$ is a prime and $v_2$ denotes the $2$-adic valuation.
$x,y\in\mathbb{Z}$ with $x\neq 0$ and $y\neq 0$.
Notation: $2^t\parallel n$ means $2^t\mid n$ but $2^{t+1}\nmid n$.
$2^{656}\parallel x^2y^3$.
$2^{639}\parallel x^5y$.
ask: Compute $v=v_2\bigl(x^5y^2+x^3y^4+x^{10}\bigr)$.

parameter(s).name: p
parameter(s).purpose: selects the valuation $v_p$

parameter(s).name: x
parameter(s).purpose: first integer input

parameter(s).name: y
parameter(s).purpose: second integer input

parameter(s).name: relation_mode
parameter(s).purpose: controls whether $v_p(x+y)$ is exactly determined or only lower-bounded

invariant(s).name: valuation_additivity
invariant(s).statement: For nonzero integers, $v_p(xy)=v_p(x)+v_p(y)$ and $v_p(x/y)=v_p(x)-v_p(y)$ when $y\mid x$ in $\mathbb{Z}_{(p)}$.

invariant(s).name: min_rule_for_sum_when_unequal
invariant(s).statement: If $v_p(x)\ne v_p(y)$ then $v_p(x+y)=\min\{v_p(x),v_p(y)\}$.

essential_assumption(s): In the default mode, the instance enforces $v_p(x)\ne v_p(y)$ so that $v_p(x+y)$ is uniquely determined.
When using the equal-valuation mode, the instance must provide enough auxiliary information (e.g., $v_p(x/p^t + y/p^t)$) if an exact value beyond the lower bound is desired.
uniqueness_certificate(s): Under the condition $v_p(x)\ne v_p(y)$, the valuation of the sum is forced to be the minimum, hence uniquely determined; $v_p$ itself is uniquely defined for each nonzero integer.

technique(s).name: valuation_vp_definition
technique(s).purpose: encode divisibility by prime powers as an exponent
outputs: the integer $v_p(n)$ such that $p^{v_p(n)}\mid n$ but $p^{v_p(n)+1}\nmid n$

technique(s).name: vp_product
technique(s).purpose: linearize multiplicative divisibility constraints

technique(s).name: vp_sum_exact_when_unequal
technique(s).purpose: compute $v_p(x+y)$ exactly

technique(s).name: compare_vp_on_both_sides
technique(s).purpose: compare valuations to deduce contradictions or to isolate unknown exponents
outputs: inequalities/equalities of integers coming from valuations

theorem(s).name: exact_division_arguments
theorem(s).role: translate "$p^t\mid n$" into "$v_p(n)\ge t$" and vice versa

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: Divisibility by prime powers is controlled by a single integer exponent; valuation rules convert multiplicative structure into additive bookkeeping.

critical_trick(s): Factor out the smaller power of $p$ from a sum and use that $p$ does not divide the remaining unit-sum when valuations are unequal.
Use additivity of $v_p$ on products to convert given exact divisibilities into a solvable linear system for $v_p(x)$ and $v_p(y)$.

library(s).name: math
library(s).reason: Core integer arithmetic and sanity checks (e.g., gcd).

library(s).name: itertools
library(s).reason: Small enumerations for preflight heuristics.

library(s).name: random

task(s).name: primary_structural_check
task(s).reason:
Correctness + NT5 preflight:
  - Correctness: solve for (a,b)=(v2(x),v2(y)) from v2(x^2y^3)=656 and v2(x^5y)=639; compute v2 of each term in x^5y^2+x^3y^4+x^{10}; certify a unique minimum; conclude v2 of the sum.
  - NT5(a) small-mod-only: for mods in {2,3,4,5,7,8,9,11}, sampled many valid (x,y) and recorded the residue sets of S=x^5y^2+x^3y^4+x^{10} modulo each modulus; found S\equiv0 mod 2,4,8 always, but S mod 3/5/7/9/11 varies widely, so small-mod data cannot determine v2(S) (it only forces v2(S)\geq3).
  - NT5(b) tiny bounded scan: brute-forced |x|,|y|\leq200 for exact satisfaction of 2^{656}||x^2y^3 and 2^{639}||x^5y; found no solutions, so the answer cannot be guessed from tiny-range enumeration.
  - NT5(c) template fits: searched for simple affine templates α \cdot 656+β \cdot 639+γ with α,β\in[-3,3], γ\in[-50,50], and also common min/max/sum/diff/gcd/lcm combinations; none matched the true answer.
  - NT6 collision: the plausible wrong-branch value 907 differs from the true value.

task(s).name: secondary_independent_check
task(s).reason: Independent randomized check: construct many examples x=2^{97}u, y=2^{154}v with odd u,v, verify the givens by direct v2 computation, and compute v2(x^5y^2+x^3y^4+x^{10}) directly; confirm it is constant.

title: $p$-adic valuation algebra (sum/product rules, compare $v_p$) - Buffed Seed

identity.short_name: $p$-adic valuation algebra (sum/product rules, compare $v_p$) - Buffed Seed

problem_spec.summary: Let $p$ and $q$ be distinct odd primes and let $x,y$ be nonzero integers with $v_p(x)=v_p(y)$ and $v_q(x)

title: Hensel-lifted i mod p^4 + mixed-order unit ratio; compute v_p(x^t-y^t)+v_q(x-y)+v_2(C(2t,t)) mod 1000

identity.short_name: Hensel-lifted i mod p^4 + mixed-order unit ratio; compute v_p(x^t-y^t)+v_q(x-y)+v_2(C(2t,t)) mod 1000

problem_spec.summary: Construct w as the Hensel lift of a square root of -1 modulo p^4 (forcing a 4-torsion Teichmueller component). Build x,y with equal p-adic valuation and define r\equivuv^{-1} (mod p^4). Use Teichmueller×principal-unit splitting to force t=ord_{p^4}(r)=4p^3, then compute v_p(x^t-y^t) via transfer+LTE, compute v_q(x-y) via the unequal-valuation min rule at q, and compute v_2(C(2t,t)) via Legendre/Kummer digit-sum. Output modulo 1000.
givens: $p,q$ are distinct odd primes with $p\equiv 1\pmod{4}$.
Choose $w_0$ with $w_0^2\equiv -1\pmod p$ and $p\nmid 2w_0$; let $w$ be the unique Hensel lift modulo $p^4$ with $w\equiv w_0\pmod p$ and $w^2\equiv -1\pmod{p^4}$.
Let $x=p^{a}(w\,q^{b}+p\,q^{c})$ and $y=p^{a}q^{b}$ with integers $a\ge 1$ and $b>c\ge 0$.
Define $r\in(\mathbb{Z}/p^{4}\mathbb{Z})^{\times}$ by $r\equiv uv^{-1}\pmod{p^{4}}$ after writing $x=p^a u$, $y=p^a v$ with $p\nmid uv$.
Assume $q\nmid p$ and $b>c$ so that $v_q(x)\ne v_q(y)$ (hence the min rule applies).
ask: Let $t:=\operatorname{ord}_{p^4}(r)$. Compute $N:=v_p(x^t-y^t)+v_q(x-y)+v_2\binom{2t}{t}$.
Output the unique integer $N_0\in\{0,1,\dots,999\}$ such that $N_0\equiv N\pmod{1000}$.

parameter(s).name: p
parameter(s).purpose: prime for the p-adic valuation and modulus $p^4$; ensures -1 is a quadratic residue

parameter(s).name: q
parameter(s).purpose: secondary valuation via unequal-valuation min rule at q

parameter(s).name: w0
parameter(s).purpose: selects the Hensel lift branch

parameter(s).name: a
parameter(s).purpose: common p-adic valuation of x and y

parameter(s).name: b
parameter(s).purpose: controls the q-adic valuation of y

parameter(s).name: c
parameter(s).purpose: forces unequal q-valuations $v_q(x)=c$ and $v_q(y)=b$

invariant(s).name: hensel_lifted_i_root
invariant(s).statement: $w\equiv w_0\pmod p$ and $w^2\equiv -1\pmod{p^4}$; w is unique modulo $p^4$ (simple-root Hensel lift).

invariant(s).name: teichmueller_component_order_4
invariant(s).statement: $\operatorname{ord}_{p^4}(w)=4$.

invariant(s).name: equal_p_valuation
invariant(s).statement: $v_p(x)=v_p(y)=a$ and $x=p^a u$, $y=p^a v$ with $p\nmid uv$.

invariant(s).name: teichmueller_times_depth1_principal_unit
invariant(s).statement: With $s:=rw^{-1}$ in $(\mathbb{Z}/p^4\mathbb{Z})^{\times}$, one has $s\equiv 1\pmod p$ but $s\not\equiv 1\pmod{p^2}$, hence $\operatorname{ord}_{p^4}(s)=p^3$.

invariant(s).name: forced_mixed_order_4p3
invariant(s).statement: $\operatorname{ord}_{p^4}(r)=4p^3$.

invariant(s).name: unequal_q_valuations
invariant(s).statement: $v_q(x)\ne v_q(y)$, hence $v_q(x-y)=\min\{v_q(x),v_q(y)\}$.

invariant(s).name: central_binomial_chain_invariant
invariant(s).statement: $v_2\binom{2t}{t}=s_2(t)$ where $s_2(t)$ is the number of 1s in the binary expansion of t.

essential_assumption(s): $p\equiv 1\pmod 4$ and $w_0^2\equiv -1\pmod p$ with $p\nmid 2w_0$, so Hensel lifting gives a unique $w\pmod{p^4}$.
$s=rw^{-1}$ is a nontrivial depth-1 principal unit (not $\equiv 1\pmod{p^2}$), forcing $\operatorname{ord}_{p^4}(s)=p^3$.
$p\nmid uv$, so LTE applies to $(u^4,v^4)$ at the prime p.
$v_q(x)\ne v_q(y)$, so the q-adic term $v_q(x-y)$ is rigid by the min rule.
uniqueness_certificate(s): Hensel rigidity: the simple-root Hensel lemma forces a unique $w\pmod{p^4}$ and hence forces $\operatorname{ord}_{p^4}(w)=4$.
Mixed-order rigidity: $r=w\cdot s$ with $\operatorname{ord}(w)=4$ and $\operatorname{ord}(s)=p^3$, and $\gcd(4,p)=1$, so $t=\operatorname{ord}(r)=4p^3$ is forced.
Valuation rigidity: transfer gives $v_p(u^4-v^4)=v_p(r^4-1)$, LTE gives $v_p(u^t-v^t)=v_p(u^4-v^4)+3$, and the unequal-valuation rule gives $v_q(x-y)$, so N is forced.
Binomial rigidity: Legendre/Kummer gives $v_2\binom{2t}{t}=s_2(t)$, which is uniquely determined once t is forced.

theorem(s).name: hensel_lemma_simple_root
theorem(s).role: unique lifting of a simple root from mod p to mod p^4; defines w.

theorem(s).name: euler_criterion_minus_one
theorem(s).role: (-1/p)=(-1)^{(p-1)/2}; ensures -1 is a quadratic residue when p\equiv1 (mod 4).

theorem(s).name: teichmueller_unit_decomposition
theorem(s).role: supports splitting a unit modulo p^4 into torsion and principal-unit parts.

theorem(s).name: principal_unit_order_mod_p4_depth1
theorem(s).role: if $s\equiv 1\pmod p$ but $s\not\equiv 1\pmod{p^2}$, then $\operatorname{ord}_{p^4}(s)=p^3$.

theorem(s).name: LTE_for_u_n_minus_v_n
theorem(s).role: for odd p dividing U-V and p not dividing UV, $v_p(U^n-V^n)=v_p(U-V)+v_p(n)$.

theorem(s).name: min_valuation_rule_for_sum_or_difference
theorem(s).role: if $v_q(A)\ne v_q(B)$ then $v_q(A\pm B)=\min\{v_q(A),v_q(B)\}$.

theorem(s).name: legendre_formula_factorial_valuation
theorem(s).role: gives $v_2(n!)=n-s_2(n)$; yields $v_2(C(2t,t))=s_2(t)$.

theorem(s).name: kummer_carry_theorem
theorem(s).role: equivalent interpretation of binomial 2-adic valuation via carries.

p-adic valuation as a divisibility invariant
lifting roots via Hensel lemma

Digit-sum evaluation: compute $v_2\binom{2t}{t}$ via Legendre/Kummer.

title: LTE-style exponent lifting for $v_p(a^n\pm b^n)$

identity.short_name: LTE-style exponent lifting for $v_p(a^n\pm b^n)$

problem_spec.summary: Given integers $a,b,n$ and a prime $p$ satisfying an LTE-admissible divisibility condition (e.g., $p\mid a-b$), compute the exact $p$-adic valuation of $a^n-b^n$ (and variants for sums of powers), thereby solving exponent-lifting divisibility questions.
givens: $p$ is a prime
$a,b\in\mathbb{Z}$ with $a\ne b$
$n\in\mathbb{N}$
LTE-admissibility constraints (variant-dependent), e.g. $p\mid (a-b)$ and $p\nmid ab$ for odd $p$ in the difference case; special handling when $p=2$.
ask: Compute $v = v_p(a^n-b^n)$ (or, in a sum-variant, $v=v_p(a^n+b^n)$) exactly in terms of $v_p(a-b)$, $v_p(a+b)$, and $v_p(n)$ as applicable.

parameter(s).name: p
parameter(s).purpose: selects the valuation

parameter(s).name: a
parameter(s).purpose: first base in the power expression

parameter(s).name: b
parameter(s).purpose: second base (difference/sum pattern)

parameter(s).name: n
parameter(s).purpose: exponent to be lifted

parameter(s).name: sign_variant
parameter(s).purpose: selects which LTE formula applies

invariant(s).name: valuation_lift_formula
invariant(s).statement: In admissible cases, $v_p(a^n-b^n)$ decomposes as $v_p(a-b)+v_p(n)$ (with corrections in the $p=2$ and sum cases), reflecting exponent lifting.

invariant(s).name: unit_reduction
invariant(s).statement: After factoring out common $p$-powers from $a-b$ (or $a+b$), the remaining unit controls whether additional $p$-powers appear in the binomial expansion.

essential_assumption(s): The instance must satisfy the standard LTE preconditions (e.g., $p\mid a-b$, $p\nmid ab$ for odd $p$ in the difference case).
For $p=2$, the instance must specify parity constraints on $a,b$ and whether $n$ is even/odd to select the correct even-odd LTE branch.
uniqueness_certificate(s): Once LTE preconditions hold, the valuation $v_p(a^n\pm b^n)$ is determined by the relevant LTE formula, hence uniquely determined as an integer.

technique(s).name: LTE_difference_of_powers
technique(s).purpose: compute $v_p(a^n-b^n)$ exactly via lifted exponent

technique(s).name: LTE_sum_of_powers_cases
technique(s).purpose: handle $a^n+b^n$ cases (often requiring $n$ odd)
outputs: exact valuation or reduction to difference case

technique(s).name: LTE_even_odd_cases
technique(s).purpose: apply the special 2-adic lifting formulas

technique(s).name: compare_vp_on_both_sides
technique(s).purpose: convert divisibility questions to inequalities in $t$

theorem(s).name: vp_sum_lower_bound
theorem(s).role: used after factoring to bound valuations and justify LTE steps

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: Binomial expansion after extracting the maximal common $p$-power from $a-b$ (or $a+b$) reveals exactly how many additional $p$ factors appear as the exponent grows.

critical_trick(s): Factor $a-b$ (or $a+b$) as $p^t\cdot u$ with $p\nmid u$ and expand $(b+p^t u)^n$; show the first nonvanishing binomial term dictates $v_p(a^n-b^n)$.
Separate the $p=2$ case and track the extra $v_2(a+b)$ contribution when both bases are odd.

title: LTE-style exponent lifting for $v_p(a^n\pm b^n)$ -- Buffed Seed

identity.short_name: LTE-style exponent lifting for $v_p(a^n\pm b^n)$ -- Buffed Seed

problem_spec.summary: Fix distinct odd primes $p,q$ and integers $A,B\ge 2$. Let $M=p^{A+1}q^{B+1}$ and choose divisors $d_p\mid(p-1)$, $d_q\mid(q-1)$ with $d_p,d_q>1$ and cross-coprime to the other prime. Define $n=\operatorname{lcm}(d_p,d_q)\,p^{A-1}q^{B-1}$ and count the units $x\pmod M$ whose reductions modulo $p$ and $q$ have orders $d_p$ and $d_q$, and for which $x^n-1$ has exact $p$-adic depth $A$ and exact $q$-adic depth $B$. Report the count modulo $1000$.
givens: $p$ and $q$ are distinct odd primes ($p\ge 3$, $q\ge 3$, $p\ne q$).
$A,B\in\mathbb{Z}$ with $A\ge 2$ and $B\ge 2$.
$d_p,d_q\in\mathbb{Z}$ with $1<d_p\mid(p-1)$ and $1<d_q\mid(q-1)$, and additionally $q\nmid d_p$ and $p\nmid d_q$.
Define the structured modulus $M=p^{A+1}q^{B+1}$.
Define the exponent $n=\operatorname{lcm}(d_p,d_q)\,p^{A-1}q^{B-1}$.
ask: Let $S\subset(\mathbb{Z}/M\mathbb{Z})^\times$ be the set of residue classes $x\pmod M$ such that the reduction of $x$ modulo $p$ has multiplicative order exactly $d_p$, the reduction modulo $q$ has multiplicative order exactly $d_q$, and simultaneously $v_p(x^n-1)=A$ and $v_q(x^n-1)=B$ (where $v_r$ denotes the usual $r$-adic valuation). Determine $N=|S|$ and report the final answer as $N\bmod 1000$.

parameter(s).name: p
parameter(s).purpose: first prime controlling an order constraint (mod $p$) and a valuation-depth constraint (via $v_p$)

parameter(s).name: q
parameter(s).purpose: second prime; forces a genuinely two-prime valuation regime and prevents single-prime LTE templating

parameter(s).name: A
parameter(s).purpose: target exact $p$-adic depth $v_p(x^n-1)=A$ and structural modulus exponent $p^{A+1}$

parameter(s).name: B
parameter(s).purpose: target exact $q$-adic depth $v_q(x^n-1)=B$ and structural modulus exponent $q^{B+1}$

parameter(s).name: d_p
parameter(s).purpose: prescribed nontrivial multiplicative order modulo $p$; the cross-condition $q\nmid d_p$ ensures $v_q(n/d_q)$ is controlled

parameter(s).name: d_q
parameter(s).purpose: prescribed nontrivial multiplicative order modulo $q$; the cross-condition $p\nmid d_q$ ensures $v_p(n/d_p)$ is controlled

parameter(s).name: n
parameter(s).purpose: structured exponent that couples ORDER information (via $d_p,d_q$) to VP constraints (via $p^{A-1},q^{B-1}$)

parameter(s).name: M
parameter(s).purpose: structured modulus with two prime-power components

invariant(s).name: anti_triviality_nontrivial_orders
invariant(s).statement: The constraints $1<d_p\mid(p-1)$ and $1<d_q\mid(q-1)$ force $\operatorname{ord}_p(x)=d_p>1$ and $\operatorname{ord}_q(x)=d_q>1$ for admissible $x$, hence $x\not\equiv 1\pmod p$ and $x\not\equiv 1\pmod q$ (blocking the direct one-step template on $x-1$).

invariant(s).name: anti_triviality_depth_positive
invariant(s).statement: $A,B\ge 2$, so the requested equalities $v_p(x^n-1)=A$ and $v_q(x^n-1)=B$ are genuinely higher-depth lifting constraints and are not vacuous at the first prime layer.

invariant(s).name: cross_coprime_order_data
invariant(s).statement: The cross-conditions $q\nmid d_p$ and $p\nmid d_q$ ensure $\gcd(\operatorname{lcm}(d_p,d_q),pq)=1$, so the $p$- and $q$-adic valuations of $n/d_p$ and $n/d_q$ are determined solely by the explicit factors $p^{A-1}$ and $q^{B-1}$.

invariant(s).name: structured_exponent_vp_targets
invariant(s).statement: With $n=\operatorname{lcm}(d_p,d_q)\,p^{A-1}q^{B-1}$ and the cross-coprimality constraints, one has $v_p(n/d_p)=A-1$ and $v_q(n/d_q)=B-1$.

invariant(s).name: order_to_valuation_bridge_LTE
invariant(s).statement: If $\operatorname{ord}_p(x)=d_p$ then $p\mid(x^{d_p}-1)$ and (for odd $p$) LTE gives $v_p(x^n-1)=v_p(x^{d_p}-1)+v_p(n/d_p)$; analogously, if $\operatorname{ord}_q(x)=d_q$ then $v_q(x^n-1)=v_q(x^{d_q}-1)+v_q(n/d_q)$.

invariant(s).name: valuation_exactness_equivalence
invariant(s).statement: Under the structured exponent choice (so $v_p(n/d_p)=A-1$ and $v_q(n/d_q)=B-1$), the exact-depth conditions $v_p(x^n-1)=A$ and $v_q(x^n-1)=B$ are equivalent to $v_p(x^{d_p}-1)=1$ and $v_q(x^{d_q}-1)=1$ (equivalently $x^{d_p}\equiv 1\pmod p$ but $x^{d_p}\not\equiv 1\pmod{p^2}$, and similarly for $q$).

essential_assumption(s): All primes in the family are restricted to odd primes ($p,q\ge 3$), so no $2$-adic exceptional LTE branches are permitted in valid instances.

The cross-coprimality constraints $q\nmid d_p$ and $p\nmid d_q$ are part of admissibility; without them, $v_p(n/d_p)$ or $v_q(n/d_q)$ can pick up extra prime factors from $\operatorname{lcm}(d_p,d_q)$ and the valuation targets cease to be sharp.

The order constraints enforce $x\not\equiv 1\pmod p$ and $x\not\equiv 1\pmod q$ for admissible $x$, explicitly ruling out the one-step template $v_r(x^n-1)=v_r(x-1)+v_r(n)$; the intended path must first reduce using multiplicative order (replace $x^n-1$ by $(x^{d_r})^{n/d_r}-1$) and only then apply LTE to obtain exact valuations.
uniqueness_certificate(s): For each admissible parameter tuple, the conditions define a finite set $S\subset(\mathbb{Z}/M\mathbb{Z})^\times$. The order constraints contribute exactly $\varphi(d_p)$ choices modulo $p$ and $\varphi(d_q)$ choices modulo $q$. Using the ORDER->LTE bridge and the structured exponent invariants, the depth conditions are equivalent to excluding the unique Hensel lift of each root of $y^{d_r}\equiv 1\pmod r$ to a root modulo $r^2$ (for $r\in\{p,q\}$), yielding exactly $(r-1)r^{A_r-1}$ admissible lifts to $r^{A_r+1}$. Coprime prime powers combine independently, so $|S|$ is uniquely determined; the required output is the unique representative of $|S|\bmod 1000$ in $\{0,1,\dots,999\}$.

technique(s).name: order_then_LTE_bridge
inputs: structured exponent $n=\operatorname{lcm}(d_p,d_q)p^{A-1}q^{B-1}$ together with $q\nmid d_p$ and $p\nmid d_q$
technique(s).purpose: Use multiplicative order to rewrite $x^n-1$ as $(x^{d_p})^{n/d_p}-1$ (and similarly for $q$), then apply LTE to convert the order data into exact valuation equalities; this is the interaction kernel between ORDER and VP.

technique(s).name: LTE_difference_of_powers
technique(s).purpose: Compute $v_r\big((x^{d_r})^m-1\big)$ exactly as $v_r(x^{d_r}-1)+v_r(m)$, enabling sharp depth constraints rather than mere lower bounds.
outputs: exact valuation formula isolating the condition $v_r(x^{d_r}-1)=1$ under the structured choice of $n$

technique(s).name: order_count_in_cyclic_group_mod_prime
technique(s).purpose: Count residues modulo $r$ with multiplicative order exactly $d_r$ using cyclicity of $(\mathbb{Z}/r\mathbb{Z})^\times$.

technique(s).name: valuation_filter_via_unique_root_lift
inputs: a residue class $x\pmod{r^{A_r+1}}$ whose reduction mod $r$ has order $d_r$
technique(s).purpose: Show that among the $r$ lifts of a fixed order-$d_r$ residue modulo $r$ to modulo $r^2$, exactly one satisfies $x^{d_r}\equiv 1\pmod{r^2}$ (Hensel uniqueness), hence the depth condition $v_r(x^{d_r}-1)=1$ excludes a single lift; then count free lifts from $r^2$ to $r^{A_r+1}$.
outputs: lift-layer count factor $(r-1)r^{A_r-1}$ for each prime power

theorem(s).name: vp_sum_lower_bound
theorem(s).role: Supports the valuation bookkeeping underlying LTE and ensures the first nonvanishing term controls the exact valuation once order-reduction identifies the correct base.

theorem(s).name: cyclicity_of_units_mod_odd_prime
theorem(s).role: $(\mathbb{Z}/r\mathbb{Z})^\times$ is cyclic for prime $r$; used to count residues of exact order $d_r$.

theorem(s).name: cyclicity_of_units_mod_odd_prime_power
theorem(s).role: For odd prime $r$, $(\mathbb{Z}/r^k\mathbb{Z})^\times$ is cyclic; supports controlled lifting structure when enumerating residues modulo $r^{A_r+1}$.

theorem(s).name: hensel_unique_lift_for_simple_roots_of_y^d_minus_1
theorem(s).role: If $r\nmid d$ and $y_0^d\equiv 1\pmod r$, then $y_0$ is a simple root of $y^d-1$ modulo $r$ and lifts uniquely to a root modulo $r^2$; used to prove there is exactly one bad lift with $v_r(y^d-1)\ge 2$.

theorem(s).name: chinese_remainder_theorem_coprime_moduli
theorem(s).role: For coprime moduli, gives a bijection between residues modulo the product and pairs of residues modulo each; used to combine the $13$- and $17$-counts.

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: Combine multiplicative-order reduction ($x^n-1=(x^{d})^{n/d}-1$) with valuation control via LTE to translate an order condition into an exact $r$-adic depth constraint.

critical_trick(s): Use $\operatorname{ord}_p(x)=d_p$ and $\operatorname{ord}_q(x)=d_q$ to force $p\mid(x^{d_p}-1)$ and $q\mid(x^{d_q}-1)$, then rewrite $x^n-1$ as $(x^{d_p})^{n/d_p}-1$ and $(x^{d_q})^{n/d_q}-1$ so that LTE applies.

Apply LTE together with $q\nmid d_p$ and $p\nmid d_q$ to deduce $v_p(x^n-1)=A \Longleftrightarrow v_p(x^{d_p}-1)=1$ and $v_q(x^n-1)=B \Longleftrightarrow v_q(x^{d_q}-1)=1$, converting the valuation constraints into a precise lift-exclusion condition modulo $p^2$ and $q^2$.

Count admissible residues by (i) $\varphi(d_r)$ choices at the prime level, (ii) excluding the unique Hensel lift to $r^2$ and freely lifting to $r^{A_r+1}$ giving a factor $(r-1)r^{A_r-1}$ for each $r\in\{p,q\}$, and (iii) combining counts via CRT; then reduce the resulting integer modulo $1000$.

library(s).name: math
library(s).reason: Core integer arithmetic (lcm, pow with modulus, gcd-free valuation logic).

task(s).name: primary_structural_check
task(s).reason:
Correctness checks:
- Brute-enumerated all units modulo $13^4$ and counted those with order $12$ mod $13$ and exact valuation $v_{13}(x^n-1)=3$; obtained $8112$.
- Brute-enumerated all units modulo $17^3$ and counted those with order $16$ mod $17$ and exact valuation $v_{17}(x^n-1)=2$; obtained $2176$.
- Multiplied the independent prime-power counts (CRT interface) to get $|S|=17651712$, hence $|S|\bmod 1000=712$.
NT5 preflight attempts:
(a) Small-mod-only attack: computed the residue modulo each $m\in\{2,3,4,5,7,8,9,11\}$ of the naive count ignoring the exact-depth filter, and compared it to the true residue; they already disagree for $m\in\{3,5,7,9,11\}$, so small-mod shortcuts cannot recover the final residue.
(b) Tiny bounded scan: counted solutions among the first $T\in\{1000,5000,20000\}$ integers viewed as residues modulo $M$ and extrapolated by scaling; the resulting estimates modulo $1000$ were $665,960,296$, none equal to $712$.
(c) Template resistance: checked that the final residue $712$ is not $0$ and is not a pure power of $2$, $3$, or $5$ modulo $1000$. Also tested several one-line shortcut residues obtained from the order-only lift count $|S|_{\text{naive}}$ by multiplying/dividing by factors in $\{13,17,12,16\}$ when integral (e.g., $|S|_{\text{naive}}\equiv 856$, $|S|_{\text{naive}}/13\equiv 912$, $|S|_{\text{naive}}/17\equiv 168$, $12\,|S|_{\text{naive}}\equiv 272$, $16\,|S|_{\text{naive}}\equiv 696$ modulo $1000$); none equals $712$.
NT6 collision resistance:
- Checked the plausible wrong-branch guess $|S|_{\text{naive}}=\varphi(12)13^3\cdot\varphi(16)17^2\equiv 856\not\equiv 712\pmod{1000}$.

task(s).name: secondary_independent_check
task(s).reason:
Independently verified the unique bad lift property:
- For each residue $y_0$ of exact order $12$ modulo $13$, enumerated the $13$ lifts to modulo $13^2$ and confirmed exactly one satisfies $y^{12}\equiv 1\pmod{13^2}$.
- For each residue $y_0$ of exact order $16$ modulo $17$, enumerated the $17$ lifts to modulo $17^2$ and confirmed exactly one satisfies $y^{16}\equiv 1\pmod{17^2}$.
This confirms the single-exclusion factor $(r-1)$ at the $r^2$ layer used in the count.

title: Mixed LTE count with an order-2 bridge and exact depths

identity.short_name: Mixed LTE count with an order-2 bridge and exact depths

problem_spec.summary: Two-prime counting problem where one prime uses a difference-of-powers valuation and the other uses a sum-of-powers valuation; order data forces the relevant base congruences and exact p-adic depths are enforced by excluding the unique Hensel lift at the prime-square layer.
givens: $p$ and $q$ are distinct odd primes.
$A,B\in\mathbb{Z}$ with $A\ge 2$ and $B\ge 2$.
$d_p\in\mathbb{Z}$ with $1<d_p\mid(p-1)$ and $q\nmid d_p$.
$d_q\in\mathbb{Z}$ with $1<d_q$ and $2d_q\mid(q-1)$ and $p\nmid d_q$.
Parity guard: $\operatorname{lcm}(d_p,d_q)/d_q$ is odd.
Define $M=p^{A+1}q^{B+1}$ and $n=\operatorname{lcm}(d_p,d_q)\,p^{A-1}q^{B-1}$.
ask: Let $S\subset(\mathbb{Z}/M\mathbb{Z})^\times$ be the set of residue classes $x\pmod M$ such that $\operatorname{ord}_p(x)=d_p$, $\operatorname{ord}_q(x)=2d_q$, and simultaneously $v_p(x^n-1)=A$ and $v_q(x^n+1)=B$. Determine $|S|$ and output the unique integer $0\le N<1000$ with $N\equiv |S|\pmod{1000}$.

parameter(s).name: p
parameter(s).purpose: prime at which a difference-of-powers LTE constraint is imposed

parameter(s).name: q
parameter(s).purpose: prime at which a sum-of-powers LTE constraint is imposed

parameter(s).name: A
parameter(s).purpose: target exact depth $v_p(x^n-1)=A$ and modulus exponent $p^{A+1}$

parameter(s).name: B
parameter(s).purpose: target exact depth $v_q(x^n+1)=B$ and modulus exponent $q^{B+1}$

parameter(s).name: d_p
parameter(s).purpose: prescribed nontrivial order modulo $p$; keeps $v_q(n/d_q)$ sharp

parameter(s).name: d_q
parameter(s).purpose: half the prescribed even order modulo $q$ (order $2d_q$); keeps $v_p(n/d_p)$ sharp

parameter(s).name: n
parameter(s).purpose: structured exponent coupling the order constraints to exact valuation depths, and (via the parity guard) enabling LTE for a sum of powers

parameter(s).name: M
parameter(s).purpose: structured two-prime modulus for independent prime-power lifting and CRT recombination

invariant(s).name: order2_bridge_to_negative_one
invariant(s).statement: If $\operatorname{ord}_q(x)=2d_q$ then $x^{d_q}$ has order $2$ in $(\mathbb{Z}/q\mathbb{Z})^\times$ and hence $x^{d_q}\equiv -1\pmod q$, so $q\mid(x^{d_q}+1)$.

invariant(s).name: parity_gate_for_sum_LTE
invariant(s).statement: The guard that $\operatorname{lcm}(d_p,d_q)/d_q$ is odd implies $n/d_q$ is odd (since $p^{A-1}q^{B-1}$ is odd), enabling the odd-exponent LTE formula for $v_q(x^n+1)$.

invariant(s).name: structured_exponent_vp_targets
invariant(s).statement: Under $q\nmid d_p$ and $p\nmid d_q$, one has $v_p(n/d_p)=A-1$ and $v_q(n/d_q)=B-1$.

invariant(s).name: mixed_order_to_LTE_bridge
invariant(s).statement: For admissible $x$, $\operatorname{ord}_p(x)=d_p$ gives $p\mid(x^{d_p}-1)$ and yields (LTE) $v_p(x^n-1)=v_p(x^{d_p}-1)+v_p(n/d_p)$; and $\operatorname{ord}_q(x)=2d_q$ gives $q\mid(x^{d_q}+1)$ and (with $n/d_q$ odd) yields $v_q(x^n+1)=v_q(x^{d_q}+1)+v_q(n/d_q)$.

invariant(s).name: exact_depth_equivalence
invariant(s).statement: Using the structured exponent valuations, the targets $v_p(x^n-1)=A$ and $v_q(x^n+1)=B$ are equivalent to $v_p(x^{d_p}-1)=1$ and $v_q(x^{d_q}+1)=1$.

essential_assumption(s): All primes are odd; no 2-adic exceptional LTE branches occur.
Cross-coprimality guards $q\nmid d_p$ and $p\nmid d_q$ ensure $v_p(n/d_p)$ and $v_q(n/d_q)$ are exactly $A-1$ and $B-1$ with no extra prime factors leaking in from $\operatorname{lcm}(d_p,d_q)$.
Parity guard: $n/d_q$ is odd (equivalently $\operatorname{lcm}(d_p,d_q)/d_q$ odd), required for the sum-of-powers LTE identity at $q$.
The order constraint at $q$ is even ($\operatorname{ord}_q(x)=2d_q$) so that $x^{d_q}\equiv -1\pmod q$; without this, $v_q(x^n+1)$ would not be controlled by LTE in the intended way.
uniqueness_certificate(s): For each admissible parameter tuple, the constraints reduce (via LTE bridges) to exact valuation conditions $v_p(x^{d_p}-1)=1$ and $v_q(x^{d_q}+1)=1$. For each residue of order $d_p$ modulo $p$ and each residue of order $2d_q$ modulo $q$, Hensel's lemma for simple roots implies there is exactly one lift to the prime-square layer with valuation $\ge 2$ for the relevant polynomial ($y^{d_p}-1$ at $p$, $y^{d_q}+1$ at $q$), hence exactly $p-1$ (resp. $q-1$) admissible lifts with valuation $1$. All higher lifts from $r^2$ to $r^{A_r+1}$ are free and contribute a factor $r^{A_r-1}$. The $p$- and $q$-conditions are independent and recombine uniquely by CRT, so $|S|$ (hence $|S|\bmod 1000$) is uniquely determined.

technique(s).name: order_count_in_cyclic_group_mod_prime
technique(s).purpose: Count residues of exact order $d_p$ modulo $p$ and exact order $2d_q$ modulo $q$ using cyclicity of unit groups modulo primes.

technique(s).name: order2_to_negative_one_bridge
technique(s).purpose: Convert an even order condition into the congruence $x^{d_q}\equiv -1\pmod q$ needed for sum-of-powers LTE.

technique(s).name: parity_gate_for_sum_LTE
technique(s).purpose: Ensure $n/d_q$ is odd so the odd-exponent LTE sum formula applies.

technique(s).name: LTE_difference_of_powers
technique(s).purpose: Compute $v_p(x^n-1)$ exactly after order reduction to a difference of powers.

technique(s).name: LTE_sum_of_powers_odd_exponent
technique(s).purpose: Compute $v_q(x^n+1)$ exactly after order reduction to a sum of powers.

technique(s).name: structured_exponent_vp_targets
technique(s).purpose: Extract the fixed values $v_p(n/d_p)=A-1$ and $v_q(n/d_q)=B-1$ under the cross-coprimality guards.

technique(s).name: compare_vp_on_both_sides
technique(s).purpose: Turn target depths $A,B$ into prime-square exclusion statements on $x^{d_p}-1$ and $x^{d_q}+1$.

technique(s).name: hensel_filter_for_exact_depth
inputs: polynomials $y^{d_p}-1$ and $y^{d_q}+1$ with simple roots modulo primes
technique(s).purpose: Show that among the $r$ lifts of a fixed root modulo $r$ to modulo $r^2$, exactly one lift makes the polynomial divisible by $r^2$; excluding it forces valuation exactly $1$.
outputs: exactly $p-1$ good lifts at the $p^2$ layer per order-$d_p$ residue
exactly $q-1$ good lifts at the $q^2$ layer per order-$2d_q$ residue

technique(s).name: mod_prime_power_lifting_count
technique(s).purpose: Count free lifts from $r^2$ to $r^{A_r+1}$ and ensure the valuation decision persists under lifting.

technique(s).name: CRT_product_count
technique(s).purpose: Recombine prime-power solutions into solutions modulo $M=p^{A+1}q^{B+1}$ via CRT bijection.

theorem(s).name: cyclicity_of_units_mod_odd_prime
theorem(s).role: $(\mathbb{Z}/r\mathbb{Z})^\times$ is cyclic; enables exact-order counting via Euler totient.

theorem(s).name: unique_element_of_order_2_in_cyclic_group
theorem(s).role: A cyclic group has a unique element of order $2$; in $(\mathbb{Z}/q\mathbb{Z})^\times$ it is $-1$, giving $x^{d_q}\equiv -1\pmod q$ when $\operatorname{ord}_q(x)=2d_q$.

theorem(s).name: vp_sum_lower_bound
theorem(s).role: Valuation bookkeeping used inside LTE arguments to isolate the first nonvanishing term.

theorem(s).name: LTE_difference_of_powers_for_odd_prime
theorem(s).role: If odd prime $r\mid(a-b)$ and $r\nmid ab$, then $v_r(a^m-b^m)=v_r(a-b)+v_r(m)$.

theorem(s).name: LTE_sum_of_powers_for_odd_prime_odd_exponent
theorem(s).role: If odd prime $r\mid(a+b)$, $r\nmid ab$, and $m$ is odd, then $v_r(a^m+b^m)=v_r(a+b)+v_r(m)$.

theorem(s).name: hensel_unique_lift_for_simple_roots
theorem(s).role: Simple roots of a polynomial modulo an odd prime lift uniquely to roots modulo the prime square; used for both $y^{d_p}-1$ and $y^{d_q}+1$.

theorem(s).name: chinese_remainder_theorem_coprime_moduli
theorem(s).role: Provides a bijection between residues modulo a product of coprime moduli and pairs of residues modulo each factor.

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: Translate multiplicative-order information into divisibility of $x^d\pm 1$, then use LTE (difference and sum) to obtain exact p-adic depths and count solutions by lifting across prime powers.

critical_trick(s): Use the even order condition $\operatorname{ord}_q(x)=2d_q$ to force $x^{d_q}\equiv -1\pmod q$ (unique order-2 element) and combine with a parity gate so that LTE applies to the sum $x^n+1$.
Apply LTE separately to $x^n-1$ at $p$ and to $x^n+1$ at $q$ to convert target depths into the prime-square exclusions $v_p(x^{d_p}-1)=1$ and $v_q(x^{d_q}+1)=1$.
Count by excluding the unique Hensel lift at each prime-square layer and then freely lifting to higher prime powers, and recombine counts via CRT.

task(s).name: primary_structural_check
task(s).reason:
Correctness checks aligned to the intended structure:
- Enumerated all residues modulo $11^2$ and counted those whose reduction mod $11$ has order $5$ and for which $11\mid(x^5-1)$ but $11^2\nmid(x^5-1)$; obtained $40$ and lifted by a factor $11^3$ to get $N_{11}=53240$.
- Enumerated all residues modulo $19^2$ and counted those whose reduction mod $19$ has order $18$ and for which $19\mid(x^9+1)$ but $19^2\nmid(x^9+1)$; obtained $108$ and lifted by a factor $19^3$ to get $N_{19}=740772$.
- Multiplied the independent prime-power counts (CRT interface) to get $|S|=39438701280$, hence $|S|\bmod 1000=280$.
Preflight attempts:
Attempt A: Naive heuristic (ignore exact-depth filters and only count order conditions with arbitrary lifts) gives $\varphi(5)\,11^4\cdot\varphi(18)\,19^4\equiv 264\pmod{1000}$, which differs from $280$.
Attempt B: Bounded scan over $x\in\{1,2,\dots,200000\}$ viewed as distinct residues modulo $M$ found $19785$ hits, i.e. $785\bmod 1000$, not $280$.
Attempt C: Partial information (e.g. only knowing $|S|\equiv 30\pmod{125}$) leaves $8$ candidates in $\{0,\dots,999\}$ (namely $30,155,280,405,530,655,780,905$), so it cannot determine the output without the full structure.

task(s).name: secondary_independent_check
task(s).reason:
Independent verification via valuation identities:
- "Random stress test on residues modulo $11^5$: for sampled $x$ satisfying the order-$5$ condition mod $11$, verified directly (by checking congruences mod $11^k$) that $v_{11}(x^n-1)=v_{11}(x^5-1)+v_{11}(n/5)$."
- "Random stress test on residues modulo $19^5$: for sampled $x$ satisfying the order-$18$ condition mod $19$, verified directly that $v_{19}(x^n+1)=v_{19}(x^9+1)+v_{19}(n/9)$ (using the odd-exponent sum LTE regime)."
- "Verified the \"unique bad lift property computationally: for each order-$5$ residue mod $11$, among its $11$ lifts to mod $11^2$ exactly one satisfies $y^5\\equiv 1\\pmod{11^2}$; for each order-$18$ residue mod $19$, among its $19$ lifts to mod $19^2$ exactly one satisfies $y^9\\equiv -1\\pmod{19^2}$."

task(s).name: novelty_equivalence_audit
task(s).reason:
Canonicalized the parent and daughter kernel polynomials in sympy by expand+together+cancel+factor and monic normalization, and attempted equivalence under a deterministic symmetry set:
- swap the two prime components (p<->q),
- substitute $z\mapsto -z$,
- substitute $z\mapsto 1/z$ with denominator clearing,
- substitute $z\mapsto -1/z$ with denominator clearing.
No transform made the parent kernel multiset match the daughter's (the daughter includes a sum-of-powers constraint $z^{d_q}+1$ whereas the parent uses only $z^d-1$ constraints).

title: Teichmueller×principal-unit order lift; compute v_p(x^t-y^t)+v_q(x-y) mod 1000

identity.short_name: Teichmueller×principal-unit order lift; compute v_p(x^t-y^t)+v_q(x-y) mod 1000

problem_spec.summary: Given x,y with equal p-adic valuation and a unit ratio r modulo p^{k+2} that splits as a Teichmueller d-torsion unit times a nontrivial principal unit, compute t=ord_{p^{k+2}}(r) and then evaluate v_p(x^t-y^t)+v_q(x-y), finally reduced mod 1000.
givens: p,q are distinct odd primes; k>=1; d divides (p-1) with d>=3.
w is a unit modulo p^{k+2} of exact multiplicative order d.
a:=v_p(x)=v_p(y); write x=p^a u and y=p^a v with p∤uv.
Define r in (Z/p^{k+2}Z)^× by r \equiv u v^{-1} (mod p^{k+2}).
Teichmueller/principal structure: r \equiv w (mod p) and s:=r*w^{-1} satisfies s \equiv 1 (mod p^k) but s \neq 1 (mod p^{k+1}).
At the prime q, v_q(x) \neq v_q(y).
ask: Let t := ord_{p^{k+2}}(r). Compute N := v_p(x^t-y^t) + v_q(x-y).
Output the unique integer N0 in {0,...,999} such that N0 \equiv N (mod 1000).

parameter(s).name: p
parameter(s).purpose: prime for the p-adic valuation and modulus p^{k+2}

parameter(s).name: q
parameter(s).purpose: secondary valuation via the unequal-valuation min rule

parameter(s).name: k
parameter(s).purpose: depth of the principal-unit perturbation (s \equiv 1 mod p^k but not mod p^{k+1})

parameter(s).name: d
parameter(s).purpose: Teichmueller (root-of-unity) order component

parameter(s).name: w
parameter(s).domain: unit modulo p^{k+2} with ord = d
parameter(s).purpose: fixed Teichmueller component

invariant(s).name: equal_p_valuation
invariant(s).statement: v_p(x)=v_p(y)=a and x=p^a u, y=p^a v with p∤uv.

invariant(s).name: teichmueller_times_principal_unit
invariant(s).statement: r \equiv w (mod p), and s:=r*w^{-1} satisfies s \equiv 1 (mod p^k) but s \neq 1 (mod p^{k+1}).

invariant(s).name: unequal_q_valuations
invariant(s).statement: v_q(x) \neq v_q(y), hence v_q(x-y)=min(v_q(x),v_q(y)).

essential_assumption(s): w has exact order d modulo p^{k+2}.
s:=r*w^{-1} is a nontrivial principal unit at depth k (forces ord(s)=p^2 modulo p^{k+2}).
p∤uv so LTE applies to (u^d, v^d).
v_q(x) \neq v_q(y so the q-adic term is rigid by the min rule.
uniqueness_certificate(s): Mixed-order rigidity: the Teichmueller part has order d and the principal-unit part has order p^2; since the group is abelian and gcd(d,p)=1, the product has unique order t=d*p^2.
Valuation rigidity: LTE fixes v_p(u^t-v^t)=k+2 and the unequal-valuation rule fixes v_q(x-y), so N is uniquely determined.

technique(s).purpose: assemble the final integer N before normalization

theorem(s).role: a unit modulo p^{k+2} splits as (Teichmueller d-torsion)×(principal unit); used to isolate s=r*w^{-1}\equiv1 (mod p).

theorem(s).name: principal_unit_order_mod_p_kplus2

p-adic valuation as a divisibility invariant

title: Count triples with v2(a+b+c)\geqv2(a)+v2(b)+v2(c)+5 up to 25

identity.short_name: Count triples with v2(a+b+c)\geqv2(a)+v2(b)+v2(c)+5 up to 25

subdomain(s): valuation behavior under sums vs products

problem_spec.summary: For strictly increasing triples bounded by N, define 2-adic valuation statistics on product and sum; since product valuation additivity makes W=D automatic, count triples where the sum valuation exceeds the weight by a fixed gap.
givens: Positive integers a<b<c with 1\leqa<b<c\leq25.
v2(n) is the 2-adic valuation of n.
Parking weight W=v2(a)+v2(b)+v2(c).
Parking depth D=v2(abc).
Parking sum S=v2(a+b+c).
Fixed gap parameter g=5.
A triple is parking-ultraharmonic if W=D and S \geq W+g.
ask: Count parking-ultraharmonic triples (a,b,c) with 1\leqa<b<c\leq25.

parameter(s).name: N
parameter(s).purpose: upper bound for the triple (here 25)

parameter(s).name: prime_p
parameter(s).purpose: p-adic valuation base (here p=2)

parameter(s).name: strict_order
parameter(s).purpose: enforces increasing triples (no repetition)

parameter(s).name: valuation_gap
parameter(s).purpose: strengthens the sum-valuation requirement to S \geq W + g (here g=5)

invariant(s).name: product_valuation_additivity
invariant(s).statement: D=v2(abc)=v2(a)+v2(b)+v2(c)=W, so W=D always holds.

invariant(s).name: reduced_condition_with_gap
invariant(s).statement: Parking-ultraharmonic reduces to v2(a+b+c) \geq v2(a)+v2(b)+v2(c)+g.

invariant(s).name: sum_valuation_upper_bound_from_size
invariant(s).statement: Since a+b+c \leq N+(N-1)+(N-2)=3N-3, we have v2(a+b+c) \leq floor(log2(3N-3)).

invariant(s).name: parity_feasibility_for_large_gap
invariant(s).statement: If v2(a)+v2(b)+v2(c)=0 then a,b,c are odd and a+b+c is odd, so v2(a+b+c)=0; thus any requirement v2(a+b+c)\geqg with g\geq1 forces at least one even term.

essential_assumption(s): v2(0) never occurs since a+b+c>0 and a,b,c>0.
Strict ordering a<b<c is part of the counting domain.
uniqueness_certificate(s): Since W=D is automatic by valuation additivity on products, the count is uniquely determined by the single inequality v2(a+b+c) \geq v2(a)+v2(b)+v2(c)+g.
For fixed N and large g, the upper bound v2(a+b+c) \leq floor(log2(3N-3)) forces W to be very small, reducing the problem to finitely many parity/valuation patterns and a finite residue/size check.

technique(s).name: eliminate_redundant_constraint
technique(s).purpose: show W=D always, reducing the definition

technique(s).name: bound_sum_valuation_by_size
technique(s).purpose: bound v2(a+b+c) from above using magnitude

technique(s).name: split_by_parity_pattern
inputs: parity / small v2 patterns of a,b,c
technique(s).purpose: determine which valuation patterns can satisfy a large gap
outputs: W forced to be 1 in the instance (N=25,g=5)

technique(s).name: convert_high_v2_to_congruence_and_exact_sum
technique(s).purpose: turn a high 2-adic valuation into a modular condition and then an exact sum equality

technique(s).name: count_by_even_choice_and_odd_pair_sum
inputs: unique even element e with v2(e)=1 and sum constraint
technique(s).purpose: enumerate admissible e and count odd pairs with fixed sum within bounds
outputs: explicit list of solutions and per-case counts

technique(s).name: sum_case_counts
technique(s).purpose: produce final count

theorem(s).name: p-adic valuation additivity on products
theorem(s).role: v2(abc)=v2(a)+v2(b)+v2(c)

theorem(s).name: valuation upper bound from size
theorem(s).role: if 0<n<2^{k+1} then v2(n)\leqk (applied to n=a+b+c)

theorem(s).name: valuation of sum via congruences
theorem(s).role: v2(t)\geqk iff t\equiv0 (mod 2^k), used to deduce a+b+c\equiv0 (mod 64)

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: 2-adic valuation reduces divisibility conditions to congruences modulo powers of 2.

critical_trick(s): Recognize W=D is automatic, so only the strengthened sum-valuation inequality matters.
Use the size bound a+b+c\leq72 to force v2(a+b+c)\leq6; with gap 5 this forces W=1 and v2(a+b+c)=6.
Convert v2(a+b+c)=6 in the small range to the exact equation a+b+c=64, then do a short finite enumeration of admissible parity patterns.

title: Euclid/factorial constructions forcing new prime divisors

identity.short_name: Euclid/factorial constructions forcing new prime divisors

problem_spec.summary: Construct a structured integer $E$ that is coprime to a prescribed set of small primes (e.g., $E=n!\pm 1$ or $E=a^n-b^n$), and output the least prime divisor of $E$, whose size/location certifies a "new prime" phenomenon (and optionally yields prime-gap/compositeness witnesses).
givens: A construction mode is chosen, producing an integer $E>1$ from integer parameters.
Typical modes: (i) factorial: $E=n!\pm 1$; (ii) prime-gap witness: $E=n!+k$ with $2\le k\le n$; (iii) exponential: $E=a^n-b^n$ with $a>b\ge 1$.
ask: Let $p_{\min}$ be the smallest prime divisor of $E$. Determine $p_{\min}$ (as an integer).

parameter(s).name: mode
parameter(s).purpose: selects a Euclid-style construction that forces nontrivial prime divisors

parameter(s).name: n
parameter(s).purpose: controls the factorial scale and the set of excluded small primes

parameter(s).name: k
parameter(s).purpose: selects a particular shifted factorial term $n!+k$

parameter(s).name: a,b
parameter(s).purpose: controls exponential construction $a^n-b^n$ and primitive divisor behavior

invariant(s).name: Euclid_coprime_invariant
invariant(s).statement: The construction guarantees $\gcd(E,\prod_{p\le n}p)=1$ in factorial modes (and analogous "new prime" exclusion in exponential modes), forcing a prime divisor outside the excluded set.

invariant(s).name: least_prime_divisor_is_unique
invariant(s).statement: Every integer $E>1$ has a unique smallest prime divisor $p_{\min}$.

essential_assumption(s): The construction mode must ensure $E>1$ (e.g., $n\ge2$, $a>b\ge1$).
When the instance wants a strict "new prime > n" guarantee, it must use a factorial mode where $E$ is coprime to all integers $2,3,\dots,n$.
uniqueness_certificate(s): The smallest prime divisor $p_{\min}$ of a fixed integer $E>1$ is unique by the well-ordering principle on the set of prime divisors.

technique(s).name: Euclid_style_construction
technique(s).purpose: build $E$ so that all "old" primes are excluded as divisors
outputs: a coprimality statement implying existence of a new prime divisor

technique(s).name: factorial_trick_n_factorial_plus_minus_1
technique(s).purpose: ensure any prime divisor of $n!\pm 1$ is $>n$
outputs: lower bound on the least prime divisor

technique(s).name: prime_gap_witness_n_factorial_plus_k
technique(s).purpose: produce explicit composite numbers $n!+k$ and engineer intervals without primes/prime powers

technique(s).name: primitive_prime_divisor_patterns
technique(s).purpose: force existence of a prime divisor not appearing at earlier exponents (Zsigmondy-style)
outputs: a prime $p$ with controlled order properties

theorem(s).name: fundamental_theorem_arithmetic
theorem(s).role: permits talking about the least prime divisor and uniqueness of prime factorization

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: If an integer is coprime to a large set of smaller integers, any prime divisor must be genuinely new and hence large.

critical_trick(s): Show $E$ is not divisible by any prime in a prescribed set (typically all primes $\le n$) by reducing $E$ modulo those primes.
Conclude existence of a prime divisor outside the set; take the smallest one to make the output canonical.

title: Euclid/factorial constructions forcing new prime divisors - Buffed Seed

identity.short_name: Euclid/factorial constructions forcing new prime divisors - Buffed Seed

problem_spec.summary: Let $p$ and $q$ be distinct odd primes with $5\le p$ and $2p<q<5p$. Define $k=v_p((q-1)!)$ and set the structured modulus $N=p^k q$. Let $E=(q-1)!+1$, let $p_{\min}$ be the least prime divisor of $E$, and let $r$ be the unique integer with $0\le r<N$ and $r\equiv E\pmod N$. Your final output is the residue of $r$ modulo $1000$.
givens: Let $p$ and $q$ be distinct odd primes with $5\le p$ and $2p<q<5p$.
Define $k=v_p((q-1)!)$ and $N=p^k q$.
Define $E=(q-1)!+1$ and let $p_{\min}$ be the least prime divisor of $E$.
Let $r$ be the unique integer with $0\le r<N$ and $r\equiv E\pmod N$.
ask: (Existence/uniqueness) Prove that $p_{\min}=q$, and deduce that $r$ satisfies the coupled congruences $r\equiv 0\pmod q$ and $r\equiv 1\pmod{p^k}$; conclude that such an $r$ is uniquely determined modulo $N$.
(Computation) Determine $r\bmod 1000$ (i.e., report the unique integer in $\{0,1,\dots,999\}$ congruent to $r$ modulo $1000$).

parameter(s).name: p
parameter(s).purpose: controls the prime-power part $p^k$ of the modulus via the valuation $k=v_p((q-1)!)$

parameter(s).name: q
parameter(s).purpose: sets the factorial $(q-1)!$ and (via Wilson/Euclid) forces the least prime divisor of $(q-1)!+1$

invariant(s).name: anti_triviality_range_and_distinctness
invariant(s).statement: The constraints $p\ge5$ and $2p<q<5p$ force $q>p$ (hence $\gcd(p,q)=1$) and ensure the valuation exponent $k=v_p((q-1)!)$ is nontrivial (in particular $k\ge2$).

invariant(s).name: anti_triviality_prime_power_structure
invariant(s).statement: Because $p\ge5$ implies $5p\le p^2$ and $q<5p$, we have $q<p^2$; hence $v_p((q-1)!)=\lfloor\tfrac{q-1}{p}\rfloor$ and therefore $2\le k\le 4$. In particular, $N=p^k q$ contains a genuine prime-power factor $p^k$ with $k\ge2$ (so $N$ is not squarefree).

invariant(s).name: Euclid_Wilson_and_valuation_congruences
invariant(s).statement: For $E=(q-1)!+1$, Wilson's theorem gives $E\equiv0\pmod q$, and the definition of $k=v_p((q-1)!)$ gives $E\equiv1\pmod{p^k}$.

invariant(s).name: least_prime_divisor_identification
invariant(s).statement: The least prime divisor $p_{\min}$ of $E$ equals $q$: any prime $\ell<q$ divides $(q-1)!$ and hence cannot divide $(q-1)!+1$, while $q\mid (q-1)!+1$ by Wilson.

essential_assumption(s): The exponent $k$ is not an independent knob: it is defined by the valuation $k=v_p((q-1)!)$. Any valid solution must first determine $k$ (VP regime) before solving the Bezout/extended-gcd linear congruence $q\,t\equiv1\pmod{p^k}$ to reconstruct $r$ (LIN regime).

The range guard $2p<q<5p$ is essential: it forces $k\ge2$ and (via $q<p^2$) collapses Legendre's formula to a single nonzero term, preventing degenerate $k\in\{0,1\}$ and ruling out a one-line CRT template with an arbitrary exponent.
uniqueness_certificate(s): Given $(p,q)$, the valuation uniquely fixes $k$ and hence $N=p^k q$. Since $\gcd(p^k,q)=1$, the system $r\equiv0\pmod q$, $r\equiv1\pmod{p^k}$ has a unique solution modulo $N$; imposing $0\le r<N$ makes $r$ unique. Finally, the required normalization reports this unique $r$ reduced modulo $1000$, yielding a unique integer in $\{0,1,\dots,999\}$.

technique(s).name: Bezout_linear_congruence_glue_step
technique(s).purpose: Interaction kernel (VP->LIN): after $k$ is obtained from the valuation, solve $q\,t\equiv 1\pmod{p^k}$ via the extended Euclidean algorithm to reconstruct the unique $r$ with $r\equiv0\pmod q$ and $r\equiv1\pmod{p^k}$.
outputs: an explicit residue $r=q\cdot(q^{-1}\bmod p^k)$ modulo $N=p^k q$, hence $r\bmod1000$

technique(s).name: Wilson_factorial_congruence_mod_q
technique(s).purpose: Certify $q\mid((q-1)!+1)$ so that $E\equiv0\pmod q$.

technique(s).name: Euclid_style_small_prime_exclusion
technique(s).purpose: Exclude every prime $\ell<q$ from dividing $E$ by reducing $E$ modulo $\ell$.
outputs: identification of the least prime divisor $p_{\min}$ as $q$

technique(s).name: Legendre_valuation_of_factorial
technique(s).purpose: Compute $k=v_p((q-1)!)$ (simplified to $\lfloor\tfrac{q-1}{p}\rfloor$ under $q<p^2$) to pin down the exact prime power $p^k$ in $N=p^k q$.
outputs: the valuation-defined exponent $k$ and the congruence $E\equiv1\pmod{p^k}$

theorem(s).name: fundamental_theorem_arithmetic
theorem(s).role: permits talking about the least prime divisor and guarantees its uniqueness

theorem(s).name: Wilson_theorem
theorem(s).role: gives $(q-1)!\equiv-1\pmod q$, hence $q\mid((q-1)!+1)$

theorem(s).name: Legendre_formula_for_v_p_of_factorial
theorem(s).role: computes $v_p((q-1)!)$; under $q<p^2$ this reduces to a single floor term

theorem(s).name: Bezout_identity_and_extended_gcd
theorem(s).role: produces the modular inverse of $q$ modulo $p^k$, enabling reconstruction of $r$

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: Couple congruence information from factorial identities with an exact $p$-adic valuation to determine the modulus, then solve a linear congruence to reconstruct the unique residue class.

critical_trick(s): Use Wilson's theorem to force $q\mid (q-1)!+1$ and then exclude all primes $<q$ by the Euclid factorial argument, so $p_{\min}=q$.
Use the constraint $2p<q<5p$ to compute $k=v_p((q-1)!)$ explicitly and ensure $k\ge2$, so the modulus includes a genuine prime power $p^k$.
Interaction step: after $k$ is fixed (VP), solve $q\,t\equiv1\pmod{p^k}$ by extended gcd (LIN) and take $r=q t$; finally reduce $r$ modulo $1000$.

library(s).name: math
library(s).reason: Core integer arithmetic (gcd) and safe modular multiplication for factorial residues.

task(s).name: primary_structural_check
task(s).reason:
Correctness:
- Recompute $k=v_p((q-1)!)$ by Legendre's floor-sum and confirm $k=\lfloor\frac{q-1}{p}\rfloor$ under $q<p^2$.
- Reconstruct $r$ via $r=q\cdot(q^{-1}\bmod p^k)$ and verify $r\equiv0\pmod q$ and $r\equiv1\pmod{p^k}$.
- Verify $p_{\min}=q$ by checking $E\bmod q=0$ and $E\bmod \ell=1$ for every prime $\ell<q$.
NT5 preflight attempts (cheap strategies that must fail):
- "Small-mod-only attack: compute $E\\bmod m$ for $m\\in\\{2,3,4,5,7,8,9,11\\}$ (here all are $1$), then (incorrectly) force $x\\equiv E\\pmod m$ for all these $m$ and search $x\\in\\{0,1,\\dots,999\\}$; this naive lift returns $x=001$, not the true answer."
- "Tiny bounded scan: search $t\\le 50\\,000$ for $q t\\equiv1\\pmod{p^k}$; no solution appears (the true inverse is much larger), so brute scanning does not reveal $r$ quickly."
- "Template guesses checked and rejected: $r\\bmod1000\\in\\{0,1,q\\bmod1000,(q-1)!+1\\bmod1000\\}$."
NT6 collision resistance:
- Force a plausible wrong branch by replacing $k$ with $k-1$ and recompute the resulting residue modulo $1000$; confirm it differs from the true output.

task(s).name: secondary_independent_check
task(s).reason: Independently compute $E=(q-1)!+1$ modulo $N=p^k q$ by direct modular factorial multiplication (no CRT / inverse reconstruction), and confirm it matches the reconstructed $r$; compare both modulo $1000$.

title: Extremal factorization under size bounds (maximize/divisor-structure)

identity.short_name: Extremal factorization under size bounds (maximize/divisor-structure)

problem_spec.summary: Given a size bound $N$, determine an integer $n\le N$ that optimizes a divisor-structure objective (typically maximizing the number of divisors, or forcing $n$ to be a prime power/squarefree/etc.), reported with a deterministic tie-break rule (e.g., smallest such $n$).
givens: $N\in\mathbb{N},\ N\ge 2$
Objective is fixed for the family (default: maximize divisor count among $1\le n\le N$, with tie-break to the smallest maximizer).
ask: Find $n_*\in\{1,2,\dots,N\}$ maximizing the chosen objective, and if there are multiple, output the smallest such $n_*$.

parameter(s).name: N
parameter(s).purpose: sets a finite search universe and makes extremal arguments meaningful

parameter(s).name: objective_mode
parameter(s).purpose: selects a coherent divisor-structure target within the same prime-exponent framework

invariant(s).name: nonincreasing_exponent_shape
invariant(s).statement: For extremal divisor objectives under a size cap, the prime exponents in $n_* = \prod p_i^{e_i}$ typically satisfy $e_1\ge e_2\ge\cdots$ (otherwise one can swap exponents to reduce $n$ without decreasing the objective).

invariant(s).name: prime_packing_heuristic
invariant(s).statement: Extremal solutions prioritize small primes first: if a large prime $q$ divides $n_*$, replacing $q$ by a smaller prime (when feasible) improves size efficiency for the same exponent budget.

essential_assumption(s): A deterministic tie-break rule (e.g., "choose the smallest maximizer") is part of the family definition and is required for uniqueness.
In "smoothness_constraints_light" mode, the smoothness bound is an explicit parameter so the decision problem stays decidable.
uniqueness_certificate(s): Because the search universe $\{1,\dots,N\}$ is finite, an optimum exists. The tie-break rule selects exactly one integer, making the output globally unique.

technique(s).name: prime_exponent_vectors
technique(s).purpose: translate divisor-structure objectives into constraints on exponent vectors
outputs: optimization problem on integer exponent vectors

technique(s).name: extremal_divisor_choice
technique(s).purpose: choose an extremal object and perform local modifications to derive structural constraints

technique(s).name: minimal_prime_factor_choice
inputs: a counterexample extremal with an avoidable large prime factor
technique(s).purpose: replace a large prime factor by a smaller one while preserving objective to get contradiction
outputs: proof that only small primes appear in extremal candidates

technique(s).name: inequality_bounds_for_integers
technique(s).purpose: convert factorization constraints into numeric bounds on exponents
outputs: finite enumeration range for feasible exponent vectors

theorem(s).name: fundamental_theorem_arithmetic
theorem(s).role: justify that prime exponent vectors parameterize all $n$ uniquely

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: Integers are uniquely determined by their prime exponents; optimizing an arithmetic property often reduces to optimizing over exponent vectors under a size constraint.

critical_trick(s): Convert the objective to constraints on the prime exponent vector and show exponents must be nonincreasing with primes in increasing order.
Use a local replacement argument (swap primes/exponents) to rule out non-optimal shapes, then enumerate the remaining finite candidates.
